content: The co-ordinates of the point in which line joining $(1,1,1)$ and $(2,2,2)$ intersects the plane $x+y+\mathrm{z}=9$ are
explanation:
The equation of the line joining $(1,1,1)$ and ( $2,2,2$ ) is

$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{x-1}{2-1}=\frac{y-1}{2-1}=\frac{z-1}{2-1} \\ & \Rightarrow x-1=y-1=z-1=\lambda(\text { say }) \end{aligned} $$

Then, any point on the line is

$$ \mathrm{P} \equiv(\lambda+1, \lambda+1, \lambda+1) $$

The point P lies on the plane $x+y+\mathrm{z}=9$.

$$ \begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \lambda+1+\lambda+1+\lambda+1=9 \\ & \Rightarrow 3 \lambda=6 \\ & \Rightarrow \lambda=2 \\ \therefore & P \equiv(3,3,3) \end{array} $$

content: The equation of plane passing through $(1,0,0)$ and $(0,1,0)$ and making an angle $45^{\circ}$ with the plane $x+y-3=0$ is
The distance of the point $(5,3,-1)$ from the plane passing through points $(2,1,0),(3,-2,4)$ and $(1,-3,3)$ is
explanation:
Equation of the plane passing through $(2,1,0)$, ( $3,-2,4$ ) and ( $1,-3,3$ ) is

$$ \begin{aligned} & \left|\begin{array}{ccc} x-2 & y-1 & z-0 \\ 3-2 & -2-1 & 4-0 \\ 1-2 & -3-1 & 3-0 \end{array}\right|=0 \\ & \Rightarrow\left|\begin{array}{ccc} x-2 & y-1 & z \\ 1 & -3 & 4 \\ -1 & -4 & 3 \end{array}\right|=0 \\ & \Rightarrow(x-2)(7)-(y-1)(7)+z(-7)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow x-2-y+1-z=0 \\ & \Rightarrow x-y-z-1=0 \end{aligned} $$

$\therefore \quad$ The distance of this plane from $(5,3-1)$ is $\mathrm{d}=\left|\frac{5-3+1-1}{\sqrt{1+1+1}}\right|=\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}}$ units

content: The equation of a line passing through the point $(-1,2,3)$ and perpendicular to the lines $\frac{x}{2}=\frac{y-1}{-3}=\frac{z+2}{-2}$ and $\frac{x+3}{-1}=\frac{y+3}{2}=\frac{z-1}{3}$ is
explanation:
Required line is perpendicular to the lines

$$ \frac{x}{2}=\frac{y-1}{-3}=\frac{z+2}{-2} \text { and } \frac{x+3}{-1}=\frac{y+3}{2}=\frac{z-1}{3} $$

$\therefore \quad$ Required line is parallel to the vector

$$ \overline{\mathrm{b}}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{\mathrm{i}} & \hat{\mathrm{j}} & \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ 2 & -3 & -2 \\ -1 & 2 & 3 \end{array}\right|=-5 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-4 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}} $$

$\therefore \quad$ The equation of the required line is

$$ \frac{x+1}{5}=\frac{y-2}{4}=\frac{z-3}{-1} $$

content: A line L is passing through points $\mathrm{A}(1,3,2)$ and $\mathrm{B}(2,2,1)$. If mirror image of point $\mathrm{P}(1,1,-1)$ in the line L is $(x, y, z)$ then $x+y+\mathrm{z}=$
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Equation of line } L \text { is }\\ &\begin{aligned} & \frac{x-1}{2-1}=\frac{y-3}{2-3}=\frac{z-2}{1-2} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{x-1}{1}=\frac{y-3}{-1}=\frac{z-2}{-1}=\lambda(\text { say }) \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

Any general point on this line is

$$ \mathrm{M}=(\lambda+1,-\lambda+3,-\lambda+2) $$

The d.r.s. of PM are $\lambda,-\lambda+2,-\lambda+3$

Since PM is perpendicular to line L .

$$ \begin{array}{ll} \therefore & 1(\lambda)-1(-\lambda+2)-1(-\lambda+3)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 3 \lambda-5=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \lambda=\frac{5}{3} \\ \therefore & M=\left(\frac{8}{3}, \frac{4}{3}, \frac{1}{3}\right) \end{array} $$

M is the midpoint of PQ .

$$ \begin{array}{ll} \therefore \quad & \left(\frac{8}{3}, \frac{4}{3}, \frac{1}{3}\right)=\left(\frac{1+x}{2}, \frac{1+y}{2}, \frac{-1+z}{2}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow x=\frac{13}{3}, y=\frac{5}{3}, z=\frac{5}{3} \\ & \Rightarrow x+y+z=\frac{23}{3} \end{array} $$

content: The lines $\frac{6 x-6}{18}=\frac{y+1}{3}=\frac{z-1}{5} \quad$ and $\frac{3 x+6}{12}=\frac{y-1}{3}=\frac{z+1}{2}$ are $\ldots$
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { The given lines are }\\ &\frac{6 x-6}{18}=\frac{y+1}{3}=\frac{z-1}{5} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \frac{x-1}{3}=\frac{y+1}{3}=\frac{z-1}{5} \\ & \text { and } \frac{3 x+6}{12}=\frac{y-1}{3}=\frac{z+1}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{x+2}{4}=\frac{y-1}{3}=\frac{z+1}{2} \\ & \left|\begin{array}{ccc} x_2-x_1 & y_2-y_1 & z_2-z_2 \\ a_1 & b_1 & c_1 \\ a_2 & b_2 & c_2 \end{array}\right|=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} -3 & 2 & -2 \\ 3 & 3 & 5 \\ 4 & 3 & 2 \end{array}\right| \\ & =-3(-9)-2(-14)-2(-3) \\ & =27+28+6 \\ & =61 \neq 0 \end{aligned}\\ &\therefore \quad \text { The given lines are not intersecting. } \end{aligned} $$

content: The line $\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y+2}{-1}=\frac{z}{1}$ intersects the XY plane and the YZ plane at points A and B respectively. The equation of line through the points A and B is
explanation:
Since $\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y+2}{-1}=\frac{z}{1}$ intersect the XY plane,

$$ \begin{array}{ll} & z=0 \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y+2}{-1}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{x-1}{2}=0 \text { and } \frac{y+2}{-1}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow x=1 \text { and } y=-2 \\ \therefore \quad & A \equiv(1,-2,0) \end{array} $$

Let $\overline{\mathrm{a}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}$

Also, $\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y+2}{-1}=\frac{\mathrm{z}}{1}$ intersect the YZ plane,

$$ \begin{array}{ll} & x=0 \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{-1}{2}=\frac{y+2}{-1}=\frac{z}{1} \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{y+2}{-1}=\frac{-1}{2} \text { and } z=\frac{-1}{2} \end{array} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{array}{ll} & \Rightarrow y=\frac{-3}{2} \text { and } z=-\frac{1}{2} \\ \therefore & B \equiv\left(0, \frac{-3}{2}, \frac{-1}{2}\right) \\ & \text { Let } \bar{b}=\frac{-3}{2} \hat{j}-\frac{1}{2} \hat{k} \end{array}\\ &\text { The vector equation of the line is }\\ &\begin{aligned} & \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}=\overline{\mathrm{a}}+\lambda(\overline{\mathrm{b}}-\overline{\mathrm{a}}) \\ & \Rightarrow \overline{\mathrm{r}}=(\hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+0 \hat{\mathrm{k}})+\lambda\left(-\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\frac{1}{2} \hat{\mathrm{j}}-\frac{1}{2} \hat{\mathrm{k}}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow[\overline{\mathrm{r}}-(\hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+0 \hat{\mathrm{k}})] \times\left(-\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\frac{1}{2} \hat{\mathrm{j}}-\frac{1}{2} \hat{\mathrm{k}}\right)=\overline{0} \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

content: The distance of the point $\mathrm{A}(3,-4,5)$ from the plane $2 x+5 y-6 z=16$ measured along the line $\frac{x}{2}=\frac{y}{1}=\frac{z}{-2}$ is
explanation:
The equation of the line passing through $\mathrm{A}(3,-4,5)$ and parallel to $\frac{x}{2}=\frac{y}{1}=\frac{\mathrm{z}}{-2}$ is

$$ \frac{x-3}{2}=\frac{y+4}{1}=\frac{z-5}{-2}=\lambda(\text { say }) $$

$\therefore \quad$ Co-ordinates of any point on the line are given

$$ \text { as } \mathrm{P}(2 \lambda+3, \lambda-4,-2 \lambda+5) $$

This point lies on the plane $2 x+5 y-6 z=16$.

$$ \begin{array}{ll} \therefore \quad & 2(2 \lambda+3)+5(\lambda-4)-6(-2 \lambda+5)=16 \\ & \Rightarrow 4 \lambda+6+5 \lambda-20+12 \lambda-30=16 \\ & \Rightarrow 21 \lambda=60 \\ & \Rightarrow \lambda=\frac{20}{7} \\ \therefore \quad & P \equiv\left(\frac{61}{7}, \frac{-8}{7}, \frac{-5}{7}\right) \\ \therefore \quad & A P=\sqrt{\left(\frac{61}{7}-3\right)^2+\left(\frac{-8}{7}+4\right)^2+\left(\frac{-5}{7}-5\right)^2} \end{array} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & =\sqrt{\left(\frac{40}{7}\right)^2+\left(\frac{20}{7}\right)^2+\left(\frac{-40}{7}\right)^2} \\ & =\sqrt{\frac{3600}{49}} \\ & =\frac{60}{7} \text { units } \end{aligned} $$

content: The direction cosines of a normal to the plane passing through $(4,2,3),(-1,4,2)$ and $(3,2,1)$ are …..
explanation:
Equation of plane passing through $(4,2,3)$, ( $-1,4,2$ ) and ( $3,2,1$ ) is

$$ \begin{aligned} & \left|\begin{array}{ccc} x-4 & y-2 & z-3 \\ -1-4 & 4-2 & 2-3 \\ 3-4 & 2-2 & 1-3 \end{array}\right|=0 \\ & \Rightarrow\left|\begin{array}{ccc} x-4 & y-2 & z-3 \\ -5 & 2 & -1 \\ -1 & 0 & -2 \end{array}\right| \\ & \Rightarrow(x-4)(-4)-(y-2)(10-1) \\ & \Rightarrow-4 x+16-9 y+18+2 z-6=0 \\ & \Rightarrow-4 x-9 y+2 z+28=0 \end{aligned} $$

$\therefore \quad$ Direction cosines are

$$ \frac{-4}{\sqrt{16+81+4}}, \frac{-9}{\sqrt{16+81+4}}, \frac{2}{\sqrt{16+81+4}} $$

i.e. $\frac{-4}{\sqrt{101}}, \frac{-9}{\sqrt{101}}, \frac{2}{\sqrt{101}}$

content: The equation of the plane passing through the point $(1,1,1)$ and through the line of intersection of $x+2 y-z+1=0$ and $3 x-y-4 z+3=0$ is
explanation:
The equation of the required plane is

$$ (x+2 y-z+1)+\lambda(3 x-y-4 z+3)=0 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)$$

The plane passes through $(1,1,1)$.

$$ \begin{array}{ll} \therefore \quad & (1+2-1+1)+\lambda(3-1-4+3)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \lambda=-3 \end{array} $$

Substituting $\lambda=-3$ in (i), we get

$$ \begin{aligned} & (x+2 y-z+1)-3(3 x-y-4 z+3)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 8 x-5 y-11 z+8=0 \end{aligned} $$

content: If the foot of the perpendicular drawn from the origin to a plane is $\mathrm{P}(-1,-1,2)$, then equation of the plane is
explanation:
$\mathrm{P}(-1,-1,2)$ is the foot of perpendicular from origin to the plane.

$\therefore \quad$ d.r.s. of normal are $-1-0,-1-0,2-0$, i.e., $-1,-1,2$

$\therefore \quad$ Equation of required plane is

$$ \begin{aligned} & -1(x+1)-1(y+1)+2(z-2)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow x+y-2 z+6=0 \end{aligned} $$

content: The angle between lines whose direction cosines satisfy the equation $l+m+n=0$ and $l^2-\mathrm{m}^2-\mathrm{n}^2=0$, is
explanation:
Putting $l=-\mathrm{m}-\mathrm{n}$ in $l^2=\mathrm{m}^2+\mathrm{n}^2$, we get

$$ \begin{aligned} & (-m-n)^2=m^2+n^2 \\ & \Rightarrow m n=0 \Rightarrow m=0 \text { or } n=0 \end{aligned} $$

If $\mathrm{m}=0$, then $l=-\mathrm{n}$

$$ \therefore \quad \frac{l}{-1}=\frac{\mathrm{m}}{0}=\frac{\mathrm{n}}{1} $$

If $\mathrm{n}=0$, then $l=-\mathrm{m}$

$$ \therefore \quad \frac{l}{-1}=\frac{\mathrm{m}}{1}=\frac{\mathrm{n}}{0} $$

$\therefore \quad \mathrm{a}_1, \mathrm{~b}_1, \mathrm{c}_1=-1,0,1$ and

$$ a_2, b_2, c_2=-1,1,0 $$

$\therefore \quad$ The angle between the lines is given by

$$ \cos \theta=\frac{1+0+0}{\sqrt{1+0+1} \sqrt{1+1+0}}=\frac{1}{2} $$

$\therefore \quad \theta=\frac{\pi}{3}$

content: A triangle ABC is formed by $\mathrm{A}(1,-1,0)$, $B(3,5,3), C(-11,-5,6)$. The equation of internal angle bisector of angle $A$ is
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} & A B=\sqrt{4+36+9}=\sqrt{49}=7 \\ & A C=\sqrt{144+16+36}=\sqrt{196}=14 \end{aligned} $$

By angle bisector theorem,

$$ \frac{\mathrm{AB}}{\mathrm{AC}}=\frac{\mathrm{BD}}{\mathrm{DC}}=\frac{7}{14}=\frac{1}{2} $$

$$ \therefore $$$D$ divides $B C$ internally in the ratio $1: 2$.

$$ \begin{aligned} \therefore \text { Co-ordinates of } \mathrm{D} & \equiv\left(\frac{6-11}{3}, \frac{10-5}{3}, \frac{6+6}{3}\right) \\ & \equiv\left(\frac{-5}{3}, \frac{5}{3}, 4\right) \end{aligned} $$

d.r.s. of AD are $\left(\frac{-5}{3}-1, \frac{5}{3},+1,4-0\right)$

$$ \equiv\left(\frac{-8}{3}, \frac{8}{3}, 4\right) $$

$\therefore \quad$ Equation of AD is

$\frac{x-1}{\frac{-8}{3}}=\frac{y-(-1)}{\frac{8}{3}}=\frac{z-0}{4}$ i.e. $\frac{1-x}{2}=\frac{y+1}{2}=\frac{z}{3}$

content: The mirror image of the point $\mathrm{P}(-1,2,-4)$ in the plane $x-y-2 z+1=0$ is
explanation:
The d.r.s. of normal to the plane are $-1,1,2$

$\therefore \quad$ Equation of line PM is

$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{x+1}{-1}=\frac{y-2}{1}=\frac{z+4}{2}=\lambda \\ & \Rightarrow x=-\lambda-1, y=\lambda+2, z=2 \lambda-4 (say)\end{aligned} $$

Let $\mathrm{M} \equiv(-\lambda-1, \lambda+2,2 \lambda-4)$

$\therefore \quad$ Equation of plane becomes

$$ \begin{array}{ll} & -\lambda-1-(\lambda+2)-2(2 \lambda-4)+1=0 \\ & \Rightarrow-6 \lambda+6=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \lambda=1 \\ \therefore \quad & M \equiv(-2,3,-2) \end{array} $$

Since M is the midpoint of PQ .

$$ \begin{aligned} \therefore & \frac{-1+a}{2}=-2, \frac{2+b}{2}=3, \frac{-4+c}{2}=-2 \\ & \Rightarrow a=-3, b=4, c=0 \end{aligned} $$

content: If the plane $\frac{x}{2}+\frac{y}{3}+\frac{z}{6}=1$ cuts the co-ordinate axes at points $A, B, C$ respectively, then area of the triangle ABC is
explanation:
Given equation of plane is $\frac{x}{2}+\frac{y}{3}+\frac{z}{6}=1$

For point on X -axis, $y=0, \mathrm{z}=0 \Rightarrow x=2$

$\therefore \quad$ Co-ordinates of point are $\mathrm{A} \equiv(2,0,0)$

For point on Y-axis, $x=0, \mathrm{z}=0 \Rightarrow y=3$

$\therefore \quad$ Co-ordinates of point are $\mathrm{B} \equiv(0,3,0)$

For point on Z-axis, $x=0, y=0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{z}=6$

$\therefore \quad$ Co-ordinates of point are $C \equiv(0,0,6)$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \mathrm{A}(\triangle \mathrm{ABC})=\frac{1}{2}|\overline{\mathrm{AB}} \times \overline{\mathrm{AC}}| \\ & =\frac{1}{2}|(-2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}) \times(-2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+6 \hat{\mathrm{k}})|=\frac{1}{2}|18 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+12 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+6 \hat{\mathrm{k}}| \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{324+144+36}=\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{504}=\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{36 \times 14} \\ & =3 \sqrt{14} \text { sq. units } \end{aligned} $$

content: If the plane $\frac{x}{2}-\frac{y}{3}-\frac{\mathrm{z}}{5}=1$ cuts the co-ordinate axes in points $\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}, \mathrm{C}$ respectively, then the area of the triangle $A B C$ is
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} &\text { The vertices of triangle } \mathrm{ABC} \text { are }\\ &\begin{array}{ll} & \mathrm{A} \equiv(2,0,0), \mathrm{B} \equiv(0,-3,0), \mathrm{C} \equiv(0,0,-5) \\ \therefore & \overline{\mathrm{AB}}=-2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-3 \hat{\mathrm{j}} \text { and } \overline{\mathrm{AC}}=-2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-5 \hat{\mathrm{k}} \end{array}\\ &\begin{aligned} \overline{\mathrm{AB}} \times \overline{\mathrm{AC}} & =\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{\mathrm{i}} & \hat{\mathrm{j}} & \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ -2 & -3 & 0 \\ -2 & 0 & -5 \end{array}\right| \\ & =15 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-10 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-6 \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ \therefore \quad A(\triangle A B C) & =\frac{1}{2}|\overline{\mathrm{AB}} \times \overline{\mathrm{AC}}| \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{225+100+36} \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{361}=\frac{19}{2} \text { sq. units } \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

content: If the lines $x=a y-1=z-2$ and $x=3 y-2=\mathrm{bz}-2(\mathrm{ab} \neq 0)$ are coplanar, then
explanation:
The given lines can be written as

$\frac{x}{1}=\frac{y-\frac{1}{\mathrm{a}}}{\frac{1}{\mathrm{a}}}=\frac{z-2}{1}$ and

$\frac{x}{1}=\frac{y-\frac{2}{3}}{\frac{1}{3}}=\frac{z-\frac{2}{b}}{\frac{1}{b}}$

Since the lines are coplanar.

$$ \begin{aligned} \therefore & \left|\begin{array}{ccc} 0 & \frac{2}{3}-\frac{1}{a} & \frac{2}{b}-2 \\ 1 & \frac{1}{a} & 1 \\ 1 & \frac{1}{3} & \frac{1}{b} \end{array}\right|=0 \\ & \Rightarrow-\left(\frac{2}{3}-\frac{1}{a}\right)\left(\frac{1}{b}-1\right)+\left(\frac{2}{b}-2\right)\left(\frac{1}{3}-\frac{1}{a}\right)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{1}{a b}-\frac{1}{a}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow b=1, a \in R-\{0\} \quad \ldots[a b \neq 0] \end{aligned} $$

content: The Cartesian equation of the plane $\overline{\mathrm{r}}=(2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-3 \hat{\mathrm{j}})+\lambda(\hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}})+\mu(2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}})$ is
explanation:
The given plane passes through the point $(2,-3,0)$ and perpendicular to the vectors $\overline{\mathrm{p}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}$ and $\overline{\mathrm{q}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}$

Here, $\bar{a}=2 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \bar{n}=\bar{p} \times \bar{q}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 1 & 2 & -1 \\ 2 & 3 & 1 \end{array}\right| \\ & =\hat{i}(2+3)-\hat{j}(1+2)+\hat{k}(3-4) \\ & =5 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}-\hat{k} \end{aligned} $$

Equation of the plane is

$$ \begin{aligned} & \overline{\mathrm{r}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{n}}=\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{n}} \\ & \Rightarrow \overline{\mathrm{r}} \cdot(5 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}})=(2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}) \cdot(5 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}) \\ & \Rightarrow \overline{\mathrm{r}} \cdot(5 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}})=19 \end{aligned} $$

$\therefore \quad$ Cartesian equation is

$$ 5 x-3 y-z=19 $$

content: If the line $\frac{x-3}{2}=\frac{y+5}{-1}=\frac{z+2}{2}$ lies in the plane $\alpha x+3 y-z+\beta=0$, then values of $\alpha$ and $\beta$ respectively are ….
explanation:
Line $\frac{x-3}{2}=\frac{y+5}{-1}=\frac{z+2}{2}$ lies in the plane $\alpha x+3 y-z+\beta=0$.

The direction ratios of the line are $2,-1,2$.

The direction ratios of the normal to the plane are $\alpha, 3,-1$.

The given line is perpendicular to the normal of plane.

$$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & 2(\alpha)-1(3)+2(-1)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 2 \alpha=5 \\ & \Rightarrow \alpha=\frac{5}{2} \end{array}\\ &\text { Also, point }(3,-5,-2) \text { lies on the plane }\\ &\begin{array}{ll} & \alpha x+3 y-z+\beta=0 \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{5}{2}(3)+3(-5)+2+\beta=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \beta=\frac{11}{2} \end{array} \end{aligned} $$

content: The lines $\bar{r}=(\hat{i}+\hat{j}-\hat{k})+\lambda(3 \hat{i}-\hat{j})$ and $\overline{\mathrm{r}}=(4 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}})+\mu(2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}})$ are
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned}Here,\,\,\,\, & \bar{b}_1=3 \hat{i}-\hat{j}, \bar{b}_2=2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{k} \\ & \bar{a}_1=\hat{i}+\hat{j}-\hat{k}, \bar{a}_2=4 \hat{i}-\hat{k} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} \overline{b_1} \times \overline{b_2} & =\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 3 & -1 & 0 \\ 2 & 0 & 3 \end{array}\right| \\ & =-3 \hat{i}-9 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k} \end{aligned} $$

$\overline{\mathrm{a}_2}-\overline{\mathrm{a}_1}=3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}$

Since $\left(\overline{a_2}-\overline{a_1}\right) \cdot\left(\overline{b_1} \times \overline{b_2}\right)=0$, shortest distance will be 0 .

$\therefore \quad$ The given lines are intersecting.

content: The lines $\frac{x-0}{1}=\frac{y-2}{2}=\frac{z+3}{\lambda}$ and $\frac{x-2}{2}=\frac{y-6}{3}=\frac{z-3}{\lambda}$ are coplanar and $p$ is the plane containing these lines, then which of following point does not lie on the plane.
explanation:
If the lines $\frac{x-x_1}{a_1}=\frac{y-y_1}{b_1}=\frac{z-z_1}{c_1}$ and $\frac{x-x_2}{\mathrm{a}_2}=\frac{y-y_2}{\mathrm{~b}_2}=\frac{\mathrm{z}-\mathrm{z}_2}{\mathrm{c}_2}$ are coplanar, then $\left|\begin{array}{ccc}x_2-x_1 & y_2-y_1 & z_2-z_1 \\ a_1 & b_1 & c_1 \\ a_2 & b_2 & c_2\end{array}\right|=0$ and equation of plane containing them is

$$ \left|\begin{array}{ccc} x-x_1 & y-y_1 & z-z_1 \\ a_1 & b_1 & c_1 \\ a_2 & b_2 & c_2 \end{array}\right|=0 $$

Since the given lines are coplanar.

$$ \begin{aligned} & \therefore \left|\begin{array}{ccc} 2-0 & 6-2 & 3+3 \\ 1 & 2 & \lambda \\ 2 & 3 & \lambda \end{array}\right|=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 2(-\lambda)-4(-\lambda)+6(-1)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 2 \lambda=6 \\ & \Rightarrow \lambda=3 \end{aligned} $$

Equation of the plane containing the given lines is

$$ \left|\begin{array}{ccc} x-0 & y-2 & z+3 \\ 1 & 2 & \lambda \\ 2 & 3 & \lambda \end{array}\right|=0 $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow x(-\lambda)-(y-2)(-\lambda)+(z+3)(-1)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow-3 x+3 y-6-z-3=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 3 x-3 y+z+9=0 \end{aligned}\\ &\text { Only option (D) satisfies this equation. } \end{aligned} $$

content: The length of the foot of the perpendicular from the point $\left(1, \frac{3}{2}, 2\right)$ to the plane $2 x-2 y+4 z+17=0$ is
explanation:
The length of the perpendicular from a point $\left(x_1, y_1, z_1\right)$ to the plane $\mathrm{a} x+\mathrm{b} y+\mathrm{cz}+\mathrm{d}=0$ is

$$ \begin{aligned} d & =\left|\frac{a x_1+b y_1+c z_1+d}{\sqrt{a^2+b^2+c^2}}\right| \\ & =\left|\frac{2(1)-2\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)+4(2)+17}{\sqrt{4+4+16}}\right| \\ & =\frac{24}{\sqrt{24}} \\ & =\sqrt{24} \\ & =2 \sqrt{6} \text { units } \end{aligned} $$

content: If the lines $\frac{1-x}{2}=\frac{7 y+4}{2 \lambda}=\frac{2 z-5}{2}$ and $\frac{7-7 x}{3 \lambda}=\frac{y-1}{7}=\frac{6-\mathrm{z}}{5}$ are at right angle, then the value of $\lambda$ is
explanation:
Given lines can be written as $\frac{x-1}{-2}=\frac{y+\frac{4}{7}}{\frac{2 \lambda}{7}}=\frac{z-\frac{5}{2}}{1}$ and $\frac{x-1}{-\frac{3 \lambda}{7}}=\frac{y-1}{7}=\frac{z-6}{-5}$

Since these lines are at right angles,

$$ \begin{aligned} & (-2)\left(\frac{-3 \lambda}{7}\right)+\left(\frac{2 \lambda}{7}\right)(7)+1(-5)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{6 \lambda}{7}+2 \lambda-5=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{20 \lambda}{7}=5 \\ & \Rightarrow \lambda=\frac{7}{4} \end{aligned} $$

content: In 3-dimensional space, the equation $x^2-8 x+12=0$ represents ....

options.content: a pair of straight lines passing through the origin

explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} & x^2-8 x+12=0 \\ & \Rightarrow(x-6)(x-2)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow x-6=0 \text { or } x-2=0 \\ & \Rightarrow x=6 \text { or } x=2, \text { which represents two planes } \\ & \text { parallel to YZ-plane. } \end{aligned} $$

content: Let M and N be foots of the perpendiculars drawn from the point $\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{a}, \mathrm{a})$ on the lines $x-y=0, \mathrm{z}=1$ and $x+y=0, \mathrm{z}=-1$ respectively and if $\angle \mathrm{MPN}=90^{\circ}$ then $\mathrm{a}^2=$
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} & x-y=0, z=1 \\ & \Rightarrow x=y=\lambda, z=1 \end{aligned} $$

Any point on the line is $\mathrm{M} \equiv(\lambda, \lambda, 1)$

$\therefore \quad$ The d.r.s. of PM are $\lambda-\mathrm{a}, \lambda-\mathrm{a}, 1-\mathrm{a}$

Since PM is perpendicular to the line.

$$ \begin{array}{ll} \therefore & 1(\lambda-a)+1(\lambda-a)+0(1-a)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \lambda=a \\ \therefore & M \equiv(a, a, 1) \\ & x+y=0, z=-1 \\ & \Rightarrow x=-y=\mu, z=-1 \end{array} $$

Any point on the line is $\mathrm{N} \equiv(\mu,-\mu,-1)$

$\therefore \quad$ The d.r.s. of PN are $\mu-\mathrm{a},-\mu-\mathrm{a},-1-\mathrm{a}$

Since PN is perpendicular to the line.

$$ \begin{array}{ll} \therefore & 1(\mu-a)-1(-\mu-a)+0(-1-a)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \mu=0 \\ \therefore & \mathrm{~N} \equiv(0,0,-1) \\ & \overline{\mathrm{PM}}=(0,0,1-a) \\ & \overline{\mathrm{PN}}=(-a,-a,-1-a) \\ & \angle \mathrm{MPN}=90^{\circ} \\ & \Rightarrow \overline{\mathrm{PM}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{PN}}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 0+0+(1-a)(-1-a)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow a^2=1 \end{array} $$

content: If $\theta$ is the angle between the lines whose direction cosines are given by $6 \mathrm{mn}-2 \mathrm{n} l+5 l \mathrm{~m}=0$ and $3 l+\mathrm{m}+5 \mathrm{n}=0$, then $\sin \theta=$
explanation:
Given equations are

$$ \begin{aligned} & 6 m n-2 n l+5 l m=0 \\ & 3 l+m+5 n=0 \\ & \Rightarrow m=-3 l-5 n \end{aligned} $$

Substituting above value in (i), we get

$$ 6 n(-3 l-5 n)-2 n l+5 l(-3 l-5 n)=0 $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow-18 \mathrm{n} l-30 \mathrm{n}^2-2 \mathrm{n} l-15 l^2-25 \mathrm{n} l=0 \\ & \Rightarrow-30 \mathrm{n}^2-45 \mathrm{n} l-15 l^2=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 2 \mathrm{n}^2+3 \mathrm{n} l+l^2=0 \\ & \Rightarrow(2 \mathrm{n}+l)(\mathrm{n}+l)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow l=-2 \mathrm{n} \text { or } l=-\mathrm{n}\,\,\,\, …[From (ii)]\end{aligned} $$

When $l=-2 \mathrm{n}$,

$$ \begin{aligned} m & =-3 l-5 n \\ & =6 n-5 n \\ \Rightarrow m & =n \end{aligned} $$

Direction cosines are $(-2,1,1)$

When $l=-\mathrm{n}\,\,\,\,…[From (ii)]$

$$ \begin{aligned} & m=-3 l-5 n=3 n-5 n \\ & \Rightarrow m=-2 n \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Direction cosines are }(1,2,-1)\\ &\begin{aligned} \cos \theta & =\left|\frac{a_1 a_2+b_1 b_2+c_1 c_2}{\sqrt{a_1^2 b_1^2 c_1^2} \cdot \sqrt{a_2^2+b_2^2+c_2^2}}\right| \\ & =\frac{(-2)(1)+(1)(2)+(1)(-1)}{\sqrt{(-2)^2+1^2+1^2} \cdot \sqrt{1^2+2^2+(-1)^2}} \\ & =\left|\frac{-1}{6}\right| \\ \Rightarrow \cos \theta & =\frac{1}{6} \\ \sin \theta & =\sqrt{1-\cos ^2 \theta}=\sqrt{1-\frac{1}{36}}=\frac{\sqrt{35}}{6} \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

content: The line passing through the points $(a, 1,6)$ and $(3,4, \mathrm{~b})$ crosses the $y z$-plane at $\left(0, \frac{17}{2}, \frac{-13}{2}\right)$, then the value of $(3 a+4 b)$ is
explanation:
The equation of the line passing through (a, 1, 6) and ( $3,4, b$ ) is

$$ \frac{x-a}{3-a}=\frac{y-1}{3}=\frac{z-6}{b-6} $$

Since the line crosses the $y z$-plane at

$$ \begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \frac{-a}{3-a}=\frac{\frac{17}{2}-1}{3}=\frac{-\frac{13}{2}-6}{b-6} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{-a}{3-a}=\frac{5}{2}=\frac{-25}{2(b-6)} \\ & \Rightarrow 2 a=5(a-3) \text { and } b-6=-5 \\ & \Rightarrow a=5 \quad \text { and } b=1 \\ & \therefore \quad 3 a+4 b=3(5)+4(1) \\ & \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,= 15+4=19 \end{aligned} $$

content: The angle between the lines $x=y, z=0$ and $y=0, \mathrm{z}=0$ is
explanation:
The equation of the given lines can be written as $\frac{x}{1}=\frac{y}{1}, \mathrm{z}=0$ and $y=0, \mathrm{z}=0$ i.e., $x$-axis

$\therefore \quad$ Direction ratios of the lines are $(1,1,0)$ and $(1,0,0)$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \cos \theta=\left|\frac{(1)(1)+(1)(0)+(0)(1)}{\sqrt{1^2+1^2+0^2} \cdot \sqrt{1^2+0^2+0^2}}\right| \\ & \quad \quad=\left|\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right| \\ & \therefore \quad \theta=45^{\circ} \end{aligned} $$

content: If the foot of the perpendicular drawn from the origin to a plane is $\mathrm{P}(2,-1,4)$, then the equation of the plane is
explanation:
Step 1: Recall the concept

If $P$ is the foot of the perpendicular from a point $O(0,0,0)$ to a plane, then:

  • Vector $OP = (2, -1, 4)$ is perpendicular to the plane (i.e. it is parallel to the plane’s normal).

  • Therefore, the plane has a normal vector $n = (2, -1, 4)$.

  • The plane passes through $P(2, -1, 4)$.

So the equation of the plane:

$ n \cdot \big((x,y,z) - P\big) = 0. $

Step 2: Write the equation

$ (2, -1, 4) \cdot ( (x-2), (y+1), (z-4) ) = 0 $

$ = 2(x-2) + (-1)(y+1) + 4(z-4) = 0. $

$ = 2x - 4 - y - 1 + 4z - 16 = 0. $

$ 2x - y + 4z - 21 = 0. $

Step 3: Match the option

That corresponds to Option D.

✅ Final Answer:

The equation of the plane is

$ \boxed{2x - y + 4z - 21 = 0} \quad (\text{Option D}) $

content: Let the plane passing through point $(2,1,-1)$ containing line joining the points $(1,3,2)$ and $(1,2,1)$ makes intercepts $\mathrm{p}, \mathrm{q}, \mathrm{r}$ on co-ordinate axes, then $\mathrm{p}+\mathrm{q}+\mathrm{r}=$
explanation:
Let $\mathrm{A}(2,1,-1), \mathrm{B}(1,3,2)$ and $\mathrm{C}(1,2,1)$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \overline{A C}=-\hat{i}+\hat{j}+2 \hat{k} \\ & \overline{B C}=-\hat{j}-\hat{k} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \bar{n}=\overline{A C} \times \overline{B C}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ -1 & 1 & 2 \\ 0 & -1 & -1 \end{array}\right|=\hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k} $$

Equation of required plane is

$$ \begin{aligned} & \overrightarrow{\mathbf{r}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathbf{n}}=\mathbf{a} \cdot \overline{\mathbf{n}} \\ & \Rightarrow(x \hat{\mathbf{i}}+y \hat{\mathbf{j}}+z \hat{\mathbf{k}}) \cdot(\hat{\mathbf{i}}-\hat{\mathbf{j}}+\hat{\mathbf{k}})=(\hat{\mathbf{i}}-\hat{\mathbf{j}}+\hat{\mathbf{k}}) \cdot(2 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{j}}-\hat{\mathbf{k}}) \\ & \Rightarrow x-y+\mathrm{z}=0 \\ & \text { For } x \text { intercept }, y=0, \mathrm{z}=0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{p}=0 \end{aligned} $$

For $y$ intercept $x=0, \mathrm{z}=0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{q}=0$

For z intercept, $x=0, y=0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{r}=0$

$\therefore \quad$ Sum of intercepts $=0$

content: The angle between the line $x=\frac{y-1}{2}=\frac{z-3}{\lambda}$ and the plane $x+2 y+3 z=6$ is $\cos ^{-1} \sqrt{\frac{5}{14}}$, then the value of $\lambda$ is
explanation:
The d.r.s. of line are $1,2, \lambda$ and

The d.r.s. of normal to the plane are $1,2,3$.

$$ \begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \sin \theta=\frac{1(1)+2(2)+\lambda(3)}{\sqrt{1+4+9} \sqrt{1+4+\lambda^2}} \\ & \Rightarrow \sin \theta=\frac{5+3 \lambda}{\sqrt{14} \sqrt{5+\lambda^2}} \\ & \Rightarrow \sin ^2 \theta=\frac{(5+3 \lambda)^2}{14\left(5+\lambda^2\right)} \\ & \Rightarrow 1-\frac{5}{14}=\frac{(5+3 \lambda)^2}{14\left(5+\lambda^2\right)} \\ &\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, \quad \ldots .\left[\because \cos \theta=\sqrt{\frac{5}{14}} \text { (given) }\right] \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\Rightarrow \frac{9}{14}=\frac{25+30 \lambda+9 \lambda^2}{14\left(5+\lambda^2\right)}\\ &\text { On solving, we get }\\ &\lambda=\frac{2}{3} \end{aligned} $$

content: The equation of the line passing through the point of intersection of $\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y-2}{3}=\frac{z-3}{4}$ and $\frac{x-4}{5}=\frac{y-1}{2}=z$ and also through the point ( $2,1,-2$ ) is
explanation:
Let $\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y-2}{3}=\frac{z-3}{4}=\lambda$

and $\frac{x-4}{5}=\frac{y-1}{2}=z=\mu$

$$ \Rightarrow x=2 \lambda+1, y=3 \lambda+2, z=4 \lambda+3 $$

and $x=5 \mu+4, y=2 \mu+1, \mathrm{z}=\mu$

On solving, we get

$$ x=-1, y=-1, \mathrm{z}=-1 $$

The equation of the line passing through $(-1,-1,-1)$ and ( $2,1,-2$ ) is

$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{x+1}{2+1}=\frac{y+1}{1+1}=\frac{z+1}{-2+1} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{x+1}{3}=\frac{y+1}{2}=\frac{z+1}{-1} \end{aligned} $$

content: If the lines $\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y+1}{3}=\frac{z-1}{4}$ and $\frac{x-3}{1}=\frac{y-\mathrm{k}}{2}=\frac{\mathrm{z}}{1}$ intersect, then the value of k is
explanation:
As the given lines are intersecting, the shortest distance between them is zero.

$$ \begin{aligned} & \therefore\left|\begin{array}{ccc} x_2-x_1 & y_2-y_1 & z_2-z_1 \\ a_1 & b_1 & c_1 \\ a_2 & b_2 & c_2 \end{array}\right|=0 \\ & \therefore\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 3-1 & k+1 & 0-1 \\ 2 & 3 & 4 \\ 1 & 2 & 1 \end{array}\right|=0 \\ & \therefore\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 2 & k+1 & -1 \\ 2 & 3 & 4 \\ 1 & 2 & 1 \end{array}\right|=0 \\ & \therefore k=\frac{9}{2} \end{aligned} $$

content: The distance of the point $\mathrm{P}(3,4,4)$ from the point of intersection of the line joining the points $\mathrm{Q}(3,-4,-5), \mathrm{R}(2,-3,1)$ and the plane $2 x+y+z=7$ is
explanation:
The equation of the line passing through ( $3,-4,-5$ ) and ( $2,-3,1$ ) is

$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{x-3}{2-3}=\frac{y+4}{-3+4}=\frac{z+5}{1+5} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{x-3}{-1}=\frac{y+4}{1}=\frac{z+5}{6}=\lambda(\text { say }) \end{aligned} $$

$\therefore \quad$ The co-ordinates of any point on the line are

$$ M \equiv(-\lambda+3, \lambda-4,6 \lambda-5) $$

This point lies on the plane $2 x+y+z=7$.

$$ \begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad 2(-\lambda+3)+\lambda-4+6 \lambda-5=7 \\ & \quad \Rightarrow 5 \lambda=10 \\ & \Rightarrow \lambda=2 \\ & \therefore \quad \mathrm{M} \equiv(1,-2,7) \\ & \begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \mathrm{PM} & =\sqrt{(1-3)^2+(-2-4)^2+(7-4)^2} \\ & =\sqrt{4+36+9} \\ & =\sqrt{49} \\ & =7 \text { units } \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

content: The equation of the plane containing the line $\frac{x}{1}=\frac{y}{2}=\frac{z}{3}$ and perpendicular to the plane containing the lines $\frac{x}{2}=\frac{y}{3}=\frac{z}{1}$ and $\frac{x}{3}=\frac{y}{2}=\frac{z}{1}$ is
explanation:
Equation of the plane containing $\frac{x}{2}=\frac{y}{3}=\frac{z}{1}$ and

$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{x}{3}=\frac{y}{2}=\frac{z}{1} \text { is } \\ & \left|\begin{array}{lll} x & y & z \\ 2 & 3 & 1 \\ 3 & 2 & 1 \end{array}\right|=0 \\ & \Rightarrow x(1)-y(-1)+z(-5)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow x+y-5 z=0 \end{aligned} $$

Now, required plane is perpendicular to this plane.

Consider option (C),

$$ 13(1)-8(1)+1(-5)=0 $$

$\therefore \quad$ Option (C) is the correct answer.

content: The equation of the plane containing the line $\frac{x+1}{2}=\frac{y+2}{1}=\frac{z-2}{3}$ and the point $(1,-1,3)$ is

The line L is passing through $(1,2,3)$. The distance of any point on the line L from the line $\overline{\mathrm{r}}=(3 \lambda-1) \hat{\mathrm{i}}+(-2 \lambda+3) \hat{\mathrm{j}}+(4+\lambda) \hat{\mathrm{k}}$ is constant. Then the line L does not pass through the point
explanation:
Given equation of line is

$$ \begin{aligned} & \overline{\mathrm{r}}=(3 \lambda-1) \hat{\mathrm{i}}+(-2 \lambda+3) \hat{\mathrm{j}}+(4+\lambda) \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ & \Rightarrow \overline{\mathrm{r}}=-\hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{k}}+\lambda(3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}) \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)\end{aligned} $$

$\therefore \quad$ d.r.s. of the line are $3,-2,1$

The required line $(\mathrm{L})$ is parallel to the line (i).

Since $L$ passes through $(1,2,3)$.

$\therefore \quad$ Equation of L is

$$ \frac{x-1}{3}=\frac{y-2}{-2}=\frac{z-3}{1}=\mu(\text { say }) $$

$\therefore \quad$ Any point on line $L$ will be of the form

$$ \equiv(3 \mu+1,-2 \mu+2, \mu+3) $$

Consider option (D)

$$ \begin{aligned} & 3 \mu+1=-5 \Rightarrow \mu=-2 \\ & -2 \mu+2=6 \Rightarrow \mu=-2 \\ & \mu+3=2 \Rightarrow \mu=-1 \neq-2 \end{aligned} $$

$\therefore \quad(-5,6,2)$ does not lie on the line L .

content: The distance of the plane $\overline{\mathrm{r}}=(\hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}})+\lambda(\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}})+\mu(\hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}})$ from the origin is
explanation:
Given equation of plane is

$$ \begin{aligned} & \overline{\mathrm{r}}=(\hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}})+\lambda(\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}})+\mu(\hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}) \\ & \text { Vector normal to the plane }=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{\mathrm{i}} & \hat{\mathrm{j}} & \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -2 & 3 \end{array}\right| \\ &=\hat{\mathrm{i}}(3+2)-\hat{\mathrm{j}}(3-1)+\hat{\mathrm{k}}(-2-1) \\ &=5 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-3 \hat{\mathrm{k}} \end{aligned} $$

Since the plane passes through $(1,-1,0)$ and vector normal to the plane is $5 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}$, the equation of plane is

$$ \begin{aligned} & 5(x-1)-2(y+1)-3(z-0)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 5 x-2 y-3 z=7 \end{aligned} $$

$\therefore \quad$ Distance from origin

$$ \begin{aligned} & =\frac{|d|}{\sqrt{a^2+b^2+c^2}} \\ & =\frac{7}{\sqrt{25+4+9}} \\ & =\frac{7}{\sqrt{38}} \text { units } \end{aligned} $$

content: If the angle between the line $x=\frac{y-1}{2}=\frac{z-3}{\lambda}$ and the plane $x+2 y+3 z=4$ is $\cos ^{-1} \sqrt{\frac{5}{14}}$, then the value of $\lambda$ is
explanation:
$$ \begin{gathered} \text { Here } a_1=1, b_1=2, c_1=\lambda \\ a_2=1, b_2=2, c_2=3 \\ \sin \theta=\left|\frac{a_1 a_2+b_1 b_2+c_1 c_2}{\sqrt{a_1^2+b_1^2+c_1^2} \sqrt{a_2^2+b_2^2+c_2^2}}\right| \\ =\frac{1+4+3 \lambda}{\sqrt{1+4+\lambda^2} \sqrt{1+4+9}} \\ \therefore \quad \sin \theta=\frac{5+3 \lambda}{\sqrt{14} \cdot \sqrt{5+\lambda^2}} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)\end{gathered} $$

Given that $\theta=\cos ^{-1} \sqrt{\frac{5}{14}}$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \cos \theta=\sqrt{\frac{5}{14}} \Rightarrow \sin \theta=\frac{3}{\sqrt{14}} \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{5+3 \lambda}{\sqrt{14} \sqrt{5+\lambda^2}}=\frac{3}{\sqrt{14}} \quad \ldots[\text { From (i) }] \\ & \Rightarrow 5+3 \lambda=3 \sqrt{5+\lambda^2} \\ & \Rightarrow 9 \lambda^2+30 \lambda+25=9\left(5+\lambda^2\right) \end{aligned} $$

                                                               …[Squaring both sides]

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow 30 \lambda=20 \\ & \Rightarrow \lambda=\frac{2}{3} \end{aligned} $$

content: If the point $(1, \alpha, \beta)$ lies on the line of the shortest distance between the lines $\frac{x+2}{-3}=\frac{y-2}{4}=\frac{z-5}{2}$ and $\frac{x+2}{-1}=\frac{y+6}{2}, \mathrm{z}=1$, then $\alpha+\beta=$
explanation:
$$ \text { Let } L_1 \text { and } L_2 \text { be the given lines. } $$

$$ \begin{array}{ll} & \text { d.r.s. of } \mathrm{PQ}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{\mathrm{i}} & \hat{\mathrm{j}} & \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ -3 & 4 & 2 \\ -1 & 2 & 0 \end{array}\right|=-4 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ \therefore \quad & \text { d.r.s. of } \mathrm{PQ} \text { are }(2,1,1) \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)\\ & \mathrm{L}_1: \frac{x+2}{-3}=\frac{y-2}{4}=\frac{z-5}{2}=\lambda \text { (say) } \\ \therefore \quad & \text { Co-ordinates of } \mathrm{P} \equiv(-3 \lambda-2,4 \lambda+2,2 \lambda+5) \\ & \mathrm{L}_2: \frac{x+2}{-1}=\frac{y+6}{2}, \mathrm{z}=1 \\ \therefore \quad & \text { Co-ordinates of } \mathrm{Q} \equiv(-\mu-2,2 \mu-6,1) \\ \therefore \quad & \text { d.r.s. of } \mathrm{PQ} \equiv(3 \lambda-\mu, 2 \mu-4 \lambda-8,-2 \lambda-4) \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(ii)\end{array} $$

From (i) and (ii), we get

$$ \frac{3 \lambda-\mu}{2}=\frac{2 \mu-4 \lambda-8}{1}=\frac{-2 \lambda-4}{1} $$

$3 \lambda-\mu=-4 \lambda-8$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \mu=7 \lambda+8 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(iii)\\ & 3 \lambda-\mu=4 \mu-8 \lambda-16 \\ & \Rightarrow 11 \lambda=5 \mu-16 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(iv)\end{aligned} $$

On solving (iii) and (iv), we get

$$ \begin{array}{ll} & \lambda=-1, \mu=1 \\ \therefore \quad & P \equiv(1,-2,3) \\ & Q \equiv(-3,-4,1) \end{array} $$

$\therefore \quad$ Equation of PQ is

$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{x-x_1}{x_2-x_1}=\frac{y-y_1}{y_2-y_1}=\frac{z-z_1}{z_2-z_1} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{x+3}{4}=\frac{y+4}{2}=\frac{z-1}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{x+3}{2}=\frac{y+4}{1}=\frac{z-1}{1} \end{aligned} $$

Since $(1, \alpha, \beta)$ lies on the line.

$$ \therefore \quad 2=\alpha+4=\beta-1 $$

On solving we get

$$ \begin{array}{ll} & \alpha=-2, \beta=3 \\ \therefore& \alpha+\beta=-2+3=1 \end{array} $$

content: The angle between the lines $x-3 y-4=0,4 y-z+5=0$ and $x+3 y-11=0,2 y-z+6=0$ is
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Given equation of plane are }\\ &\left.\begin{array}{l} x-3 y-4=0 \\ 4 y-z+5=0 \end{array}\right\} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)\end{aligned} $$

$$ \left.\begin{array}{l} x+3 y-11=0 \\ 2 y-z+6=0 \end{array}\right\}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(ii) $$

Let $l_1, \mathrm{~m}_1, \mathrm{n}_1$ and $l_2, \mathrm{~m}_2, \mathrm{n}_2$ be the direction cosines of lines (i) and (ii)

Since line (i) is perpendicular to the normals of each plane

$$ x-3 y-4=0 \text { and } 4 y-z+5=0 $$

$$ \begin{array}{ll} \therefore & l_1-3 m_1=0 \\ & 4 m_1-n_1=0 \\ \therefore & \frac{l_1}{3-0}=\frac{m_1}{0-(-1)}=\frac{n_1}{4-0} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{l_1}{3}=\frac{m_1}{1}=\frac{n_1}{4}=\mathrm{k} \text { (say) } \end{array} $$

Similarly for line (ii), we get

$$ \begin{aligned} & l_2+3 \mathrm{~m}_2=0 \\ & 2 \mathrm{~m}_2-\mathrm{n}_2=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{l_2}{-3}=\frac{\mathrm{m}_2}{1}=\frac{\mathrm{n}_2}{2}=\mathrm{t}(\text { say }) \end{aligned} $$

Let $\theta$ be the angle between the lines (i) and (ii)

$$ \begin{aligned} \cos \theta & =l_1 l_2+m_1 m_2+n_1 n_2 \\ & =3 k(-3 t)+k t+4 k(2 t) \\ & =0 \\ \Rightarrow \theta & =\frac{\pi}{2} \end{aligned} $$

content: If the planes $\overline{\mathrm{r}} \cdot(2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\lambda \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}})=3$ and $\overline{\mathrm{r}} \cdot(4 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\mu \hat{\mathrm{k}})=5$ are parallel, then $\lambda+\mu=$
explanation:
Given planes are parallel

Then,

$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{a_1}{a_2}=\frac{b_1}{b_2}=\frac{c_1}{c_2} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{2}{4}=\frac{-\lambda}{-1}=\frac{1}{\mu} \\ & \Rightarrow \lambda=\frac{1}{2} \text { and } \mu=2 \\ & \Rightarrow \lambda+\mu=\frac{1}{2}+2=\frac{5}{2} \end{aligned} $$

content: The perimeter of a square whose two sides have equations $\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y+2}{3}=\frac{z-3}{4}$ and $\frac{x}{2}=\frac{y-1}{3}=\frac{z+1}{4}$ is
explanation:
l: $\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y+2}{3}=\frac{z-3}{4}$ and

l2: $\frac{x}{2}=\frac{y-1}{3}=\frac{z+1}{4}$

Let $r_1$ and $r_2$ be the position vectors of point on lines $l_1$ and $l_2$ respectively.

$$ \therefore \quad \bar{r}_1=(1,-2,3) \text { and } \bar{r}_2=(0,1,-1) $$

and direction ratios of line $l_1$ and $l_2$ are

$$ \overline{\mathrm{d}}=(2,3,4) $$

$\therefore \quad$ Lines are parallel

Distance between parallel lines $l_1$ and $l_2$ is given by,

$$ \begin{array}{ll} & \mathrm{D}=\frac{\left|\left(\bar{r}_2-\bar{r}_1\right) \times \bar{d}\right|}{|\bar{d}|} \\ & \bar{r}_2-\bar{r}_1=(-1,3,-4) \\ \therefore & \left(\bar{r}_2-\bar{r}_1\right) \times \bar{d}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ -1 & 3 & -4 \\ 2 & 3 & 4 \end{array}\right| \\ & =\hat{i}(12+12)-\hat{j}(-4+8)+\hat{k}(-3-6) \\ & =24 \hat{i}-4 \hat{j}-9 \hat{k} \\ \Rightarrow & \left|\left(\bar{r}_2-\bar{r}_1\right) \times \vec{d}\right|=\sqrt{(24)^2+(-4)^2+(-9)^2} \\ &\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, =\sqrt{673} \end{array} $$

$$ \begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \text { And }|\overline{\mathrm{d}}|=\sqrt{(2)^2+(3)^2+(4)^2}=\sqrt{29} \\ \therefore \quad & \text { Distance }(\mathrm{D})=\frac{\sqrt{673}}{\sqrt{29}} \\ & \text { Perimeter of square }=4 \times \text { Distance between } \\ & \text { lines } \\ & =4 \times \frac{\sqrt{673}}{\sqrt{29}} \\ & =\frac{4 \sqrt{673}}{\sqrt{29}} \text { units } \end{array} $$

content: If the sum of the squares of the distances of a point $\mathrm{P}(x, y, z)$ from the three co-ordinate axes is 324 , then the distance of point P from the origin is ….
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} &\text { According to given condition, }\\ &\begin{aligned} & \left(\sqrt{y^2+z^2}\right)^2+\left(\sqrt{x^2+z^2}\right)^2+\left(\sqrt{x^2+y^2}\right)^2=324 \\ & y^2+z^2+x^2+z^2+x^2+y^2=324 \\ & 2 x^2+2 y^2+2 z^2=324 \\ & x^2+y^2+z^2=\frac{324}{2} \\ & x^2+y^2+z^2=162 \end{aligned}\\ &\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \text { Distance of } \mathrm{OP} & =\sqrt{x^2+y^2+z^2} \\ & =\sqrt{162} \\ & =9 \sqrt{2} \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

content: The angle between the lines $3 x=2 y=-\mathrm{z}$ and $-x=6 y=-4 z$ is
explanation:
We are given the lines:

$ 3x = 2y = -z $

and

$ -x = 6y = -4z $

We want the angle between them.

Step 1: Find direction vector of the first line

Equation: $3x = 2y = -z = k.$

So we can parametrize with $k$:

$ x = \frac{k}{3},\ y = \frac{k}{2},\ z = -k $

Thus the direction vector is proportional to

$ \left(\tfrac{1}{3}, \tfrac{1}{2}, -1\right). $

To clear denominators multiply by 6:

$ (2, 3, -6). $

Step 2: Find direction vector of the second line

Equation: $-x = 6y = -4z = m.$

So

$ x = -m,\ y = \tfrac{m}{6},\ z = -\tfrac{m}{4}. $

Direction vector:

$ (-1, \tfrac{1}{6}, -\tfrac{1}{4}). $

Multiply through by 12:

$ (-12, 2, -3). $

Step 3: Angle between vectors

Dot product:

$ (2, 3, -6)\cdot(-12, 2, -3) = 2(-12) + 3(2) + (-6)(-3). $

Compute:

$ = -24 + 6 + 18 = 0. $

So vectors are perpendicular.

Step 4: Conclusion

Thus the angle between the lines is

$ \frac{\pi}{2}. $

Answer:

$ \text{Option C: } \frac{\pi}{2} $

content: If the lines $\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y+1}{\mathrm{k}}=\frac{\mathrm{z}}{2}$ and $\frac{x+1}{5}=\frac{y+1}{2}=\frac{\mathrm{z}}{\mathrm{k}}$ are coplanar, then the equation of the plane containing these lines are
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Let } l_1: \frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y+1}{\mathrm{k}}=\frac{\mathrm{z}}{2} \text { and }\\ &\begin{aligned} & l_2: \frac{x+1}{5}=\frac{y+1}{2}=\frac{\mathrm{z}}{\mathrm{k}} \\ & \Rightarrow x_1=1, y_1=-1, \mathrm{z}=0, \mathrm{a}_1=2, \mathrm{~b}_1=\mathrm{k}, \mathrm{c}_1=2 \\ & x_2=-1, y_2=-1, \mathrm{z}_2=0, \mathrm{a}_2=5, \mathrm{~b}_2=2, \mathrm{c}_2=\mathrm{k} \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

Given lines are coplanar

$$ \begin{aligned} \therefore & \left|\begin{array}{ccc} x_2-x_1 & y_2-y_1 & z_2-z_1 \\ a_1 & b_1 & c_1 \\ a_2 & b_2 & c_2 \end{array}\right|=0 \\ & \left|\begin{array}{ccc} -2 & 0 & 0 \\ 2 & \mathrm{k} & 2 \\ 5 & 2 & \mathrm{k} \end{array}\right|=0 \\ & \Rightarrow-2\left(\mathrm{k}^2-4\right)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{k}= \pm 2 \end{aligned} $$

The equation of the plane contains line $l_1$ and $l_2$ is given by,

$$ \left|\begin{array}{ccc} x-x_1 & y-y_1 & z-z_1 \\ a_1 & b_1 & c_1 \\ a_2 & b_2 & c_2 \end{array}\right|=0 $$

For $\mathrm{k}=2$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \left|\begin{array}{ccc} x-1 & y+1 & z \\ 2 & 2 & 2 \\ 5 & 2 & 2 \end{array}\right|=0 \\ & \Rightarrow(x-1)(0)-(y+1)(4-10)+z(4-10)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow-(y+1)(-6)+z(-6)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 6 y+6-6 z=0 \\ & \Rightarrow y-z+1=0 \end{aligned} $$

Now for $\mathrm{k}=-2$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \left|\begin{array}{ccc} x-1 & y+1 & z \\ 2 & -2 & 2 \\ 5 & 2 & -2 \end{array}\right|=0 \\ & \Rightarrow y+z+1=0 \end{aligned} $$

content: Let the line $\frac{x-2}{3}=\frac{y-1}{-5}=\frac{z+2}{2}$ lie in the plane $x+3 y-\alpha z+\beta=0$, then the value of $(\beta-\alpha)$ is equal to
explanation:
We are asked:

Let the line

$ > \frac{x-2}{3} \;=\; \frac{y-1}{-5} \;=\; \frac{z+2}{2} > $

lie in the plane

$ > x + 3y - \alpha z + \beta = 0, > $

then find $(\beta - \alpha)$.

Step 1: Write parametric form of line

Let the parameter be $t$. Then:

$ x = 2 + 3t, \quad y = 1 - 5t, \quad z = -2 + 2t. $

So point on the line when $t=0$ is $P(2,1,-2)$. The direction vector of line is $(3,-5,2)$.

Step 2: Condition for line to lie in plane

If the entire line lies in the plane, then:

  1. The direction vector of the line must be parallel to the plane (so its dot product with plane's normal is zero).

  2. One point on the line must satisfy the plane equation.

  • Normal vector of plane: $(1,3,-\alpha)$.

  • Direction vector of line: $(3,-5,2)$.

So condition:

$ (1,3,-\alpha)\cdot(3,-5,2) = 0. $

Compute:

$ 3 + (3)(-5) + (-\alpha)(2) = 0. $

$ 3 - 15 - 2\alpha = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; -12 - 2\alpha = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \alpha = -6. $

Step 3: Substitute point into plane

Equation of plane:

$ x + 3y - \alpha z + \beta = 0. $

With $\alpha = -6$, equation is

$ x + 3y -(-6)z + \beta = x + 3y + 6z + \beta = 0. $

Point $P(2,1,-2)$ lies on it, so:

$ 2 + 3(1) + 6(-2) + \beta = 0. $

$ 2 + 3 - 12 + \beta = 0. $

$ -7 + \beta = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \beta = 7. $

Step 4: Find $\beta - \alpha$

$ \beta - \alpha = 7 - (-6) = 13. $

✅ Final Answer:

$ \boxed{13} $

Correct option: B.

content: The direction ratios of the line of intersection of the planes $x-y+z-5=0$ and $x-3 y-6=0$, are

The distance between the line $\overline{\mathrm{r}}=3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}+\lambda(\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}})$ and the plane $\overline{\mathrm{r}} \cdot(2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}})=4$ is
explanation:
Let $\overline{\mathrm{r}}=3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\mathrm{k}+\lambda(\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}})$

$\Rightarrow$ point on the line is, $(3,-2,1)$

Given equation of plane is

$$ \overline{\mathrm{r}}(2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}})=4 $$

i.e. $2 x+y+z-4=0$

Distance between point on the line and plane is given by,

$$ \begin{aligned} D & =\frac{|a x+b y+c z+d|}{\sqrt{a^2+b^2+c^2}} \\ & =\frac{|2(3)+1(-2)+1(1)|}{\sqrt{2^2+1^2+1^2}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{6}} \text { units } \end{aligned} $$

content: The angle between the lines whose direction cosines are $\frac{-\sqrt{3}}{4}, \frac{1}{4}, \frac{-\sqrt{3}}{2}$ and $\frac{-\sqrt{3}}{4}, \frac{1}{4}, \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$ is
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} & \quad \cos \theta=l_1 l_2+\mathrm{m}_1 \mathrm{~m}_2+\mathrm{n}_1 \mathrm{n}_2 \\ & =\frac{-\sqrt{3}}{4} \times \frac{-\sqrt{3}}{4}+\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{1}{4}+\frac{-\sqrt{3}}{2} \times \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \\ & =\frac{3}{16}+\frac{1}{16}-\frac{3}{4} \\ & =\frac{1}{4}-\frac{3}{4} \\ & =-\frac{1}{2} \\ & \therefore \quad \theta=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{-1}{2}\right) \\ & \therefore \quad \theta=120^{\circ} \end{aligned} $$

content: The length of the altitude through the point $D$ of tetrahedron where the vertices of the tetrahedron are $A(2,3,1), B(4,1,-2), C(6,3,7), D(-5,-4,8)$, is
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Let, }\\ &\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{A}=(2,3,1) \\ & \mathrm{B}=(4,1,-2) \\ & \mathrm{C}=(6,3,7) \\ & \mathrm{D}=(-5,-4,8) \\ & \overline{\mathrm{AB}}=(2,-2,-3) \\ & \overline{\mathrm{AC}}=(4,0,6), \overline{\mathrm{AD}}=(-7,-7,7) \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Normal vectors to the plane is given by, }\\ &\begin{aligned} \begin{aligned} \overline{\mathrm{AB}} \times \overline{\mathrm{AC}} & =\bar{n}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{j} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 2 & -2 & -3 \\ 4 & 0 & 6 \end{array}\right| \\ & =\hat{i}(-12+0)-j(12+12)+\hat{k}(0+8) \\ & =-12 \hat{i}-24 \hat{j}+8 \hat{k} \\& \Rightarrow \bar{n}=(-12,-24,8) & \\ \text { Distance } & =\frac{|\overline{\mathrm{AD}} \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{AB}} \times \overline{\mathrm{AC}})|}{|\overline{\mathrm{AB}} \times \overline{\mathrm{AC}}|} \\ & =\frac{(-7)(-12)+(-7)(-24)+(7)(8)}{\sqrt{(-12)^2+(-24)^2+8^2}} \\ & =\frac{308}{\sqrt{784}}=\frac{308}{28}=11 \text { units. } \end{aligned} \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

content: The angle between the lines $\frac{x-1}{l}=\frac{y+1}{m}=\frac{z}{n}$ and $\frac{x+1}{\mathrm{~m}}=\frac{y-3}{\mathrm{n}}=\frac{\mathrm{z}-1}{l}$, where $l>\mathrm{m}>\mathrm{n}$ and $1, \mathrm{~m}, \mathrm{n}$ are roots of the equation $x^3+x^2-4 x-4=0$, is
explanation:
Given lines are

$$ \frac{x-1}{l}=\frac{y+1}{\mathrm{~m}}=\frac{\mathrm{z}}{\mathrm{n}} \text { and } \frac{x+1}{\mathrm{~m}}=\frac{y-3}{\mathrm{n}}=\frac{\mathrm{z}-1}{l} $$

Angle between lines

$$ \begin{aligned} & \cos \theta=\left[\frac{l \mathrm{~m}+\mathrm{mn}+\mathrm{n} l}{\sqrt{l^2+\mathrm{m}^2+\mathrm{n}^2} \cdot \sqrt{\mathrm{~m}^2+\mathrm{n}^2+l^2}}\right] \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)\\ & \cos \theta=\left[\frac{l \mathrm{~m}+\mathrm{mn}+\mathrm{n} l}{l^2+\mathrm{m}^2+\mathrm{n}^2}\right] \end{aligned} $$

Given equation is $x^3+x^2-4 x-4=0$

Sum of roots $=l+\mathrm{m}+\mathrm{n}=-1$

Also, $l \mathrm{~m}+\mathrm{mn}+\mathrm{n} l=-4$

Using, $(l+\mathrm{m}+\mathrm{n})^2=l^2+\mathrm{m}^2+\mathrm{n}^2 +2(l \mathrm{~m}+\mathrm{mn}+\mathrm{n} l) $

$$ \begin{aligned} & 1=l^2+\mathrm{m}^2+\mathrm{n}^2+2(-4) \\ & \Rightarrow l^2+\mathrm{m}^2+\mathrm{n}^2=9 \end{aligned} $$

From (i),

$$ \cos \theta=\frac{-4}{9} \Rightarrow \theta=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{-4}{9}\right) $$

content: The distance of the point $\mathrm{P}(3,8,2)$ from the line $\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y-3}{4}=\frac{z-2}{3}$ measured parallel to the plane $3 x+2 y-2 z+15=0$ is
explanation:
Let point Q lies on line

$$ \frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y-3}{4}=\frac{z-2}{3}=t $$

$\therefore \quad$ Co-ordinates of Q are

$$ \begin{array}{ll} & (2 t+1,4 t+3,3 t+2) \\ \therefore \quad & P Q=(2 t-2,4 t-5,3 t) \end{array} $$

Since PQ is parallel to plane

$$ \begin{array}{ll} & 3 x+2 y-2 z+15=0 \\ \therefore & 3(2 t-2)+2(4 t-5)-2(3 t)=0 \\ & 6 t-6+8 t-10-6 t=0 \\ & \Rightarrow t=2 \end{array} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \mathrm{Q} \equiv(5,11,8) \\ & \therefore \quad \mathrm{d}(\mathrm{PQ})=\sqrt{(5-3)^3+(11-8)^2+(8-2)^2} \\ &\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,=\sqrt{4+9+36} \\&\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,=7 \text { units. } \end{aligned} $$

content: The shortest distance between the lines $\bar{r}=(4 \hat{i}-\hat{j})+\lambda(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k})$ and $\bar{r}=(\hat{i}-\hat{j}+2 \hat{k})+\mu(2 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k})$ is
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} & \text { Here, } \overline{a_1}=4 \hat{i}-\hat{j}, \overline{b_1}=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k} \\ & \overline{a_2}=\hat{i}-\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}, \bar{b}_2=2 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k} \\ & \begin{aligned} \bar{a}_2-\bar{a}_1 & =-3 \hat{i}+2 \hat{k} \\ \bar{b}_1 \times \bar{b}_2= & \left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 1 & 2 & -3 \\ 2 & 4 & -5 \end{array}\right|=2 \hat{i}-\hat{j} \end{aligned} \\ & \begin{aligned} \left|\bar{b}_1 \times \bar{b}_2\right| & =\sqrt{5} \end{aligned} \\ & \text { Shortest distance }=\left|\frac{\left(\bar{a}_2-\bar{a}_1\right) \cdot\left(\bar{b}_1 \times \bar{b}_2\right) \mid}{\left|\bar{b}_1 \times \bar{b}_2\right|}\right| \\ & =\left|\frac{(-3 \hat{i}+2 \hat{k}) \cdot(2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}) \mid}{\sqrt{5}}\right| \\ & =\frac{6}{\sqrt{5}} \text { units } \end{aligned} $$

content: If the distance of the point $\mathrm{P}(1,-2,1)$ from the plane $x+2 y-2 z=\alpha$, where $\alpha>0$ is 5 units, then the foot of the perpendicular from P to the plane is
explanation:
Distance of point $\mathrm{P}(1,-2,1)$ from the plane $x+2 y-2 z=\alpha$ is 5 .

$$ \begin{aligned} \therefore & \frac{|1-4-2-\alpha|}{\sqrt{1+4+4}}=5 \\ & \Rightarrow|\alpha+5|=15 \\ & \Rightarrow \alpha+5= \pm 15 \\ & \Rightarrow \alpha=10,-20 \\ & \Rightarrow \alpha=10 \,\,\,\,\,\ldots[\because \alpha>0]\end{aligned} $$

The equation of line PM whose d.r.s. are $1,2,-2$ is

$$ \frac{x-1}{1}=\frac{y+2}{2}=\frac{z-1}{2}=\lambda(\text { say }) $$

The co-ordinates of $M$ are

$$ (\lambda+1,2 \lambda-2,-2 \lambda+1) $$

Since $M$ lies on the plane

$$ \begin{array}{ll} \quad & x+2 y-2 z=10 \\\therefore & \lambda+1+4 \lambda-4+4 \lambda-2=10 \\ \Rightarrow & 9 \lambda=15 \\ \Rightarrow & \lambda=\frac{5}{3} \end{array} $$

Hence, the co-ordinates of M are $\left(\frac{8}{3}, \frac{4}{3}, \frac{-7}{3}\right)$

content: The equation of the plane containing the line $\frac{x-2}{3}=\frac{y+1}{2}=\frac{z-4}{-2}$ and the point $(0,5,0)$ is
explanation:
The equation of plane passing through $(0,5,0)$ is

$$ \begin{aligned} & \mathrm{a}(x-0)+\mathrm{b}(y-5)+\mathrm{c}(\mathrm{z}-0)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{a} x+\mathrm{b}(y-5)+\mathrm{cz}=0 \end{aligned} $$

The plane contains the line

$$ \frac{x-2}{3}=\frac{y+1}{2}=\frac{z-4}{-2} $$

i.e. it passes through $(2,-1,4)$

$$ \begin{array}{ll} \therefore \quad & a(2)+b(-1-5)+c(4)=0 \\ \therefore \quad & 2 a-6 b+4 c=0 \\ & \text { i.e. } a-3 b+2 c=0 \\ & \text { Also, } 3 a+2 b-2 c=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{a}{2}=\frac{b}{8}=\frac{c}{11} \end{array} $$

$\therefore \quad$ The equation of plane is

$$ 2 x+8 y+11 z-40=0 \quad \ldots[\text { From (i) }] $$

content: The equation of the plane passing through the point of intersection of the planes $2 x-y+z-3=0$ and $4 x-3 y+5 z+9=0$ and parallel to the line $\frac{x+1}{2}=\frac{y+3}{4}=\frac{z-3}{5}$ is $\alpha x+\beta y+\gamma z+d=0$ Then $\alpha+\beta+\gamma+d=$
explanation:
Equation of the plane passing through the intersection of given planes is

$$ (2 x-y+z-3)+\lambda(4 x-3 y+5 z+9)=0 $$

$$ \begin{array}{r} \Rightarrow(2+4 \lambda) x+(-1-3 \lambda) y+(1+5 \lambda) z -3+9 \lambda=0 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)\end{array} $$

Direction ratios of the line $\frac{x+1}{2}=\frac{y+3}{4}=\frac{z-3}{5}$ are $2,4,5$.

Since the plane is parallel to the given line,

$$ \begin{aligned} & (2+4 \lambda)(2)+(-1-3 \lambda)(4)+(1+5 \lambda)(5)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 4+8 \lambda-4-12 \lambda+5+25 \lambda=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 5+21 \lambda=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \lambda=\frac{-5}{21} \end{aligned} $$

Substituting $\lambda=\frac{-5}{21}$ in equation (i), we get

$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{22}{21} x-\frac{6}{21} y-\frac{4}{21} z-\frac{108}{21}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 11 x-3 y-2 z-54=0 \end{aligned} $$

Comparing with $\alpha x+\beta y+\gamma z+\mathrm{d}=0$, we get

$$ \begin{aligned} & \alpha=11, \beta=-3, \gamma=-2, d=-54 \\ & \Rightarrow \alpha+\beta+\gamma+d=11-3-2-54=-48 \end{aligned} $$

content: The distance of the point $(2,4,0)$ from the point of intersection of the lines $\frac{x+6}{3}=\frac{y}{2}=\frac{z+1}{1}$ and $\frac{x-7}{4}=\frac{y-9}{3}=\frac{z-4}{2}$ is
The co-ordinates of the point where the line joining the points $(2,-3,1)$ and $(3,-4,-5)$ and intersects the plane $2 x+y+z=7$ are
explanation:
The equation of the line passing through the points ( $2,-3,1$ ) and ( $3,-4,-5$ ) is

$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{x-2}{3-2}=\frac{y+3}{-4+3}=\frac{z-1}{-5-1} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{x-2}{1}=\frac{y+3}{-1}=\frac{z-1}{-6}=t(\text { say }) \\ & \Rightarrow x=t+2, y=-t-3, z=-6 t+1 \end{aligned} $$

Substituting the values of $x, y, \mathrm{z}$ in the given equation of plane, we get

$$ \begin{array}{ll} & 2(\mathrm{t}+2)+(-\mathrm{t}-3)+(-6 \mathrm{t}+1)=7 \\ & \Rightarrow 2-5 \mathrm{t}=7 \\ & \Rightarrow 5 \mathrm{t}=-5 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{t}=-1 \\ \therefore \quad & x=-1+2=1 \\ & y=-(-1)-3=-2 \\ & \mathrm{z}=-6(-1)+1=7 \\ \therefore \quad & (x, y, \mathrm{z})=(1,-2,7) \end{array} $$

content: If the line $\frac{x+1}{3}=\frac{y-k}{7}=\frac{z-4}{8}$ lies in the plane $2 x+\mathrm{p} y+7 z-41=0$ which is perpendicular to the plane $x+4 y-2 z+13=0$ then $\mathrm{k}=$
explanation: $$ \begin{array}{ll} & 2 x+\mathrm{p} y+7 \mathrm{z}-41=0 \text { is perpendi } \\ & x+4 y-2 z+13=0 \\ \therefore & 2(1)+\mathrm{p}(4)+7(-2)=0 \\ \Rightarrow & 4 \mathrm{p}=12 \\ \Rightarrow & \mathrm{p}=3 \\ & \text { Point }(-1, \mathrm{k}, 4) \text { lies on the plane } \\ & 2 x+\mathrm{p} y+7 \mathrm{z}-41=0 \\ \therefore & 2(-1)+3(\mathrm{k})+7(4)-41=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 3 \mathrm{k}=15 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{k}=5 \end{array} $$

content: The direction cosines of the line $x-y+2 z=5$ and $3 x+y+z=6$ are
explanation:
Given equation of planes are

$$ \begin{aligned} & x-y+2 z=5 \\ & 3 x+y+z=6 \end{aligned} $$

Vectors normal to given planes are

$$ \begin{aligned} & \overline{n_1}=\hat{i}-\hat{j}+2 \hat{k} \text { and } \\ \overline{n_2} & =3 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k} \\ \bar{n} & =\overline{n_1} \times \overline{n_2} \\ & =\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 1 & -1 & 2 \\ 3 & 1 & 1 \end{array}\right| \\ & =-3 \hat{i}+5 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k} \\ \therefore \quad \hat{n} & =\frac{-3}{5 \sqrt{2}} \hat{i}+\frac{5}{5 \sqrt{2}} \hat{j}+\frac{4}{5 \sqrt{2}} \hat{k} \end{aligned} $$

content: If the angle between the planes $x-2 y+3 z-5=0$ and $x+\alpha y+2 z+7=0$ is $\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{14}\right)$ then the difference between the values of $\alpha$ is
explanation:
The d.r.s of normal to the given planes are $1,-2,3$ and $1, \alpha, 2$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \cos \theta=\frac{1(1)-2(\alpha)+3(2)}{\sqrt{1+4+9} \cdot \sqrt{1+\alpha^2+4}} \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{1}{14}=\frac{7-2 \alpha}{\sqrt{14\left(5+\alpha^2\right)}} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \frac{1}{14^2}=\frac{(7-2 \alpha)^2}{14\left(5+\alpha^2\right)} \,\,\,\,\, …[Squaring\,\, both \,\,\,sides]\\ \therefore & 14(7-2 \alpha)^2=\alpha^2+5\end{array} $$

On solving, we get

$$ 55 \alpha^2-392 \alpha+681=0 $$

$\therefore \quad$ Roots are $\frac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2-4 a c}}{2 a}$

$$ \begin{aligned} \therefore \text { Difference between roots } & =\frac{\sqrt{b^2-4 a c}}{a} \\ & =\frac{\sqrt{(-392)^2-4(55)(681)}}{55} \\ & =\frac{\sqrt{153664-149820}}{55} \\ & =\frac{\sqrt{3844}}{55} \\ & =\frac{62}{55} \end{aligned} $$

content: If the shortest distance between the lines $\frac{x-\mathrm{k}}{2}=\frac{y-4}{3}=\frac{\mathrm{z}-3}{4}$ and $\frac{x-2}{4}=\frac{y-4}{6}=\frac{\mathrm{z}-7}{8}$ is $\frac{13}{\sqrt{29}}$, then $\mathrm{k}=$
explanation:
$$ \begin{array}{ll} & \bar{r}_1=(k \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k})+\alpha(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}) \\ & \bar{r}_2=2 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}+\beta(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}) \\ \therefore \quad & \bar{b}=2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k} \\ & \bar{a}_1=k \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k} \\ & \bar{a}_2=2 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k} \\ & \text { Shortest distance }=\frac{\left|\bar{b} \times\left(\bar{a}_2-\bar{a}_1\right)\right|}{|b|} \end{array} $$

$$ \begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \frac{13}{\sqrt{29}}=\frac{\mid(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}) \times[(2-k) \hat{i}+4 \hat{k}]}{\sqrt{29}} \\ \therefore & |-8 \hat{j}-3(2-k) \hat{k}+12 \hat{i}+4(2-k) \hat{j}|=13 \\ \therefore & |12 \hat{i}-4 k \hat{j}+(3 k-6) \hat{k}|=13 \\ \therefore & 144+16 k^2+(3 k-6)^2=169 \\ \therefore & 16 k^2+(3 k-6)^2=25 \\ \therefore & k=1 \end{array} $$

content: The acute angle between the lines $x=-2+2 \mathrm{t}, y=3-4 \mathrm{t}, \mathrm{z}=-4+\mathrm{t}$ and $x=-2-\mathrm{t}, y=3+2 \mathrm{t}, \mathrm{z}=-4+3 \mathrm{t}$ is
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Given equations of lines are }\\ &\begin{aligned} & x=-2+2 t, y=3-4 t, z=-4+t \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{x+2}{2}=\frac{y-3}{-4}=\frac{z+4}{1}=t \quad \text { and } \\ & x=-2-t, y=3+2 t, z=-4+3 t \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{x+2}{-1}=\frac{y-3}{2}=\frac{z+4}{3}=t \\ & a_1, b_1, c_1=2,-4,1 \\ & a_2, b_2, c_2=-1,2,3 \\ & \cos \theta=\left|\frac{a_1 a_2+b_1 b_2+c_1 c_2}{\sqrt{a_1{ }^2+b_1{ }^2+c_1{ }^2} \sqrt{a_2{ }^2+b_2{ }^2+c_2{ }^2}}\right| \\ & =\left|\frac{-2-8+3}{\sqrt{4+16+1} \sqrt{1+4+9}}\right| \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{gathered} =\left|\frac{7}{\sqrt{21} \sqrt{14}}\right| \\ =\frac{1}{\sqrt{6}} \\ \therefore \quad \theta=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{6}}\right) \end{gathered} $$

content: If the plane $\frac{x}{3}+\frac{y}{2}-\frac{z}{4}=1$ cuts the co-ordinate axes at points $\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}$ and C , then the area of the triangle ABC is
explanation:
Given equation of plane is $\frac{x}{3}+\frac{y}{2}-\frac{z}{4}=1$

For $\mathrm{A}, y=0, \mathrm{z}=0$

$$ \therefore \quad A \equiv(3,0,0) $$

For $\mathrm{B}, x=0, \mathrm{z}=0$

$$ \therefore \quad B \equiv(0,2,0) $$

For $\mathrm{C}, x=0, y=0$

$\therefore \quad \mathrm{C} \equiv(0,0,-4)$

If a triangle is formed by the intercepts $\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}, \mathrm{c}$ on the $\mathrm{X}, \mathrm{Y}$ and Z axes, then area of triangle is giv-

en by $\mathrm{A}=\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{\mathrm{a}^2 \mathrm{~b}^2+\mathrm{b}^2 \mathrm{c}^2+\mathrm{a}^2 \mathrm{c}^2}$

$$ \begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \text { Required area } & =\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{9 \times 4+4 \times 16+16 \times 9} \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{36+64+144} \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{244} \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \times 2 \sqrt{61} \\ & =\sqrt{61} \text { sq. units } \end{aligned} $$

content: A plane passes through $(2,1,2)$ and $(1,2,1)$ and parallel to the line $2 x=3 y$ and $\mathrm{z}=1$, then the plane also passes through the point
explanation:
A plane passes through the points, $A=(2,1,2)$ and $B=(1,2,1)$ So, direction vector in the plane is,

$$ \begin{aligned} & \overrightarrow{\mathrm{AB}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}-\overrightarrow{\mathrm{A}}=(1,2,1)-(2,1,2) \\ & =(-1,1,-1) \end{aligned} $$

Since plane is parallel to the line $2 x=3 y$ and $\mathrm{z}=1$

$$ \Rightarrow \frac{x}{3}=\frac{y}{2}=\mathrm{k} \text { and } \mathrm{z}=1 $$

So, the direction vector of the line is,

$$ \overrightarrow{\mathrm{d}}=(3,2,0) $$

Now, taking cross product to get normal vector

$$ \begin{aligned} & \overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{AB}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{d}}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \mathrm{i} & \mathrm{j} & \mathrm{k} \\ -1 & 1 & -1 \\ 3 & 2 & 0 \end{array}\right| \\ & =\hat{\mathrm{i}}(0+2)-\hat{\mathrm{j}}(0+3)+\hat{\mathrm{k}}(-2-3) \\ & \Rightarrow \overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}=(2,-3,-5) \end{aligned} $$

Equation of the plane is given by,

$$ \begin{aligned} & a\left(x-x_1\right)+b\left(y-y_1\right)+c\left(z-z_1\right)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 2(x-2)-3(y-1)-5(z-2)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 2 x-3 y-5 z+9=0 \end{aligned} $$

Option: $(-2,0,1)$ satisfies the equation of plane.

$\Rightarrow$ plane passes through the point $(-2,0,1)$

content: The equation of the plane passing through the line of intersection of the planes $x+y+z=1$ and $3 x+4 y+5 z=2$ and perpendicular to the XY- plane is
explanation:
The equation of the plane passing through the line of intersection of the planes $P_1$ and $P_2$ is, $P_1+\lambda P_2=0$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow(x+y+z-1)+\lambda(3 x+4 y+5 z-2)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow(1+3 \lambda) x+(1+4 \lambda) y+(1+5 \lambda) z-(1+2 \lambda)=0 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)\end{aligned} $$

Plane is $\perp$ to the XY plane …(Given)

coefficient of $z=0$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow 1+5 \lambda=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \lambda=\frac{-1}{5} \end{aligned} $$

Substituting $\lambda=\frac{-1}{5}$ in equation (i), we get

$$ \begin{aligned} & \left(1+3\left(\frac{-1}{5}\right)\right) x+\left(1+4\left(\frac{-1}{5}\right)\right) y+\left(1+5\left(\frac{-1}{5}\right)\right) z -\left(1+2\left(\frac{-1}{5}\right)\right)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{2}{5} x+\frac{1}{5} y+0 . z-\frac{3}{5}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 2 x+y-3=0 \end{aligned} $$

content: The coordinates of the foot of the perpendicular drawn from a point $\mathrm{P}(-1,1,2)$ to the plane $2 x-3 y+z-11=0$ are
explanation:
Let the foot of the $\perp$ be $\mathrm{Q}(x, y, z)$.

Direction ratios of QP are $(2,-3,1)$

Point Q lies on a line QP

passing through $\mathrm{P}(-1,1,2)$ with direction ratios $(2,-3,1)$.

Parametric equation of the line are,

$$ x=-1+2 \mathrm{t}, y=1-3 \mathrm{t}, \mathrm{z}=2+\mathrm{t} $$

Also, Q lies on the given plane.

$$ \begin{array}{ll} \therefore & 2(-1+2 t)-3(1-3 t)+(2+t)=11 \\ & \Rightarrow t=1 \end{array} $$

Hence, $\mathrm{Q}=(x, y, \mathrm{z})=(1,-2,3)$

content: The lines $\frac{x-3}{1}=\frac{y-2}{1}=\frac{z-5}{-k}$ and $\frac{x-4}{\mathrm{k}}=\frac{y-3}{1}=\frac{\mathrm{z}-3}{2}$ are coplanar, hence $\mathrm{k}=$
explanation:
Here,

$$ \begin{aligned} & x_1=3, y_1=2, \mathrm{z}_1=5 \\ & \mathrm{a}_1=1, \mathrm{~b}_1=1, \mathrm{c}_1=-\mathrm{k} \\ & x_2=4, y_2=3, \mathrm{z}_2=3 \\ & \mathrm{a}_2=\mathrm{k}, \mathrm{~b}_2=1, \mathrm{c}_2=2 \end{aligned} $$

Since the given lines are coplanar.

$$ \begin{aligned} \therefore & \left|\begin{array}{ccc} x_2-x_1 & y_2-y_1 & z_2-z_1 \\ a_1 & b_1 & c_1 \\ a_2 & b_2 & c_2 \end{array}\right|=0 \\ & \Rightarrow\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 1 & -2 \\ 1 & 1 & -k \\ k & 1 & 2 \end{array}\right|=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 1(2+k)-1\left(2+k^2\right)-2(1-k)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 2+k-2-k^2-2+2 k=0 \\ & \Rightarrow k^2-3 k+2=0 \\ & \Rightarrow(k-1)(k-2)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow k=1 \text { or } k=2 \end{aligned} $$

content: If the shortest distance between the lines $\bar{r}_1=\alpha \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}+\lambda(\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}), \lambda \in \mathbb{R}, \alpha>0 \quad$ and $\bar{r}_2=-4 \hat{i}-\hat{k}+\mu(3 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k}), \mu \in R$, is 9 , then the value of $\alpha$ is
explanation:
Shortest distance between the lines $\overline{\mathrm{r}}=\overline{\mathrm{a}_1}+\lambda \overline{\mathrm{b}_1}$ and $\overline{\mathrm{r}}=\overline{\mathrm{a}_2}+\mu \overline{\mathrm{b}_2}$ is

$$ \bar{d}=\left|\frac{\left(\overline{a_2}-\overline{a_1}\right) \cdot\left(\overline{b_1} \times \overline{b_2}\right)}{\left|\overline{b_1} \times \overline{b_2}\right|}\right| $$

Here, $\overline{a_1}=\alpha \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}, \overline{a_2}=-4 \hat{i}-\hat{k}$,

$$ \overline{b_1}=\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}, \overline{b_2}=3 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k} $$

Now, $\overline{a_2}-\overline{a_1}=(-4-\alpha) \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \overline{b_1} \times \overline{b_2}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 1 & -2 & 2 \\ 3 & -2 & -2 \end{array}\right| \\ & =\hat{i}(4+4)-\hat{j}(-2-6)+\hat{k}(-2+6) \\ & =8 \hat{i}+8 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\therefore \quad \text { shortest distance (d) }\\ &\begin{aligned} & =\left|\frac{(-4-\alpha) \hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}) \cdot(8 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+8 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{k}})}{\sqrt{64+64+16}}\right| \\ & \Rightarrow 9=\frac{|-60-8 \alpha|}{12} \\ & \Rightarrow 108=|-60-8 \alpha| \\ & \Rightarrow-60-8 \alpha= \pm 108 \\ & \text { Since } \alpha>0 \\ & -60-8 \alpha=-108 \\ & \Rightarrow \alpha=6 \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

content: The Cartesian equation of plane through $\mathrm{A}(7,8,6)$ and parallel to the XY plane is
explanation:
The equation of plane through $A(7,8,6)$ is

$$a(x-7)+b(y-8)+c(z-6)=0\quad\text{...... (i)}$$

Since, the required plane is parallel to XY -plane

$\therefore \quad$ Direction ratios are $0,0,1$

$\therefore \quad$ Equation of plane is

$$\begin{aligned} & 0(x-7)+0(y-0)+1(z-6)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow z-6=0 \\ & \Rightarrow z=6 \end{aligned}$$

content: The distance of the point $(-3,2,3)$ from the line passing through $(4,6,-2)$ and having direction ratios $-1,2,3$ is $\qquad$ units.
explanation:
Let $\mathrm{P} \equiv(-3,2,3)$

Equation of line passing through $(4,6,-2)$ and having d.r.'s $-1,2,3$ are

$$\frac{x-4}{-1}=\frac{y-6}{2}=\frac{z+2}{3}$$

Let $\frac{x-4}{-1}=\frac{y-6}{2}=\frac{z+2}{3}=\lambda$

$$\Rightarrow x=4-\lambda, y=2 \lambda+6, \mathrm{z}=3 \lambda-2$$

Co-ordinates of point

$$Q=(4-\lambda, 2 \lambda+6,3 \lambda-2)$$

$\therefore \quad$ the d.r's of PQ are

$$\begin{aligned} & (4-\lambda-(-3), 6+2 \lambda-2,-2+3 \lambda-3) \\ & \equiv 7-\lambda, 4+2 \lambda,-5+3 \lambda \end{aligned}$$

Since PQ is perpendicular to given lines

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & -1(7-\lambda)+(2)(4+2 \lambda)+3(-5+3 \lambda)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow-7+\lambda+8+4 \lambda-15+9 \lambda=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \lambda=1 \end{array}$$

$\therefore \quad$ Co-ordinates of Q are $(3,8,1)$

$$\therefore \quad \mathrm{PQ}=\sqrt{(3-(-3))^2+(8-2)^2+(1-3)^2}$$

$\qquad=\sqrt{76}=2\sqrt{19}$

content: A plane passes through $(1,-2,1)$ and is perpendicular to the planes $2 x-2 y+z=0$ and $x-y+2 z=4$. The distance of the point $(1,2,2)$ from this plane is ________ units.
explanation:
The equation of a plane passing through $(1,-2,1)$ is

$$a(x-1)+b(y+2)+c(z-1)=0\quad\text{..... (i)}$$

Plane (i) is perpendicular to planes

$$2 x-2 y+z=0 \text { and } x-y+2 z=4$$

$\therefore \quad 2 \mathrm{a}-2 \mathrm{~b}+\mathrm{c}=0$, and $\quad\text{..... (ii)}$

$$a-b+2 c=0\quad\text{..... (iii)}$$

Solving (ii) and (iii), we get

$$a=-3, b=-3, c=0$$

Substituting the values of $\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}, \mathrm{c}$ in equation (i), we get

$$x+y+1=0$$

$\therefore \quad$ The distance of this plane from $(1,2,2)$ is

$$d=\left|\frac{1+2+1}{\sqrt{1+1}}\right|=2 \sqrt{2}$$

content: If the sum of the squares of the distance of the point $\mathrm{P}(x, y, \mathrm{z})$ from the co-ordinate axes is 242 , then the distance of the point P from the origin is units.
explanation:
The distance of the point $P(x, y, z)$ from the $x$-axis is:

$$ \sqrt{y^2 + z^2} $$

From the $y$-axis:

$$ \sqrt{x^2 + z^2} $$

From the $z$-axis:

$$ \sqrt{x^2 + y^2} $$

The sum of the squares of these distances is:

$ (y^2 + z^2) + (x^2 + z^2) + (x^2 + y^2) $

Let's add the terms:

$ y^2 + z^2 + x^2 + z^2 + x^2 + y^2 = (x^2 + x^2) + (y^2 + y^2) + (z^2 + z^2) = 2x^2 + 2y^2 + 2z^2 $

We are told this sum is $242$:

$ 2x^2 + 2y^2 + 2z^2 = 242 $

Divide both sides by $2$:

$ x^2 + y^2 + z^2 = 121 $

The distance of $P$ from the origin is:

$ \sqrt{x^2 + y^2 + z^2} = \sqrt{121} = 11 $

Correct answer: Option B (11)

content: If the points $\mathrm{A}(2-x, 2,2), \mathrm{B}(2,2-y, 2)$, $\mathrm{C}(2,2,2-\mathrm{z})$ and $\mathrm{D}(1,1,1)$ are coplanar, then the locus of point $\mathrm{P}(x, y, \mathrm{z})$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & \overline{\mathrm{AB}}=x \hat{\mathrm{i}}-y \hat{\mathrm{j}} \\ & \overline{\mathrm{AC}}=x \hat{\mathrm{i}}-z \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ & \overline{\mathrm{AD}}=(x-1) \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}} \end{aligned}\\ &\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{~B}, \mathrm{C}, \mathrm{D} \text { are coplanar. } \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{ccc} \overline{\mathrm{AB}} & \overline{\mathrm{AC}} & \overline{\mathrm{AD}} \end{array}\right]=0 \\ & \Rightarrow\left|\begin{array}{ccc} x & -y & 0 \\ x & 0 & -z \\ x-1 & -1 & -1 \end{array}\right|=0 \\ & \Rightarrow x(-z)+y(-x+z(x-1))=0 \\ & \Rightarrow-x z-x y+x y z-z y=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{1}{x}+\frac{1}{y}+\frac{1}{z}=1 \end{aligned}$$

content: If the lines $\frac{3-x}{2}=\frac{5 y-2}{3 \lambda+1}=5-\mathrm{z}$ and $\frac{x+2}{-1}=\frac{1-3 y}{7}=\frac{4-z}{2 \mu}$ are at right angles, then $7 \lambda-10 \mu=$
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} &\frac{x-3}{-2}=\frac{y-\frac{2}{5}}{\frac{3 \lambda+1}{5}}=\frac{z-5}{-1} \text { and } \frac{x+2}{-1}=\frac{y-\frac{1}{3}}{-\frac{7}{3}}=\frac{z-4}{-2 \mu}\\ &\text { As these lines are at right angles, we get }\\ &\begin{aligned} & (-2)(-1)+\left(\frac{3 \lambda+1}{5}\right)\left(\frac{-7}{3}\right)+(-1)(-2 \mu)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 2-\left(\frac{21 \lambda+7}{15}\right)+2 \mu=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 30-21 \lambda-7+30 \mu 0 \\ & \Rightarrow 7 \lambda-10 \mu=\frac{23}{3} \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: If the angle $\theta$ between the line $\frac{x+1}{1}=\frac{y-1}{2}=\frac{z-2}{2}$ and the plane $2 x-y+\sqrt{\lambda} z+4=0$ is such that $\sin \theta=\frac{1}{3}$, then $\lambda+1=$
explanation:
The d.r.s of line and plane are $1,2,2$ and $2,-1, \sqrt{\lambda}$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \sin \theta=\frac{2(1)+(-1)(2)+(\sqrt{\lambda})(2)}{\sqrt{1^2+2^2+2^2} \cdot \sqrt{2^2+(-1)^2+(\sqrt{\lambda})^2}} \\ & \quad \Rightarrow \frac{1}{3}=\frac{2 \sqrt{\lambda}}{3 \cdot \sqrt{5+\lambda}} \\ & \quad \Rightarrow \sqrt{5+\lambda}=2 \sqrt{\lambda} \\ & \quad \Rightarrow \lambda=\frac{5}{3} \\ & \quad \Rightarrow \lambda+1=\frac{8}{3} \end{aligned}$$

content: If the directed line makes an angle $45^{\circ}$ and $60^{\circ}$ with the X and Y -axes respectively, then the obtuse angle $\theta$ made by the line with the Z -axis is
explanation:
Let the direction cosines of the line be $l, m, n$.

Given:

  • The line makes an angle $45^\circ$ with the X-axis $\Rightarrow l = \cos 45^\circ = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$

  • The line makes an angle $60^\circ$ with the Y-axis $\Rightarrow m = \cos 60^\circ = \dfrac{1}{2}$

Let $\theta$ be the angle with the Z-axis, so $n = \cos \theta$.

By the direction cosine formula,

$$ l^2 + m^2 + n^2 = 1 $$

Substitute the values:

$ \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^2 + n^2 = 1 $

Calculate:

$ \frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{4} + n^2 = 1 $

$ \frac{3}{4} + n^2 = 1 $

$ n^2 = 1 - \frac{3}{4} = \frac{1}{4} $

So,

$ n = \pm\frac{1}{2} $

But $n = \cos\theta$. There are two solutions:

  • $\cos\theta = \frac{1}{2} \implies \theta = 60^\circ$ (acute angle)

  • $\cos\theta = -\frac{1}{2} \implies \theta = 120^\circ$ (obtuse angle)

Required obtuse angle:

$ \boxed{120^\circ} $

Option B is correct.

content: The co-ordinates of the foot of the perpendicular from the point $(0,2,3)$ on the line $\frac{x+3}{5}=\frac{y+1}{2}=\frac{z+4}{3}$ is
explanation:
Let $\frac{x+3}{5}=\frac{y+1}{2}=\frac{z+4}{3}=\lambda$

Any point on the line is

$$P \equiv(5 \lambda-3,2 \lambda-1,3 \lambda-4)$$

Given point is $\mathrm{A}(0,2,3)$

$\therefore \quad$ The d.r.s. of AP are $5 \lambda-3,2 \lambda-3,3 \lambda-7$

Since the line AP is perpendicular to the given line.

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore \quad & 5(5 \lambda-3)+2(2 \lambda-3)+3(3 \lambda-7)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 38 \lambda-42=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \lambda=\frac{21}{19} \\ \therefore & P \equiv\left(\frac{48}{19}, \frac{23}{19}, \frac{-13}{19}\right) \end{array}$$

content: A line having direction ratios $1,-4,2$ intersects the lines $\frac{x-7}{3}=\frac{y-1}{-1}=\frac{z+2}{1}$ and $\frac{x}{2}=\frac{y-7}{3}=\frac{z}{1}$ at the points $A$ and $B$ resp., then co-ordinates of points A and B are
explanation:
Let $\frac{x-7}{3}=\frac{y-1}{-1}=\frac{z+2}{1}=\lambda$

$$\Rightarrow x=3 \lambda+7, y=-\lambda+1, z=\lambda-2$$

Let $\frac{x}{2}=\frac{y-7}{3}=\frac{z}{1}=\mu$

$$\Rightarrow x=2 \mu, y=3 \mu+7, z=\mu$$

Co-ordinates of a point on the first line are

$$\mathrm{A}(3 \lambda+7,1-\lambda, \lambda-2)$$

Co-ordinates of a point on the second line are

$$\mathrm{B}(2 \mu, 3 \mu+7, \mu)$$

D.r.s. of $A B$ are

$$3 \lambda-2 \mu+7,-\lambda-3 \mu-6, \lambda-\mu-2$$

D.r.s. of $A B$ are $1,-4,2$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad & \frac{3 \lambda-2 \mu+7}{1}=\frac{-\lambda-3 \mu-6}{-4}=\frac{\lambda-\mu-2}{2} \\ & 3 \lambda-2 \mu+7=\frac{\lambda+3 \mu+6}{4} \\ & \Rightarrow \lambda-\mu+2=0 \quad\text{.... (i)}\\ & \frac{\lambda+3 \mu+6}{4}=\frac{\lambda-\mu-2}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow \lambda-5 \mu-10=0 \quad\text{.... (ii)} \end{aligned}\\ &\text { Solving (i) and (ii), we get }\\ &\begin{aligned} & \lambda=-5, \mu=-3 \\ \therefore \quad & A \equiv(-8,6,-7), \\ & B \equiv(-6,-2,-3) \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: A plane makes positive intercepts of unit length on each of $X$ and $Y$ axis. If it passes through the point $(-1,1,2)$ and makes angle $\theta$ with the X -axis, then $\theta$ is
explanation:
Equation of plane in intercept form is

$$\frac{x}{a}+\frac{y}{b}+\frac{z}{c}=1$$

Here, $\mathrm{a}=1, \mathrm{~b}=1$

$$\therefore \quad \frac{x}{1}+\frac{y}{1}+\frac{z}{c}=1$$

Since this plane passes through the point $(-1,1,2)$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad & -1+1+\frac{2}{c}=1 \\ & \Rightarrow c=2 \end{aligned}$$

$\therefore \quad$ Equation of plane is

$$\begin{aligned} & x+y+\frac{z}{2}=1 \\ & \Rightarrow 2 x+2 y+z=2 \end{aligned}$$

D.r.s of X -axis are $1,0,0$.

$$\therefore \quad \sin \theta=\frac{2(1)+0+0}{\sqrt{4+4+1} \sqrt{1}}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \sin \theta=\frac{2}{3} \\ & \Rightarrow \theta=\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{2}{3}\right) \end{aligned}$$

content: The equation of plane through the point $(2,-1,-3)$ and parallel to lines $\frac{x-1}{3}=\frac{y+2}{2}=\frac{z}{-4}$ and $\frac{x}{2}=\frac{y-1}{-3}=\frac{z-2}{2}$ is
explanation:
The equation of plane passing through $(2,-1,-3)$ is

$$\mathrm{a}(x-2)+\mathrm{b}(y+1)+\mathrm{c}(\mathrm{z}+3)=0$$

Also, as the plane is parallel to the given two lines,

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad 3 a+2 b-4 c=0 \text { and } 2 a-3 b+2 c=0 \\ & \quad \Rightarrow a=-8, b=-14, c=-13 \end{aligned}$$

$\therefore \quad$ The equation of the required plane is

$$\begin{aligned} & -8(x-2)-14(y+1)-13(z+3)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 8 x+14 y+13 z+37=0 \end{aligned}$$

content: The equation of the plane, passing through the intersection of the planes $x+y+z=1$ and $2 x+3 y-z+4=0$ and parallel to $Y$-axis is
explanation:
Equation of plane passing through the intersection of given planes is

$$\begin{aligned} & (x+y+z-1)+\lambda(2 x+3 y-z+4)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow(1+2 \lambda) x+(1+3 \lambda) y+(1-\lambda) z+4 \lambda-1=0 \end{aligned}$$

Since the plane is parallel to Y -axis.

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad & 1+3 \lambda=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \lambda=\frac{-1}{3} \end{aligned}$$

$\therefore \quad$ Equation of the required plane is $x+4 z-7=0$

content: A line with positive direction cosines passes through the point $\mathrm{P}(2,-1,2)$ and makes equal angles with co-ordinate axes. The line meets the plane $2 x+y+z=9$ at point Q. Then the length of the line segment PQ equals
explanation:
Since direction cosines of PQ are equal and positive.

$\therefore \quad$ The d.r.s. of PQ are $\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}, \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}, \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$

$\therefore \quad$ The equation of the line PQ is

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{x-2}{\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}}=\frac{y+1}{\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}}=\frac{z-2}{\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}} \\ & \Rightarrow x-2=y+1=\mathrm{z}-2=\mathrm{k}, \text { say } \end{aligned}$$

$\therefore \quad$ Co-ordinates of the point Q are

$$(\mathrm{k}+2, \mathrm{k}-1, \mathrm{k}+2)$$

The point Q lies on the plane $2 x+y+z=9$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore \quad 2(k+2)+k-1+k+2=9 \\ \Rightarrow 4 k+5=9 \quad \Rightarrow k=1\\ \therefore \quad Q \equiv(3,0,3) \\ \begin{aligned} \therefore \quad P Q \end{aligned}=\sqrt{(3-2)^2+(0+1)^2+(3-2)^2} \\ \quad=\sqrt{1+1+1} \\ \quad=\sqrt{3} \text { units } \end{array}$$

content: If the distance between the plane Ax-2y+z $=\mathrm{d}$ and the plane containing the lines $\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y-2}{3}=\frac{z-3}{4}$ and $\frac{x-2}{3}=\frac{y-3}{4}=\frac{z-4}{5}$ is $\sqrt{6}$ units, then $|d|$ is
explanation:
Equation of the plane containing the given lines is

$$\begin{aligned} & \left|\begin{array}{ccc} x-1 & y-2 & z-3 \\ 2 & 3 & 4 \\ 3 & 4 & 5 \end{array}\right|=0 \\ & \Rightarrow(x-1)(15-16)-(y-2)(10-12) \\ & +(z-3)(8-9)=0 \end{aligned}$$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow(x-1)(-1)-(y-2)(-2)+(z-3)(-1)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow-x+1+2 y-4-z+3=0 \\ & \Rightarrow-x+2 y-z=0 \\ & \Rightarrow x-2 y+z=0\quad\text{.... (i)} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\text { Given equation of plane is }\\ &A x-2 y+z=d\quad\text{.... (ii)} \end{aligned}$$

The planes given by equations (i) and (ii) are parallel.

$\therefore \quad \mathrm{A}=1$

Distance between the planes (D) is

$$\begin{aligned} & \quad \quad \mathrm{D}=\left|\frac{d}{\sqrt{1^2+(-2)^2+1^2}}\right|=\left|\frac{d}{\sqrt{6}}\right| \\ & \therefore \quad\left|\frac{d}{\sqrt{6}}\right|=\sqrt{6} \\ & \quad \Rightarrow|d|=6 \end{aligned}$$

content: The length of the projection of the line segment joining the points $(5,-1,4)$ and $(4,-1,3)$ on the plane $x+y+z=7$ is
explanation:
