$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Let } \mathrm{A}=(5,-1,4), \mathrm{B}=(4,-1,3) \\ & \overline{\mathrm{AB}}=-\hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}} \Rightarrow|\overline{\mathrm{AB}}|=\sqrt{2} \end{aligned}$$

Projection of $\overline{\mathrm{AB}}$ in the plane $x+y+z=7$ is $|\overline{\mathrm{AB}}| \cos \theta=\left|\overline{\mathrm{A}^{\prime} \mathrm{B}^{\prime}}\right| \cos \theta$

Direction ratios of normal to the given plane is 1, 1, 1.

$$\begin{aligned} & \cos \left(90^{\circ}-\theta\right)=\left|\frac{1(-1)+1(0)+1(-1)}{\sqrt{1^2+1^2+1^2} \sqrt{1^2+0^2+1^2}}\right| \\ & \Rightarrow \sin \theta=\frac{2}{\sqrt{6}} \Rightarrow \cos \theta=\sqrt{1-\frac{4}{6}}=\sqrt{\frac{1}{3}} \\ & \text { Required projection }=|\overline{\mathrm{AB}}| \cos \theta \\ & =\sqrt{2} \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}=\sqrt{\frac{2}{3}} \text { units } \end{aligned}$$

content: A line makes $45^{\circ}$ angle with positive X -axis and makes equal angles with positive Y -axis ad Z-axis respectively, then the sum of the three angles which the line makes with positive X -axis, Y -axis and Z -axis is
explanation:
To find the sum of the angles a line makes with the coordinate axes, we need to consider each angle individually and their relationships.

Given that the line makes a $45^\circ$ angle with the positive X-axis, let's denote:

$\alpha = 45^\circ$ (angle with X-axis),

$\beta$ (angle with Y-axis),

$\gamma$ (angle with Z-axis).

The condition mentioned is that the line makes equal angles with the positive Y-axis and Z-axis, therefore $\beta = \gamma$.

The angles a line makes with the coordinate axes are related by the following identity derived from the direction cosines:

$$ \cos^2 \alpha + \cos^2 \beta + \cos^2 \gamma = 1 $$

Substituting $\alpha = 45^\circ$:

$$ \cos^2 45^\circ + \cos^2 \beta + \cos^2 \beta = 1 $$

$$ \left(\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}\right)^2 + 2 \cos^2 \beta = 1 $$

$$ \frac{1}{2} + 2 \cos^2 \beta = 1 $$

$$ 2 \cos^2 \beta = \frac{1}{2} $$

$$ \cos^2 \beta = \frac{1}{4} $$

$$ \cos \beta = \frac{1}{2} $$

Therefore, $\beta = \gamma = 60^\circ$ because the cosine of $60^\circ$ is $\frac{1}{2}$.

Now, sum these angles:

$$ \alpha + \beta + \gamma = 45^\circ + 60^\circ + 60^\circ = 165^\circ $$

Thus, the sum of the angles which the line makes with the positive X-axis, Y-axis, and Z-axis is $165^\circ$.

Option C: $165^\circ$

content: If the lines $\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y+2}{3}=\frac{z-1}{4}$ and $\frac{x-3}{1}=\frac{y-\mathrm{k}}{2}=\frac{\mathrm{z}}{1}$ intersect, then k has the value
explanation:
The given equation of lines are $\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y+2}{3}=\frac{z-1}{4}$ and $\frac{x-3}{1}=\frac{y-\mathrm{k}}{2}=\frac{z}{1}$

Since the lines intersect,

$$\begin{aligned} & \quad\left|\begin{array}{ccc} x_2-x_1 & y_2-y_1 & z_2-z_1 \\ \mathrm{a}_1 & \mathrm{~b}_1 & \mathrm{c}_1 \\ \mathrm{a}_2 & \mathrm{~b}_2 & \mathrm{c}_2 \end{array}\right|=0 \\ & \therefore \quad\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 2 & \mathrm{k}+2 & -1 \\ 2 & 3 & 4 \\ 1 & 2 & 1 \end{array}\right|=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 2(3-8)-(\mathrm{k}+2)(2-4)-1(4-3)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow-10+2 \mathrm{k}+4-1=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 2 \mathrm{k}-7=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{k}=\frac{7}{2} \end{aligned}$$

content: The vector equation of the plane through the line of intersection of the planes $x+y+z=1$ and $2 x+3 y+4 z=5$, which is perpendicular to the plane $x-y+z=0$, is
explanation:
The equation of the required plane

$$\begin{aligned} & (x+y+z-1)+\lambda(2 x+3 y+4 z-5)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow(1+2 \lambda) x+(1+3 \lambda) y+(1+4 \lambda) z-1-5 \lambda=0\quad\text{.... (i)} \end{aligned}$$

Let $\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}, \mathrm{c}$ be the d.r.s. of the required plane.

$\therefore \quad$ From (i), $\mathrm{a}=1+2 \lambda, \mathrm{~b}=1+3 \lambda, \mathrm{c}=1+4 \lambda$

The required plane is perpendicular to

$$\begin{aligned} & x-y+z=0 \\ \therefore\quad& \mathrm{a}-\mathrm{b}+\mathrm{c}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 1+2 \lambda-(1+3 \lambda)+1+4 \lambda=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 1+3 \lambda=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \lambda=-\frac{1}{3} \end{aligned}$$

Substituting $\lambda=-\frac{1}{3}$ in (i), we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \left(1-\frac{2}{3}\right) x+\left(1-\frac{3}{3}\right) y+\left(1-\frac{4}{3}\right) z-1+\frac{5}{3}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow x-z+2=0 \end{aligned}$$

Its vector equation is $$ \overline{\mathrm{r}} \cdot(\hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}})+2=0 $$

content: The equation of a line passing through the point $(2,-1,1)$ and parallel to the line joining the points $\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}$ and $-\hat{i}+4 \hat{j}+\hat{k}$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Let } \overline{\mathrm{b}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}, \overline{\mathrm{c}}=-\hat{\mathrm{i}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ & \Rightarrow \overline{\mathrm{c}}-\overline{\mathrm{b}}=-2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}} \end{aligned}$$

The equation of the line passing through $2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}$ and parallel to $-2 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-\hat{k}$ is $$ \bar{r}=(2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k})+\lambda(-2 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-\hat{k}) $$

content: The foot of the perpendicular drawn from origin to a plane is $\mathrm{M}(2,1,-2)$, then vector equation of the plane is
explanation:
The equation of a plane passing through a point $\text{M}(x_1, y_1, z_1)$ with normal vector $\mathbf{n} = \langle a, b, c \rangle$ is given by the vector equation:

$$ \overline{\mathbf{r}} \cdot \mathbf{n} = \mathbf{OM} \cdot \mathbf{n} $$

where $\overline{\mathbf{r}}$ is the position vector of any point $(x, y, z)$ on the plane, and $\mathbf{OM}$ is the position vector of the point $\text{M}(x_1, y_1, z_1)$.

Given that the foot of the perpendicular from the origin to the plane is $\text{M}(2, 1, -2)$, the vector $\mathbf{OM}$ from the origin to point $\text{M}$ is:

$$ \mathbf{OM} = 2 \hat{\mathbf{i}} + 1 \hat{\mathbf{j}} - 2 \hat{\mathbf{k}} $$

This vector $\mathbf{OM}$ is also normal to the plane because the foot of the perpendicular from the origin to the plane defines the normal vector. Therefore, the vector $\mathbf{n}$ is:

$$ \mathbf{n} = 2 \hat{\mathbf{i}} + 1 \hat{\mathbf{j}} - 2 \hat{\mathbf{k}} $$

Thus, the normal vector $\mathbf{n}$ is:

$$ \mathbf{n} = \langle 2, 1, -2 \rangle $$

Substitute the known $\mathbf{n}$ and $\mathbf{OM}$ into the plane equation:

$$ \overline{\mathbf{r}} \cdot \langle 2, 1, -2 \rangle = (2 \hat{\mathbf{i}} + 1 \hat{\mathbf{j}} - 2 \hat{\mathbf{k}}) \cdot (2 \hat{\mathbf{i}} + 1 \hat{\mathbf{j}} - 2 \hat{\mathbf{k}}) $$

Calculate the dot product on the right-hand side:

$$ (2 \times 2) + (1 \times 1) + (-2 \times -2) = 4 + 1 + 4 = 9 $$

Therefore, the vector equation of the plane is:

$$ \overline{\mathbf{r}} \cdot (2 \hat{\mathbf{i}} + \hat{\mathbf{j}} - 2 \hat{\mathbf{k}}) = 9 $$

The correct option is Option A:

$$ \overline{\mathbf{r}} \cdot (2 \hat{\mathbf{i}} + \hat{\mathbf{j}} - 2 \hat{\mathbf{k}}) = 9 $$

content: Let $\mathrm{L}_1: \frac{x+2}{5}=\frac{y-3}{2}=\frac{\mathrm{z}-6}{1}$ and $\mathrm{L}_2: \frac{x-3}{4}=\frac{y+2}{3}=\frac{z-3}{5}$ be the given lines. Then the unit vector perpendicular to both $\mathrm{L}_1$ and $\mathrm{L}_2$ is
explanation:
Lines $L_1$ and $L_2$ are parallel to the vectors $\bar{b}_1=5 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}$ and $\bar{b}_2=4 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k}$ respectively.

$\therefore \quad$ The unit vector perpendicular to both $\mathrm{L}_1$ and $L_2$ is

$$\hat{\mathbf{n}}=\frac{\bar{b}_1 \times \bar{b}_2}{\left|\bar{b}_1 \times \bar{b}_2\right|}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Now, } \overline{\mathrm{b}}_1 \times \overline{\mathrm{b}}_2=\left|\begin{array}{lll} \hat{\mathrm{i}} & \hat{\mathrm{j}} & \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ 5 & 2 & 1 \\ 4 & 3 & 5 \end{array}\right|=7 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-21 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+7 \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ & \begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \hat{\mathrm{n}} & =\frac{7 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-21 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+7 \hat{\mathrm{k}}}{\sqrt{539}} \\ & =\frac{7(\hat{\mathrm{i}}-3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}})}{7 \sqrt{11}}=\frac{\hat{\mathrm{i}}-3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}}{\sqrt{11}} \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: The perpendicular distance from the origin to the plane containing the two lines $\frac{x+2}{3}=\frac{y-2}{5}=\frac{z+5}{7}$ and $\frac{x-1}{1}=\frac{y-4}{4}=\frac{z+4}{7}$, is
explanation:
Equation of the plane is

$$\begin{aligned} & \left|\begin{array}{ccc} x-1 & y-4 & z+4 \\ 3 & 5 & 7 \\ 1 & 4 & 7 \end{array}\right| \\ & \Rightarrow(x-1)(7)-(y-4)(14)+(z+4)(7)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 7 x-14 y+7 z+77=0 \\ & \Rightarrow x-2 y+z+11=0 \end{aligned}$$

$\therefore \quad$ Perpendicular distance from origin to the plane is $\left|\frac{0-2(0)+0+11}{\sqrt{1+4+1}}\right|=\frac{11}{\sqrt{6}}$ units

content: Let $P(2,1,5)$ be a point in space and $Q$ be a point on the line $\bar{r}=(\hat{i}-\hat{j}+2 \hat{k})+\mu(-3 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+5 \hat{k})$. Then the value of $\mu$ for which the vector $\overline{\mathrm{PQ}}$ is parallel to the plane $3 x-y+4 z=1$ is
explanation:
Let the position vector of Q be

$$\begin{aligned} & (\hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}})+\mu(-3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+5 \hat{\mathrm{k}}) \\ & =(-3 \mu+1) \hat{\mathrm{i}}+(\mu-1) \hat{\mathrm{j}}+(5 \mu+2) \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ \therefore \quad & \overline{\mathrm{PQ}}=(-3 \mu-1) \hat{\mathrm{i}}+(\mu-2) \hat{\mathrm{j}}+(5 \mu-3) \hat{\mathrm{k}} \end{aligned}$$

Since $\overline{\mathrm{PQ}}$ is parallel to the given plane,

$$\begin{aligned} & (-3 \mu-1)(3)+(\mu-2)(-1)+(5 \mu-3)(4)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 10 \mu-13=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \mu=\frac{13}{10} \end{aligned}$$

content: The centroid of tetrahedron with vertices $\mathrm{P}(5,-7,0), \mathrm{Q}(\mathrm{a}, 5,3), \mathrm{R}(4,-6, b)$ and $\mathrm{S}(6, \mathrm{c}, 2)$ is $(4,-3,2)$, then the value of $2 a+3 b+c$ is equal to
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} &\text { Centroid of tetrahedron }\\ &\begin{aligned} & \equiv\left(\frac{5+a+4+6}{4}, \frac{-7+5-6+c}{4}, \frac{0+3+b+2}{4}\right) \\ \therefore & (4,-3,2) \equiv\left(\frac{15+a}{4}, \frac{-8+c}{4}, \frac{b+5}{4}\right) \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \frac{15+a}{4}=4 \Rightarrow a=1 \\ & \frac{-8+c}{4}=-3 \Rightarrow c=-4 \\ & \frac{b+5}{4}=2 \Rightarrow b=3 \\ \therefore\quad& 2 a+3 b+c=2(1)+3(3)-4=7 \end{aligned}$$

content: A variable plane passes through the fixed point $(3,2,1)$ and meets $X, Y$ and $Z$ axes at points $A$, B and C respectively. A plane is drawn parallel to YZ - plane through A , a second plane is drawn parallel to ZX -plan through B , a third plane is drawn parallel to XY - plane through C . Then locus of the point of intersection of these three planes, is
explanation:
Let the plane be $\frac{x}{a}+\frac{y}{b}+\frac{z}{c}=1$

If passes through $(3,2,1)$

$\therefore \quad \frac{3}{\mathrm{a}}+\frac{2}{\mathrm{~b}}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{c}}=1\quad\text{.... (i)}$

Now, coordinates of points A, B, C are (a, 0, 0), $(0, b, 0)$ and $(0,0, c)$ respectively.

$\therefore \quad$ Equations of the planes passing through $\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}, \mathrm{C}$ are $x=\mathrm{a}, y=\mathrm{b}$ and $\mathrm{z}=\mathrm{c}$ respectively.

$\therefore \quad$ From equation (i), we get

Required locus is $\frac{3}{x}+\frac{2}{y}+\frac{1}{z}=1$

content: The distance of the point $(1,-5,9)$ from the plane $x-y+z=5$ measured along the line $x=y=\mathrm{z}$ is __________ units.
explanation:
Equation of the line can be written as $\frac{x-1}{1}=\frac{y+5}{1}=\frac{z-9}{1}=\lambda$

$\therefore \quad$ Co-ordinates of any point on the line are given as $x=\lambda+1, y=\lambda-5, z=\lambda+9$

Substituting in the equation of the plane, we get

$$\begin{array}{ll} & (\lambda+1)-(\lambda-5)+(\lambda+9)=5 \\ \therefore & \lambda+15=5 \\ \therefore & \lambda=-10 \\ \therefore \quad & \text { Point on the plane is }(-9,-15,-1) \\ \therefore \quad & \text { Required distance } \\ & =\text { Distance between }(1,-5,9) \text { and }(-9,-15,-1) \\ & =\sqrt{(-9-1)^2+(-15+5)^2+(-1-9)^2} \\ & =10 \sqrt{3} \end{array}$$

content: If for some $\alpha \in \mathbb{R}$, the lines $\mathrm{L}_1: \frac{x+1}{2}=\frac{y-2}{-1}=\frac{z-1}{1}$ and $\mathrm{L}_2: \frac{x+2}{\alpha}=\frac{y+1}{5-\alpha}=\frac{z+1}{1}$ are coplanar, then the line $L_2$ passes through the point
explanation:
Here $x_1, y_1, z_1=-1,2,1$ and $x_2, y_2, z_2=-2,-1,-1$

$a_1, b_1, c_1=2,-1,1$ and $a_2, b_2, c_2=\alpha, 5-\alpha, 1$

Since the given lines are coplanar, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \left|\begin{array}{ccc} x_2-x_1 & y_2-y_1 & z_2-z_1 \\ a_1 & b_1 & c_1 \\ a_2 & b_2 & c_2 \end{array}\right|=0 \\ & \Rightarrow\left|\begin{array}{ccc} -2+1 & -1-2 & -1-1 \\ 2 & -1 & 1 \\ \alpha & 5-\alpha & 1 \end{array}\right|=0 \\ & \Rightarrow(-1)(-1-5+\alpha)-(-3)(2-\alpha) \\ & +(-2)(10-2 \alpha+\alpha)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 6-\alpha+6-3 \alpha-20+2 \alpha=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \alpha=-4 \\ & \Rightarrow L_2: \frac{x+2}{-4}=\frac{y+1}{9}=\frac{z+1}{1} \end{aligned}$$

$\therefore \quad$ Only option (B) satisfies the equation of line $\mathrm{L}_2$.

content: Let $P(3,2,6)$ be a point in space and $Q$ be a point on the line $\bar{r}=\hat{i}-\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}+\mu(-3 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+5 \hat{k})$. Then the value of $\mu$ for which the vector $\overline{\mathrm{PQ}}$ is parallel to the plane $x-4 y+3 z=1$ is
explanation:
Let the position vector of $Q$ be

$$\begin{aligned} & (\hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}})+\mu(-3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+5 \hat{\mathrm{k}}) \\ & =(-3 \mu+1) \hat{\mathrm{i}}+(\mu-1) \hat{\mathrm{j}}+(5 \mu+2) \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ \therefore \quad & \overline{P Q}=(-3 \mu-2) \hat{\mathrm{i}}+(\mu-3) \hat{\mathrm{j}}+(5 \mu-4) \hat{\mathrm{k}} \end{aligned}$$

Since $\overline{\mathrm{PQ}}$ is parallel to the plane,

$$\begin{aligned} & (-3 \mu-2)(1)+(\mu-3)(-4)+(5 \mu-4)(3)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \mu=\frac{1}{4} \end{aligned}$$

content: The perpendicular distance of the origin from the plane $2 x+y-2 z-18=0$ is
explanation:
The formula to calculate the perpendicular distance of a point $(x_0, y_0, z_0)$ from a plane $Ax + By + Cz + D = 0$ is given by:

$$ \text{Distance} = \frac{|Ax_0 + By_0 + Cz_0 + D|}{\sqrt{A^2 + B^2 + C^2}} $$

For the plane $2x + y - 2z - 18 = 0$, the coefficients are $A = 2$, $B = 1$, $C = -2$, and $D = -18$. The point for which we are calculating the distance is the origin $(0, 0, 0)$.

Substituting these values into the formula:

$$ \text{Distance} = \frac{|2(0) + 1(0) - 2(0) - 18|}{\sqrt{2^2 + 1^2 + (-2)^2}} $$

Simplify the numerator:

$$ = \frac{|-18|}{\sqrt{4 + 1 + 4}} $$

Further simplification gives:

$$ = \frac{18}{\sqrt{9}} $$

Since $\sqrt{9} = 3$, the expression simplifies to:

$$ = \frac{18}{3} = 6 $$

Therefore, the perpendicular distance of the origin from the plane is 6 units.

Option C: 6 units

content: The plane $2 x+3 y+4 z=1$ meets $X$-axis in $A$, Y -axis in B and Z -axis in C . Then the centroid of $\triangle A B C$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \text { Note that } \\ & \begin{aligned} & \mathrm{A} \equiv\left(\frac{1}{2}, 0,0\right), \mathrm{B} \equiv\left(0, \frac{1}{3}, 0\right), \mathrm{C} \equiv\left(0,0, \frac{1}{4}\right) \\ & \therefore \quad \text { Cendroid }=\left(\frac{\frac{1}{2}+0+0}{3}, \frac{0+\frac{1}{3}+0}{3}, \frac{0+0+\frac{1}{4}}{3}\right) \\ &=\left(\frac{1}{6}, \frac{1}{9}, \frac{1}{12}\right) \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: If the lines $\frac{x+1}{-10}=\frac{y+k}{-1}=\frac{z-4}{1} \quad$ and $\frac{x+10}{-1}=\frac{y+1}{-3}=\frac{z-1}{4}$ intersect each other, then the value of $k$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} &\text { Since the given lines intersect, }\\ &\begin{aligned} & \quad\left|\begin{array}{ccc} x_2-x_1 & y_2-y_1 & z_2-z_1 \\ a_1 & b_1 & c_1 \\ a_2 & b_2 & c_2 \end{array}\right|=0 \\ & \therefore\left|\begin{array}{ccc} -10+1 & -1+k & 1-4 \\ -10 & -1 & 1 \\ -1 & -3 & 4 \end{array}\right|=0 \\ & \Rightarrow\left|\begin{array}{ccc} -9 & -1+k & -3 \\ -10 & -1 & 1 \\ -1 & -3 & 4 \end{array}\right|=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 39 \mathrm{k}=117 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{k}=3 \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: The equation of the line passing through the point $(3,1,2)$ and perpendicular to the lines $\frac{x-1}{1}=\frac{y-2}{2}=\frac{z-3}{3}$ and $\frac{x}{-3}=\frac{y}{2}=\frac{z}{5}$ is
explanation:
Required line is perpendicular to the lines $\frac{x-1}{1}=\frac{y-2}{2}=\frac{z-3}{3}$ and $\frac{x}{-3}=\frac{y}{2}=\frac{z}{5}$

$\therefore \quad$ Required line is parallel to vector $\overline{\mathrm{b}}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{\mathrm{i}} & \hat{\mathrm{j}} & \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ 1 & 2 & 3 \\ -3 & 2 & 5\end{array}\right|$

$$\begin{aligned} &=4 \hat{i}-14 \hat{j}+8 \hat{k}\\ &\therefore \quad \text { The equation of the required line is }\\ &\frac{x-3}{2}=\frac{y-1}{-7}=\frac{z-2}{4} \end{aligned}$$

content: The area of the triangle with vertices $(1,2,0)$, $(1,0,2)$ and $(0,3,1)$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \text { Let } A \equiv(1,2,0), B \equiv(1,0,2) \text { and } C \equiv(0,3,1) \\ & \therefore \quad \overline{\mathrm{AB}}=-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}} \text { and } \overline{\mathrm{AC}}=-\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ & \therefore \quad \text { Area of } \triangle \mathrm{ABC}=\frac{1}{2}|\overline{\mathrm{AB}} \times \overline{\mathrm{AC}}| \\ & |\overline{\mathrm{AB}} \times \overline{\mathrm{AC}}|=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{\mathrm{i}} & \hat{\mathrm{j}} & \hat{\mathbf{k}} \\ 0 & -2 & 2 \\ -1 & 1 & 1 \end{array}\right| \\ & =\hat{\mathrm{i}}(-2-2)-\hat{\mathrm{j}}(0+2)+\hat{\mathrm{k}}(0-2) \\ & =-4 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k} \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{1}{2}|\overline{\mathrm{AB}} \times \overline{\mathrm{AC}}|=\sqrt{6} \text { sq. units } \end{aligned} $$

content: If the volume of tetrahedron whose vertices are $A \equiv(1,-6,10), B \equiv(-1,-3,7), C \equiv(5,-1, k)$ and $D \equiv(7,-4,7)$ is 11 cu . units, then the value of $k$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Let } \bar{a}=\hat{i}-6 \hat{j}+10 \hat{k}, \\ & \bar{b}=-\hat{i}-3 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}, \\ & \bar{c}=5 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+k \hat{k}, \\ & \bar{d}=7 \hat{i}-4 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k} \\ & \therefore \quad \overline{A B}=-2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k} \\ & \overline{A C}=4 \hat{i}+5 \hat{j}+(k-10) \hat{k} \\ & \overline{A D}=6 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k} \end{aligned}$$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \text { Volume of tetrahedron }=\frac{1}{6}\left[\begin{array}{lll} \overline{\mathrm{AB}} & \overline{\mathrm{AC}} & \overline{\mathrm{AD}} \end{array}\right] \\ & \quad \Rightarrow 11=\frac{1}{6}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} -2 & 3 & -3 \\ 4 & 5 & \mathrm{k}-10 \\ 6 & 2 & -3 \end{array}\right| \\ & \quad \Rightarrow 11=\frac{1}{6}\{-2(-15-2 \mathrm{k}+20)-3(-12-6 \mathrm{k}+60) -3(8-30)\}\\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{k}=7 \end{aligned}$$

content: The vector equation of the plane passing through the point $\mathrm{A}(1,2,-1)$ and parallel to the vectors $2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-\hat{k}$ and $\hat{i}-\hat{j}+3 \hat{k}$ is
explanation:
Let $\left(x_1, y_1, z_1\right)=(1,2,-1)$, $\mathrm{a}_1, \mathrm{~b}_1, \mathrm{c}_1=2,1,-1$ and $\mathrm{a}_2, \mathrm{~b}_2, \mathrm{c}_2=1,-1,3$

$\therefore \quad$ the equation of required plane is

$$\begin{aligned} & \left|\begin{array}{ccc} x-x_1 & y-y_1 & z-z_1 \\ a_1 & b_1 & c_1 \\ a_2 & b_2 & c_2 \end{array}\right|=0 \\ & \Rightarrow\left|\begin{array}{ccc} x-1 & y-2 & z+1 \\ 2 & 1 & -1 \\ 1 & -1 & 3 \end{array}\right|=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 2 x-2-7 y+14-3 z-3=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 2 x-7 y-3 z+9=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \overline{\mathrm{r}} \cdot(2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-7 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-3 \hat{\mathrm{k}})=-9 \end{aligned}$$

content: The shortest distance between lines $\bar{r}=(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-\hat{k})+\lambda(2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-3 \hat{k})$ and $\bar{r}=(2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+2 \hat{k})+\mu(\hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k})$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \overline{\mathrm{r}}=(\hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}})+\lambda(2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}-3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}) \text { and } \\ & \overline{\mathrm{r}}=(2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}})+\mu(\hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}) \\ & \text { Let } \mathrm{a}_1=\hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}, a_2=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ & \mathrm{~b}_1=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}-3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}, \mathrm{~b}_2=\hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ & \overline{\mathrm{a}}_2-\overline{\mathrm{a}}_1=\hat{\mathrm{i}}-3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ & \overline{\mathrm{~b}}_1 \times \overline{\mathrm{b}}_2=-2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-5 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-3 \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ & \left|\overline{\mathrm{~b}}_1 \times \overline{\mathrm{b}}_2\right|=\sqrt{38} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \text { Shortest distance } & =\left|\frac{\left(\overline{\mathrm{b}}_1 \times \overline{\mathrm{b}}_2\right) \cdot\left(\overline{\mathrm{a}}_2-\overline{\mathrm{a}}_1\right)}{\left|\overline{\mathrm{b}}_1 \times \overline{\mathrm{b}}_2\right|}\right| \\ & =\left|\frac{4}{\sqrt{38}}\right|=\left|\frac{2 \sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{19}}\right| \text { units } \end{aligned}$$

content: If the line $\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y+1}{3}=\frac{z-1}{4}$ and $\frac{x-3}{1}=\frac{y-\mathrm{k}}{2}=\frac{\mathrm{z}}{1}$ intersect, then the value of k is
explanation:
As the given lines are intersecting, the shortest distance between them is zero.

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad\left|\begin{array}{ccc} x_2-x_1 & y_2-y_1 & z_2-z_1 \\ a_1 & b_1 & c_1 \\ a_2 & b_2 & c_2 \end{array}\right|=0 \\ & \therefore \quad\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 3-1 & k+1 & 0-1 \\ 2 & 3 & 4 \\ 1 & 2 & 1 \end{array}\right|=0 \\ & \therefore \quad\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 2 & k+1 & -1 \\ 2 & 3 & 4 \\ 1 & 2 & 1 \end{array}\right|=0 \\ & \therefore \quad \mathrm{k}=\frac{9}{2} \end{aligned}$$

content: The projection of $\overline{\mathrm{AB}}$ on $\overline{\mathrm{CD}}$, where $A \equiv(2,-3,0), B \equiv(1,-4,-2), C \equiv(4,6,8)$ and $\mathrm{D} \equiv(7,0,10)$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & \overline{\mathrm{AB}}=-\hat{i}-\hat{j}-2 \hat{k} \\ & \overline{C D}=3 \hat{i}-6 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k} \end{aligned}\\ &\text { Scalar projection of } \overline{\mathrm{AB}} \text { on } \overline{\mathrm{CD}}\\ &=\frac{\overline{\mathrm{AB}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{CD}}}{\overline{\mathrm{CD}}}=\frac{-3+6-4}{\sqrt{9+36+4}}=\frac{1}{7} \end{aligned}$$

content: The equation of the plane through the point $(2,-1,-3)$ and parallel to the lines $\frac{x-1}{3}=\frac{y+2}{2}=\frac{z}{-4}$ and $\frac{x}{2}=\frac{y-1}{-3}=\frac{z-2}{2}$ is
explanation:
Equation of the plane passing through $\overline{\mathrm{a}}$ and parallel to $\overline{\mathrm{b}}$ and $\overline{\mathrm{c}}$ is

$$\begin{array}{rlrl} & \overline{\mathrm{r}} \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{~b}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}}) =\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{~b}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}}) \\ & \therefore \quad\overline{\mathrm{b}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{\mathrm{i}} & \hat{\mathrm{j}} \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ 3 & 2 & -4 \\ 2 & -3 & 2 \end{array}\right|=-8 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-14 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-13 \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ \therefore & \overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{~b}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}}) =(2)(-8)+(-1)(-14)+(-3)(-13) \\ & =-16+14+39=37 \end{array}$$

$\therefore \quad$ Required equation is

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { r} \cdot(-8 \hat{\mathbf{i}}-14 \hat{\mathbf{j}}-13 \hat{\mathrm{k}})=37 \\ & \text { i.e., } 8 x+14 y+13 z+37=0 \end{aligned}$$

content: Equation of the plane, through the points $(-1,2,-2)$ and $(-1,3,2)$ and perpendicular to $y \mathrm{z}$ - plane, is
explanation:
Equation of plane passing though $(-1,2,-2)$ and $(-1,3,2)$ is

$$\frac{x+1}{(-1+1)}=\frac{y-3}{2-3}=\frac{z-2}{-2-2}$$

Above plane is perpendicular to $y z$ - plane

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore\quad& \frac{y-3}{-1}=\frac{z-2}{-4} \\ & \Rightarrow 4(y-3)=z-2 \\ & \Rightarrow 4 y-12-z+2=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 4 y-z=10 \end{aligned}$$

content: If the lines $\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y-2}{3}=\frac{z-1}{4}$ and $\frac{x-3}{-1}=\frac{y-\mathrm{k}}{2}=\frac{\mathrm{z}}{1}$ intersect, then k is equal to
explanation:
As the given lines are intersecting, the shortest distance between them is zero

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore\left|\begin{array}{ccc} x_2-x_1, & y_2-y_1, & z_2-z_1 \\ a_1 & b_1 & c_1 \\ a_2 & b_2 & c_2 \end{array}\right|=0 \\ & \therefore\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 3-1 & k-2 & 0-1 \\ 2 & 3 & 4 \\ -1 & 2 & 1 \end{array}\right|=0 \\ & \therefore \quad\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 2 & k-2 & -1 \\ 2 & 3 & 4 \\ -1 & 2 & 1 \end{array}\right|=0 \\ & \therefore \quad k=\frac{-5}{6} \end{aligned}$$

content: If the line, $\frac{x-3}{2}=\frac{y+2}{1}=\frac{z+4}{3}$ lies in the plane, $\ell x+m y-z=9$, then $\ell^2+m^2$ is equal to
explanation:
Line is perpendicular to normal to plane

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow(2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}) \cdot(l \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\mathrm{m} \hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}})=0 \\ & 2 l+\mathrm{m}-3=0\quad\text{.... (i)} \end{aligned}$$

$(3,-2,-4)$ lies on the plane

$$\begin{aligned} & l x+\mathrm{m} y-\mathrm{z}=9 \\ \therefore\quad & 3 l-2 \mathrm{~m}+4=9 \\ & \Rightarrow 3 l-2 \mathrm{~m}=5\quad\text{.... (ii)} \end{aligned}$$

Solving (i) and (ii), we get

$$\begin{aligned} & l=\frac{11}{7}, \mathrm{~m}=\frac{-1}{7} \\ & l^2+\mathrm{m}^2\left(\frac{11}{7}\right)^2+\left(\frac{-1}{7}\right)^2=\frac{122}{49} \end{aligned}$$

content: If the line $\frac{x-2}{3}=\frac{y-1}{-5}=\frac{z+2}{2}$ lies in the plane $x+3 y-\alpha z+\beta=0$, then $(\alpha, \beta)=$
explanation:
Given equation of line

$$\frac{x-2}{3}=\frac{y-1}{-5}=\frac{z+2}{2}$$

$\therefore \quad$ The line passes through $(2,1,-2)$

The above point lies on the plane

$$\begin{aligned} & x+3 y-\alpha z+\beta=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 2+3+2 \alpha+\beta=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 2 \alpha+\beta=-5 \end{aligned}$$

Also the given line is perpendicular to the normal to the plane

$$\begin{aligned} & a_1 a_2+b_1 b_2+c_1 c_2=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 3(1)+((-5)(3)-2(\alpha)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \alpha=-6 \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { From (i) } \\ & \beta=7 \\ & \therefore \alpha \beta=-42 \\ &(\alpha, \beta)=(-6,7) \end{aligned}$$

content: A plane which is perpendicular to two planes $2 x-2 y+z=0$ and $x-y+2 z=4$, passes through $(1,2,1)$. The distance of the plane from the point $(2,3,4)$ is
explanation:
The equation of a plane passing through $(1,2,1)$ is $\mathrm{a}(x-1)+\mathrm{b}(y-2)+\mathrm{c}(\mathrm{z}-1)=0\quad\text{.... (i)}$

Plane (i) is perpendicular to planes

$$2 x-2 y+z=0 \text { and } x-y+2 z=4$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad 2 a-2 b+c=0 \quad\text{.... (ii)}\\ & a-b+2 c=0\quad\text{.... (iii)} \end{aligned}$$

Solving (ii) and (iii), we get

$$\mathrm{a}=-3, \mathrm{~b}=-3, \mathrm{c}=0$$

Substituting the values of $a, b, c$ in equation (i), we get

$$x+y-3=0$$

$\therefore \quad$ The distance of this plane from $(2,3,4)$ is $l=\left|\frac{2+3-3}{\sqrt{1+1}}\right|=\sqrt{2}$ units

content: The value of m such that $\frac{x-4}{1}=\frac{y-2}{1}=\frac{z+m}{2}$ lies in the plane $2 x-4 y+z=7$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} &\begin{array}{ll} & 2 x-4 y+z=7 \\ \therefore \quad & 2(4)-4(2)-m=7 \\ & \Rightarrow m=-7 \end{array}\\ &\text { Point }(4,2,-\mathrm{m}) \text { should lie in the plane } \end{aligned}$$

content: A line with positive direction cosines passes through the point $\mathrm{P}(2,1,2)$ and makes equal angles with the coordinate axes. The line meets the plane $2 x+y+\mathrm{z}=9$ at point Q . The length of the line segment PQ equals $\qquad$ units.
explanation:
Since direction cosines of PQ are equal and positive.

$\therefore \quad$ The d.r.s. of PQ are $\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}, \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}, \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$

$\therefore \quad$ The equation of the line PQ is

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{x-2}{\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}}=\frac{y-1}{\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}}=\frac{z-2}{\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}} \\ & \Rightarrow x-2=y-1=\mathrm{z}-2=\mathrm{k}, \text { say } \end{aligned}$$

$\therefore \quad$ Co-ordinates of the point Q are

$$(k+2, k+1, k+2)$$

The point Q lies on the plane $2 x+y+z=9$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & 2(\mathrm{k}+2)+\mathrm{k}+1+\mathrm{k}+2=9 \\ & \Rightarrow 4 \mathrm{k}+7=9 \\ \therefore & \mathrm{Q}=\left(\frac{5}{2}, \frac{3}{2}, \frac{5}{2}\right) \end{array} \Rightarrow \mathrm{k}=\frac{1}{2}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad P Q & =\sqrt{\left(\frac{5}{2}-2\right)^2+\left(\frac{3}{2}-1\right)^2+\left(\frac{5}{2}-2\right)^2} \\ & =\sqrt{\frac{1}{4}+\frac{1}{4}+\frac{1}{4}}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \text { units } \end{aligned}$$

content: Let L be the line of intersection of the planes $2 x+3 y+z=1$ and $x+3 y+2 z=2$. If L makes an angle $\alpha$ with the positive X -axis, then $\cos \alpha$ equals
explanation:
Vectors normal to the given planes are $\bar{n}_1=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}$ and $\overline{\mathrm{n}}_2=\hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}$.

$\therefore \quad$ The line $L$ is parallel to

$$\begin{aligned} & \overline{\mathrm{n}}=\overline{\mathrm{n}}_1 \times \overline{\mathrm{n}}_2=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{\mathrm{i}} & \hat{\mathrm{j}} & \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ 2 & 3 & 1 \\ 1 & 3 & 2 \end{array}\right|=3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ & \Rightarrow \cos \alpha=\frac{\overline{\mathrm{n}} \cdot \hat{\mathrm{i}}}{|\overline{\mathrm{n}}||\hat{\mathrm{i}}|}=\frac{3}{3 \sqrt{3}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \end{aligned}$$

content: The equation of the plane, passing through the point $(1,1,1)$ and perpendicular to the planes $2 x+y-2 z=5$ and $3 x-6 y-2 z=7$, is
explanation:
The equation of plane passing through $(1,1,1)$ is

$$a(x-1)+b(y-1)+c(z-1)=0\quad\text{... (i)}$$

Since plane (i) is perpendicular to the planes

$$2 x+y-2 z=5 \text { and } 3 x-6 y-2 z=7$$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore \quad & 2 a+b-2 c=5 \quad\text{.... (ii)}\\ & 3 a-6 b-2 c=7 \quad\text{.... (iii)} \end{array}$$

On solving (i), (ii) and (iii), we get

$$a=14, b=2, c=15$$

Substituting the values of $a, b, c$ in (i), we get

$$\begin{aligned} & 14(x-1)+2(y-1)+15(z-1)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 14 x+2 y+15 z=31 \end{aligned}$$

content: The distance of the point $(1,3,-7)$ from the plane passing through the point $(1,-1,-1)$ having normal perpendicular to both the lines $\frac{x-1}{1}=\frac{y+2}{-2}=\frac{z-4}{3}$ and $\frac{x-2}{2}=\frac{y+1}{-1}=\frac{z+7}{-1}$ is
explanation:
Normal vector $\hat{\mathbf{n}}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{\mathbf{i}} & \hat{\mathbf{j}} & \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ 1 & -2 & 3 \\ 2 & -1 & -1\end{array}\right|$

$$=\hat{i}(2+3)-\hat{j}(-1-6)+\hat{k}(-1+4)$$

$$=5 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+7 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}$$

Let $\mathrm{A} \equiv(1,-1,-1)$

$\therefore \quad \overline{\mathrm{a}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}$

$\therefore \quad$ Equation of the plane is

$$\begin{aligned} & 5(x-1)+7(y+1)+3(z+1)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 5 x+7 y+3 z+5=0 \end{aligned}$$

Distance of $(1,3,-7)$ from the above plane is $d=\left|\frac{5(1)+7(3)+3(-7)+5}{\sqrt{25+49+9}}\right|=\frac{10}{\sqrt{83}}$ units

content: The value of m , such that $\frac{x-4}{1}=\frac{y-2}{1}=\frac{z-m}{2}$ lies in the plane $2 x-4 y+z=7$, is
explanation:
The line $\frac{x-4}{1}=\frac{y-2}{1}=\frac{z-\mathrm{k}}{2}$ lies in the plane $2 x-4 y+z=7$.

$\therefore \quad$ the point $(4,2, k)$ lies on the line and hence lies in the plane

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore \quad & 2(4)-4(2)+k=7 \\ & \Rightarrow k=7 \end{array}$$

content: The length of the perpendicular from the point $\mathrm{A}(1,-2,-3)$ on the line $\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y+3}{-1}=\frac{z+1}{-2}$ is
explanation:
Let $\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y+3}{-1}=\frac{z+1}{-2}=\lambda$

$\therefore \quad$ Any general point on this line is

$$\mathrm{Q}(2 \lambda+1,-\lambda-3,-2 \lambda-1)$$

The direction ratios of $A Q$ are

$$2 \lambda,-\lambda-1,-2 \lambda+2$$

Since $A Q$ is perpendicular to given lines.

$$\begin{aligned} & 2(2 \lambda)-1(-\lambda-1)-2(-2 \lambda+2)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 4 \lambda+\lambda+1+4 \lambda-4=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 9 \lambda-3=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \lambda=\frac{1}{3} \\ \therefore \quad & Q \equiv\left(\frac{5}{3}, \frac{-10}{3}, \frac{-5}{3}\right) \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} A Q & =\sqrt{\left(1-\frac{5}{3}\right)^2-\left(-2+\frac{10}{3}\right)^2+\left(-3+\frac{5}{3}\right)^2} \\ & =\sqrt{\frac{4}{9}+\frac{16}{9}+\frac{16}{9}}=\sqrt{\frac{36}{9}}=\sqrt{4} \\ A Q & =2 \text { units } \end{aligned}$$

content: If the points $(1,-1, \lambda)$ and $(-3,0,1)$ are equidistant from the plane $3 x-4 y-12 z+13=0$, then the sum of all possible values of $\lambda$ is
explanation:
Since the points $(1,-1, \lambda)$ and $(-3,0,1)$ are equidistant from the given plane

$$\begin{aligned} & \left|\frac{3+4-12 \lambda+13}{\sqrt{9+16+144}}\right|=\left|\frac{-9-12+13}{\sqrt{9+16+144}}\right| \\ & \Rightarrow|3+4-12 \lambda+13|=|-9-12+13| \\ & \Rightarrow 20-12 \lambda= \pm 8 \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\Rightarrow \lambda=1, \frac{7}{3}\\ &\therefore \quad \text { Sum of all possible values of } \lambda=1+\frac{7}{3}=\frac{10}{3} \end{aligned}$$

content: Let P be a plane passing through the points $(2,1,0),(4,1,1)$ and $(5,0,1)$ and $R$ be the point $(2,1,6)$. Then image of $R$ in the plane $P$ is
explanation:
Equation of the plane passing through $(2,1,0)$, $(4,1,1)$ and $(5,0,1)$ is

$$\begin{aligned} & \left|\begin{array}{lll} x-2 & y-1 & z-0 \\ 4-2 & 1-1 & 1-0 \\ 5-2 & 0-1 & 1-0 \end{array}\right|=0 \\ & \Rightarrow x+y-2 z=3 \end{aligned}$$

$\mathrm{R}^{\prime}(x, y, \mathrm{z})$ is image of $\mathrm{R}(2,1,6)$ w.r.t. to plane

$$\begin{aligned} & x+y-2 \mathrm{z}=3 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{x-2}{1}=\frac{y-1}{1}=\frac{\mathrm{z}-6}{-2}=\frac{-2[2+1-2(6)-3]}{1+1+4} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{x-2}{1}=\frac{y-1}{1}=\frac{\mathrm{z}-6}{-2}=4 \\ & \Rightarrow x=6, y=5, \mathrm{z}=-2 \\ \therefore & \mathrm{R}^{\prime}(x, y, \mathrm{z}) \equiv(6,5,-2) \end{aligned}$$

content: The equation of the plane, passing through the point $(-1,2,-3)$ and parallel to the lines $\frac{x-1}{3}=\frac{y-2}{2}=\frac{z}{-4}$ and $\frac{x}{2}=\frac{y-1}{-3}=\frac{z-2}{2}$, is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & \text { Let }\left(x_1, y_1, z_1\right)=(-1,2,-3) \\ & \mathrm{a}_1, \mathrm{~b}_1, \mathrm{c}_1=3,2,-4 \text { and } \\ & \mathrm{a}_2, \mathrm{~b}_2, \mathrm{c}_2=2,-3,2 \end{aligned}\\ &\therefore \quad \text { The equation of required plane is }\\ &\begin{aligned} & \left|\begin{array}{ccc} x-x_1 & y-y_1 & z-z_1 \\ a_1 & b_1 & c_1 \\ \mathrm{a}_2 & \mathrm{~b}_2 & c_2 \end{array}\right|=0 \\ & \Rightarrow\left|\begin{array}{ccc} x+1 & y-2 & z+3 \\ 3 & 2 & -4 \\ 2 & -3 & 2 \end{array}\right|=0 \\ & \Rightarrow(x+1)(-8)-(y-2)(14)+(z+3)(-13)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow-8 x-8-14 y+28-13 z-39=0 \\ & \Rightarrow-8 x-14 y-13 z-19=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 8 x+14 y+13 z+19=0 \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: The co-ordinates of the point where the line through $\mathrm{A}(3,4,1)$ and $\mathrm{B}(5,1,6)$ crosses the $x y$-plane are
explanation:
Let $\mathrm{A}\left(x_1, y_1, \mathrm{z}_1\right)=\mathrm{A}(3,4,1)$

$$\mathrm{B}\left(x_2, y_2, \mathrm{z}_2\right)=\mathrm{B}(5,1,6)$$

The equation of line passing through the points $\left(x_1, y_1, z_1\right)$ and $\left(x_2, y_2, z_2\right)$ is given by

$$\begin{array}{ll} & \frac{x-x_1}{x_2-x_1}=\frac{y-y_1}{y_2-y_1}=\frac{z-z_1}{z_2-z_1} \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{x-3}{5-3}=\frac{y-4}{1-4}=\frac{z-1}{6-1} \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{x-3}{2}=\frac{y-4}{-3}=\frac{z-1}{5} \end{array}$$

Since the line crosses the XY plane, $\mathrm{z}=0$

$\therefore \quad \frac{x-3}{2}=\frac{y-4}{-3}=\frac{-1}{5}$

$\therefore \quad \frac{x-3}{2}=\frac{-1}{5}$ and $\frac{y-4}{-3}=\frac{-1}{5}$

$\Rightarrow x=\frac{13}{5}$ and $y=\frac{23}{5}$

Required point is $\left(\frac{13}{5}, \frac{23}{5}, 0\right)$

content: The Cartesian equation of a line is $2 x-2=3 y+1=6 z-2$, then the vector equation of the line is
explanation:
Given Cartesian equation of the line is

$$\begin{aligned} & 2 x-2=3 y+1=6 z-2 \\ & \Rightarrow 2(x-1)=3\left(y+\frac{1}{3}\right)=6\left(z-\frac{1}{3}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{x-1}{\frac{1}{2}}=\frac{y+\frac{1}{3}}{\frac{1}{3}}=\frac{z-\frac{1}{3}}{\frac{1}{6}} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{x-1}{3}=\frac{y+\frac{1}{3}}{2}=\frac{z-\frac{1}{3}}{1} \end{aligned}$$

$\therefore \quad$ The given line passes through $\left(1,-\frac{1}{3}, \frac{1}{3}\right)$ and has direction ratios proportional to $3,2,1$.

$\therefore \quad$ Vector equation is $\overline{\mathrm{r}}=\left(\hat{\mathrm{i}}-\frac{\hat{\mathrm{j}}}{3}+\frac{\hat{\mathrm{k}}}{3}\right)+\lambda(3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}})$

content: The lines $\frac{x-2}{1}=\frac{y-3}{1}=\frac{z-4}{-k} \quad$ and $\frac{x-1}{\mathrm{k}}=\frac{y-4}{2}=\frac{\mathrm{z}-5}{1}$ are coplanar if
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} &\text { If the given lines are co-planar, we get }\\ &\begin{aligned} & \left|\begin{array}{ccc} 2-1 & 3-4 & 4-5 \\ 1 & 1 & -\mathrm{k} \\ \mathrm{k} & 2 & 1 \end{array}\right|=0 \\ & \therefore \quad\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & -1 & -1 \\ 1 & 1 & -k \\ k & 2 & 1 \end{array}\right|=0 \\ & \therefore \quad 1(1+2 \mathrm{k})+1\left(1+\mathrm{k}^2\right)-1(2-\mathrm{k})=0 \\ & \therefore \quad 1+2 \mathrm{k}+1+\mathrm{k}^2-2+\mathrm{k}=0 \\ & \therefore \quad \mathrm{k}^2+3 \mathrm{k}=0 \\ & \therefore \quad \mathrm{k}=0 \text { or } \mathrm{k}=-3 \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: Let $\mathrm{L}_1: \frac{x+1}{3}=\frac{y+2}{1}=\frac{z+1}{2}$ and $\mathrm{L}_2: \frac{x-2}{1}=\frac{y+2}{2}=\frac{z-3}{3}$ be two given lines. Then the unit vector perpendicular to $L_1$ and $L_2$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} &\text { Vector perpendicular to } \mathrm{L}_1 \text { and } \mathrm{L}_2 \text { is } \mathrm{L}_1 \times \mathrm{L}_2\\ &\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad L_1 \times L_2=\left|\begin{array}{lll} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 3 & 1 & 2 \\ 1 & 2 & 3 \end{array}\right|=-\hat{i}-7 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k} \\ & \therefore \quad \text { Required unit vector }=\frac{-\hat{\mathrm{i}}-7 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+5 \hat{\mathrm{k}}}{\sqrt{(-1)^2+(-7)^2+(5)^2}} \\ & =\frac{-\hat{\mathrm{i}}-7 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+5 \hat{\mathrm{k}}}{\sqrt{75}}=\frac{-\hat{\mathrm{i}}-7 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+5 \hat{\mathrm{k}}}{5 \sqrt{3}} \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: Let $a, b \in R$. If the mirror image of the point $\mathrm{p}(\mathrm{a}, 6,9)$ w.r.t. line $\frac{x-3}{7}=\frac{y-2}{5}=\frac{z-1}{-9}$ is $(20, b,-a-9)$, then $|a+b|$ is equal to
explanation:
Note that mid-point of the line joining points $(a, 6,9)$ and $(20, b,-a-9)$ lies on the given line.

$\therefore \quad$ The midpoint is $\left(\frac{\mathrm{a}+20}{2}, \frac{6+\mathrm{b}}{2}, \frac{9-\mathrm{a}-9}{2}\right)$

Substituting this point in the equation of the given line, we get

$$\begin{array}{ll} & \frac{\frac{a+20}{2}-3}{7}=\frac{\frac{6+b}{2}-2}{5}=\frac{\frac{-a}{2}-1}{-9} \\ \therefore & \frac{a+14}{14}=\frac{a+2}{18} \\ \therefore & a=-56 \\ \therefore & \frac{-56+4}{14}=\frac{2+b}{10} \\ \therefore & b=-32 \\ \therefore \quad & |a+b|=88 \end{array}$$

content: A plane which is perpendicular to two planes $2 x-2 y+z=0$ and $x-y+2 z=4$, passes through $(1,-2,1)$. The distance of the plane from the point $(1,2,2)$ is
explanation:
The equation of a plane passing through $(1,-2,1)$ is

$$a(x-1)+b(y+2)+c(z-1)=0\quad\text{.... (i)}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Plane (i) is perpendicular to planes } \\ & 2 x-2 y+z=0 \text { and } x-y+2 z=4 . \\ & 2 a-2 b+c=0 \text {, and } \quad\text{.... (ii)}\\ & \therefore \quad a-b+2 c=0\quad\text{.... (iii)} \end{aligned}$$

Solving (ii) and (iii), we get

$$\mathrm{a}=-3, \mathrm{~b}=-3, \mathrm{c}=0$$

Substituting the values of $\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}, \mathrm{c}$ in equation (i), we get $$x+y+1=0$$

$\therefore \quad$ The distance of this plane from $(1,2,2)$ is

$$\mathrm{d}=\left|\frac{1+2+1}{\sqrt{1+1}}\right|=2 \sqrt{2}$$

content: Let $\mathrm{L}_1$ $\frac{x+1}{3}=\frac{y+2}{2}=\frac{z+1}{1}$ and $\mathrm{L}_2: \frac{x-2}{2}=\frac{y+2}{1}=\frac{z-3}{3}$ be the given lines. Then the unit vector perpendicular to $L_1$ and $L_2$ is
explanation:
To find the unit vector perpendicular to two lines in space, we first need the direction vectors of these lines.

Direction Vectors:

For line $ \mathrm{L}_1: \frac{x+1}{3}=\frac{y+2}{2}=\frac{z+1}{1} $, the direction vector is derived from the coefficients:

$ \mathbf{d}_1 = 3\hat{i} + 2\hat{j} + 1\hat{k} $

For line $ \mathrm{L}_2: \frac{x-2}{2}=\frac{y+2}{1}=\frac{z-3}{3} $, the direction vector is:

$ \mathbf{d}_2 = 2\hat{i} + 1\hat{j} + 3\hat{k} $

Cross Product:

The vector perpendicular to both direction vectors $ \mathbf{d}_1 $ and $ \mathbf{d}_2 $ is given by the cross product $ \mathbf{d}_1 \times \mathbf{d}_2 $.

$ \mathbf{d}_1 \times \mathbf{d}_2 = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 3 & 2 & 1 \\ 2 & 1 & 3 \end{vmatrix} $

Calculating the determinant, we find:

$ \mathbf{d}_1 \times \mathbf{d}_2 = \hat{i} (2 \cdot 3 - 1 \cdot 1) - \hat{j} (3 \cdot 3 - 1 \cdot 2) + \hat{k} (3 \cdot 1 - 2 \cdot 2) $

$ = \hat{i} (6 - 1) - \hat{j} (9 - 2) + \hat{k} (3 - 4) $

$ = 5\hat{i} - 7\hat{j} - \hat{k} $

Unit Vector:

The magnitude of the vector $ \mathbf{v} = 5\hat{i} - 7\hat{j} - \hat{k} $ can be calculated as:

$ |\mathbf{v}| = \sqrt{5^2 + (-7)^2 + (-1)^2} = \sqrt{25 + 49 + 1} = \sqrt{75} = 5\sqrt{3} $

The unit vector $ \hat{\mathbf{u}} $ perpendicular to both lines is:

$ \hat{\mathbf{u}} = \frac{5\hat{i} - 7\hat{j} - \hat{k}}{5\sqrt{3}} $

Conclusion:

The unit vector perpendicular to both $ \mathrm{L}_1 $ and $ \mathrm{L}_2 $ is:

$ \boxed{\frac{5 \hat{i} - 7 \hat{j} - \hat{k}}{5\sqrt{3}}} $

This corresponds to option C.

content: The equation of the plane passing through the point $(1,1,1)$ and perpendicular to the planes $2 x-y-2 z=5$ and $3 x-6 y+2 z=7$ is
explanation:
Required plane is perpendicular to planes $2 x-y-2 z=5$ and $3 x-6 y+2 z=7$

Equation of required plane is

$$\begin{aligned} & \left|\begin{array}{ccc} x-1 & y-1 & z-1 \\ 2 & -1 & -2 \\ 3 & -6 & 2 \end{array}\right|=0 \\ & \Rightarrow(x-1)(-14)-(y-1)(10) \\ & \quad+(z-1)(-12+3)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow-14 x+14-10 y+10-9 z+9=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 14 x+10 y+9 z=-33 \end{aligned}$$

content: Equation of the plane containing the straight line $\frac{x}{2}=\frac{y}{3}=\frac{z}{4}$ and perpendicular to the plane containing the straight lines $\frac{x}{3}=\frac{y}{4}=\frac{z}{2}$ and $\frac{x}{4}=\frac{y}{2}=\frac{z}{3}$ is
explanation:
Equation of the plane containing $\frac{x}{3}=\frac{y}{4}=\frac{z}{2}$ and $\frac{x}{4}=\frac{y}{2}=\frac{z}{3}$ is

$\left|\begin{array}{lll}x & y & z \\ 3 & 4 & 2 \\ 4 & 2 & 3\end{array}\right|=0$

$$\Rightarrow 8 x-y-10 z=0$$

Now required plane is perpendicular to this plane.

Consider option (C)

$$(8)(1)+(-1)(-2)+(-10)(1)=0$$

$\therefore \quad$ Option (C) is correct.

content: If $A(-4,5, P), B(3,1,4)$ and $C(-2,0, q)$ are the vertices of a triangle $A B C$ and $G(r, q, 1)$ is its centroid, then the value of $2 p+q-r$ is equal to
explanation:
$\mathrm{A}(-4,5, \mathrm{p}), \mathrm{B}(3,1,4)$ and $\mathrm{C}(-2,0, \mathrm{q})$ are vertices of triangle $A B C$

$\therefore \quad$ Centroid

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Centroid } \\ & \equiv\left(\frac{x_1+x_2+x_3}{3}, \frac{y_1+y_2+y_3}{3}, \frac{z_1+z_2+z_3}{3}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow(\mathrm{r}, \mathrm{q}, 1) \equiv\left(\frac{-4+3-2}{3}, \frac{5+1+0}{3}, \frac{p+4+q}{3}\right) \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow r=-1, q & =2, p+q+4=3 \\ \Rightarrow r=-1, & =2, p=-3 \\ \therefore\quad 2 p+q-r & =2(-3)+2+1 \\ & =-6+2+1 \\ & =-3 \end{aligned}$$

content: On which of the following lines lies the point of intersection of the line, $\frac{x-4}{2}=\frac{y-5}{2}=\frac{z-3}{1}$ and the plane $x+y+z=2$ ?
explanation:
Let $\frac{x-4}{2}=\frac{y-5}{2}=\frac{z-3}{1}=\lambda$

$$\Rightarrow x=2 \lambda+4, y=2 \lambda+5, z=\lambda+3$$

Given line lies on plane

$$x+y+z=2$$

$\therefore \quad(2 \lambda+4,2 \lambda+5, \lambda+3)$ lies on $x+y+z=2$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow(2 \lambda+4+2 \lambda+5+\lambda+3)=2 \\ & \Rightarrow 5 \lambda+12=2 \\ & \Rightarrow \lambda=-2 \end{aligned}$$

$\therefore \quad(0,1,1)$ lies on required plane.

$(0,1,1)$ satisfies option (A)

i.e., $\frac{x-1}{1}=\frac{y-3}{2}=\frac{z+4}{-5}$

content: Equation of the plane containing the straight line $\frac{x}{3}=\frac{y}{2}=\frac{z}{4}$ and perpendicular to the plane containing the straight lines $\frac{x}{4}=\frac{y}{3}=\frac{z}{2}$ and $\frac{x}{2}=\frac{y}{-4}=\frac{z}{3}$ is
explanation:
Equation of the plane containing $\frac{x}{4}=\frac{y}{3}=\frac{z}{2}$ and $\frac{x}{2}=\frac{y}{-4}=\frac{z}{3}$ is

$$\begin{aligned} & \left|\begin{array}{ccc} x & y & z \\ 4 & 3 & 2 \\ 2 & -4 & 3 \end{array}\right|=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 17 x-8 y-22 z=0 \end{aligned}$$

Now required plane is perpendicular to this plane.

Consider option (C)

$$(17)(6)+(-8)(-67)+(-22)(29)=0$$

$\therefore$ Option (C) is correct.

content: The value of $m$, such that $\frac{x-4}{1}=\frac{y-2}{1}=\frac{2 z-m}{3}$ lies in the plane $2 x-5 y+2 z=7$, is
explanation:
According to the given condition, point $\left(4,2, \frac{\mathrm{~m}}{2}\right)$ lies on the plane $2 x-5 y+2 z=7$.

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow 2(4)-5(2)+2\left(\frac{\mathrm{~m}}{2}\right)=7 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{~m}=9 \end{aligned}$$

content: The image of the line $\frac{x-1}{3}=\frac{y-3}{1}=\frac{z-4}{-5}$ in the plane $2 x-y+z+3=0$ is the line
explanation:
Given line $\frac{x-1}{3}=\frac{y-3}{1}=\frac{z-4}{-5}$ passes through the point $(1,3,4)$

Let the required line passes through the point (p, q, r)

Now, according to the given condition, we get Distance between point $(1,3,4)$ and the given plane $=$ Distance between point $(p, q, r)$ and the given plane.

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad\left|\frac{2(1)-(3)+(4)+3}{\sqrt{(2)^2+(-1)^2+(1)^2}}\right|=\left|\frac{2(p)-(q)+(r)+3}{\sqrt{(2)^2+(-1)^2+(1)^2}}\right| \\ & \therefore \quad|2 p-q+r+3|=6\quad\text{.... (i)} \end{aligned}$$

Note that line given in option (D) passes through the point $(-3,5,2)$ and this point satisfies the condition given in equation (i).

$\therefore \quad$ Option (D) is correct.

content: Let $\mathrm{P}(2,3,6)$ be a point in space and Q be a point on the line $\bar{r}=(\hat{i}-\hat{j}+2 \hat{k})+\mu(-3 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+5 \hat{k})$. Then the value of $\mu$ for which vector $\overline{\mathrm{PQ}}$ is parallel to the plane $x-4 y+4 z=1$ is
explanation:
Let the position vector of Q be

$$\begin{aligned} & (\hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}})+\mu(-3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+5 \hat{\mathrm{k}}) \\ & =(-3 \mu+1) \hat{\mathrm{i}}+(\mu-1) \hat{\mathrm{j}}+(5 \mu+2) \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ \therefore \quad & \overline{\mathrm{PQ}}=(-3 \mu-1) \hat{\mathrm{i}}+(\mu-4) \hat{\mathrm{i}}+(5 \mu-4) \hat{\mathrm{k}} \end{aligned}$$

Since $\overline{\mathrm{PQ}}$ is parallel to the plane,

$$\begin{aligned} & (-3 \mu-1)(1)+(\mu-4)(-4)+(5 \mu-4)(4)=0 \\ \Rightarrow \quad & \mu=\frac{1}{13} \end{aligned}$$

content: Distance between the parallel lines $\frac{x}{3}=\frac{y-1}{-2}=\frac{z}{1}$ and $\frac{x+4}{3}=\frac{y-3}{-2}=\frac{z+2}{1}$ is
explanation:
The vector equations of the given lines are $\bar{r}=\hat{j}+\lambda(3 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+\hat{k})$ and $$\overline{\mathrm{r}}=-4 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}+\mu(3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}})$$

The distance between the parallel lines $\bar{r}=\bar{a}_1+\lambda \bar{b}$ and $\bar{r}=\bar{a}_2+\mu \bar{b}$ is given by

$$d=\left|\frac{\left(\bar{a}_2-\bar{a}_1\right) \times \bar{b}}{|\bar{b}|}\right|$$

Here, $\bar{a}_1=\hat{j}, \bar{a}_2=-4 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k}, \bar{b}=3 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \overline{\mathrm{a}}_2-\overline{\mathrm{a}}_1=-4 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ & \left(\bar{a}_2-\bar{a}_1\right) \times \bar{b}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ -4 & 2 & -2 \\ 3 & -2 & 1 \end{array}\right| \\ & =-2 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k} \\ & |\bar{b}|=\sqrt{9+4+1}=\sqrt{14} \\ & \therefore \quad \mathrm{~d}=\left|\frac{-2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}}{\sqrt{14}}\right| \\ & =\sqrt{\frac{4+4+4}{14}} \\ & =\sqrt{\frac{12}{14}}=\sqrt{\frac{6}{7}} \text { units } \end{aligned}$$

content: The equation of the plane, passing through the mid point of the line segment of join of the points $\mathrm{P}(1,2,5)$ and $\mathrm{Q}(3,4,3)$ and perpendicular to it, is
explanation:
M is the midpoint

$$\mathrm{M}=(2,3,4)$$

d.r.s of $P Q: 2,2,-2$

Equation of plane is

$$\begin{aligned} & 2(x-2)+2(y-3)-2(z-4)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 2 x+2 y-2 z-2=0 \\ & \Rightarrow x+y-z-1=0 \end{aligned}$$

content: The area of the triangle, whose vertices are $A \equiv(1,-1,2), B \equiv(2,1,-1)$ and $C \equiv(3,-1,2)$, is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \overline{\mathrm{a}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}, \overline{\mathrm{~b}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}, \overline{\mathrm{c}}=3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ & \overline{\mathrm{AB}}=\overline{\mathrm{b}}-\overline{\mathrm{a}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k} \\ & \overline{\mathrm{AC}}=\overline{\mathrm{c}}-\overline{\mathrm{a}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{array}{ll} & \text { Area of triangle }=\frac{1}{2}|\overline{\mathrm{AB}} \times \overline{\mathrm{AC}}| \\ \therefore & \overline{\mathrm{AB}} \times \overline{\mathrm{AC}}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{\mathrm{i}} & \hat{\mathrm{j}} & \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ 1 & 2 & -3 \\ 2 & 0 & 0 \end{array}\right|=-6 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-4 \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ \therefore \quad & |\overline{\mathrm{AB}} \times \overline{\mathrm{AC}}|=\sqrt{36+16}=\sqrt{52}=2 \sqrt{13} \\ \therefore \quad & \text { Area of triangle }=\frac{1}{2}(2 \sqrt{13})=\sqrt{13} \text { sq. units } \end{array}$$

content: The equation of the line, through $\mathrm{A}(1,2,3)$ and perpendicular to the vector $2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}$ and $\hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}$, is
explanation:
To find the equation of the line passing through point $ A(1, 2, 3) $ and perpendicular to two given vectors, we need to determine a direction vector that is perpendicular to both given vectors.

The given vectors are:

$ \mathbf{v_1} = 2\hat{\mathbf{i}} + \hat{\mathbf{j}} - \hat{\mathbf{k}} $

$ \mathbf{v_2} = \hat{\mathbf{i}} + 3\hat{\mathbf{j}} + 2\hat{\mathbf{k}} $

To find a direction vector perpendicular to both, we use the cross product of $ \mathbf{v_1} $ and $ \mathbf{v_2} $.

The cross product $\mathbf{v_1} \times \mathbf{v_2}$ is calculated as follows:

$ \mathbf{v_1} \times \mathbf{v_2} = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{\mathbf{i}} & \hat{\mathbf{j}} & \hat{\mathbf{k}} \\ 2 & 1 & -1 \\ 1 & 3 & 2 \end{vmatrix} $

Expanding the determinant:

$ \mathbf{v_1} \times \mathbf{v_2} = \hat{\mathbf{i}} (1 \times 2 - (-1) \times 3) - \hat{\mathbf{j}} (2 \times 2 - (-1) \times 1) + \hat{\mathbf{k}} (2 \times 3 - 1 \times 1) $

$ = \hat{\mathbf{i}} (2 + 3) - \hat{\mathbf{j}} (4 + 1) + \hat{\mathbf{k}} (6 - 1) $

$ = 5\hat{\mathbf{i}} - 5\hat{\mathbf{j}} + 5\hat{\mathbf{k}} $

The resulting direction vector is proportional to $ \hat{\mathbf{i}} - \hat{\mathbf{j}} + \hat{\mathbf{k}} $.

Thus, the equation of the line is:

$ \overline{\mathbf{r}} = (\hat{\mathbf{i}} + 2\hat{\mathbf{j}} + 3\hat{\mathbf{k}}) + \lambda(\hat{\mathbf{i}} - \hat{\mathbf{j}} + \hat{\mathbf{k}}) $

So, the correct answer is:

Option D

$ \overline{\mathbf{r}} = (\hat{\mathbf{i}} + 2\hat{\mathbf{j}} + 3\hat{\mathbf{k}}) + \lambda(\hat{\mathbf{i}} - \hat{\mathbf{j}} + \hat{\mathbf{k}}) $

content: Let $P$ be the image of the point $(3,1,7)$ with respect to the plane $x-y+z=3$. Then the equation of the plane passing through $P$ and containing the straight line $\frac{x}{1}=\frac{y}{2}=\frac{z}{1}$ is
explanation:
The d.r.s. of the normal to the plane are $1,-1,1$

$\therefore \quad$ The equation of line QM is

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{x-3}{1}=\frac{y-1}{-1}=\frac{z-7}{1}=\lambda(\text { say }) \\ & \Rightarrow x=\lambda+3, y=-\lambda+1, z=\lambda+7 \end{aligned}$$

Let $M \equiv(\lambda+3,-\lambda+1, \lambda+7)$

$\therefore \quad$ Equation of plane becomes

$$\begin{aligned} & 1(\lambda+3)-1(-\lambda+1)+1(\lambda+7)=3 \\ & \Rightarrow 3 \lambda+6=0 \Rightarrow \lambda=-2 \\ & M \equiv(1,3,5) \end{aligned}$$

Since $M$ is the midpoint of PQ.

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \frac{3+a}{2}=1, \frac{1+b}{2}=3, \frac{7+c}{2}=5 \\ & \Rightarrow a=-1, b=5, c=3 \end{aligned}$$

Equation of the plane passing through P and containing the given line is

$$\begin{aligned} & \left|\begin{array}{ccc} x+1 & y-5 & z-3 \\ 1 & -5 & -3 \\ 1 & 2 & 1 \end{array}\right|=0 \\ & \Rightarrow x-4 y+7 z=0 \end{aligned}$$

content: The incentre of the triangle whose vertices are $P(0,3,0), Q(0,0,4)$ and $R(0,3,4)$ is
explanation:
Let $\overline{\mathrm{p}}=3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}, \overline{\mathrm{q}}=4 \hat{\mathrm{k}}, \overline{\mathrm{r}}=3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{k}}$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad & \overline{\mathrm{PQ}}=-3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{k}}, \\ & \overline{\mathrm{QR}}=3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}, \\ & \overline{\mathrm{PR}}=4 \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ & \Rightarrow|\overline{\mathrm{PQ}}|=5,|\overline{\mathrm{QR}}|=3,|\overline{\mathrm{PR}}|=4 \end{aligned}$$

Incentre of $\triangle P Q R$ is given by

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{PQ}}| \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}+|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{QR}}| \overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}}+|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{PR}}| \overrightarrow{\mathrm{q}}}{|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{PQ}}|+|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{QR}}|+|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{PR}}|} \\ & =\frac{5(3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{k}})+3(3 \hat{\mathrm{j}})+4(4 \hat{\mathrm{k}})}{5+3+4} \\ & =\frac{24 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+36 \hat{\mathrm{k}}}{12} \\ & =2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}} \end{aligned}$$

$\therefore \quad$ Incentre $\equiv(0,2,3)$

content: The vector equation of a line whose Cartesian equations are $y=2,4 x-3 z+5=0$ is
explanation:
Given cartesian equation of line is

$$\begin{aligned} & 4 x-3 z+5=0, y=2 \\ & \Rightarrow 4 x=3 z-5, y=2 \\ & \Rightarrow 4 x=3\left(z-\frac{5}{3}\right), y=2 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{x}{3}=\frac{z-\frac{5}{3}}{4}, y=2 \end{aligned}$$

$\therefore \quad$ The given line passes $\left(0,2, \frac{5}{3}\right)$ and the direction ratios are proportional to $3,0,4$.

$\therefore \quad$ The vector equation is

$$\bar{r}=\left(2 \hat{j}+\frac{5}{3} \hat{k}\right)+\lambda(3 \hat{i}+4 \hat{k})$$

content: The Cartesian equation of the plane, passing through the points $(3,1,1),(1,2,3)$ and $(-1,4,2)$, is
explanation:
Equation of plane passing through $(3,1,1)$, $(1,2,3)$ and $(-1,4,2)$ is

$$\begin{aligned} & \left|\begin{array}{ccc} x-3 & y-1 & z-1 \\ 1-3 & 2-1 & 3-1 \\ -1-3 & 4-1 & 2-1 \end{array}\right|=0 \\ & \Rightarrow\left|\begin{array}{ccc} x-3 & y-1 & z-1 \\ -2 & 1 & 2 \\ -4 & 3 & 1 \end{array}\right|=0 \\ & \Rightarrow(x-3)(1-6)-(y-1)(-2+8) \\ & +(z-1)(-6+4)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow-5(x-3)-6(y-1)-2(z-1)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow-5 x+15-6 y+6-2 z+2=0 \\ & \Rightarrow-5 x-6 y-2 z+23=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 5 x+6 y+2 z-23=0 \end{aligned}$$

content: The equation of the line passing through the point $(-1,3,-2)$ and perpendicular to each of the lines $\frac{x}{1}=\frac{y}{2}=\frac{z}{3}$ and $\frac{x+2}{-3}=\frac{y-1}{2}=\frac{z+1}{5}$, is
explanation:
Let $a, b, c$ be the direction ratios of the required line.

Since the line is perpendicular to the lines with d.r.s. 1,2,3 and $-3,2,5$.

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore \quad & a+2 b+3 c=0 \quad\text{... (i)}\\ & \text { and }-3 a+2 b+5 c=0 \quad\text{... (ii)}\\ & \Rightarrow \frac{a}{2}=\frac{b}{-7}=\frac{c}{4}\quad\text{... [From (i) and (ii)]} \end{array}$$

$\therefore \quad$ Equation of the required line is

$$\frac{x+1}{2}=\frac{y-3}{-7}=\frac{z+2}{4}$$

content: If the line $\frac{x-3}{2}=\frac{y+2}{-1}=\frac{z+4}{3}$ lies in the plane $\ell x+m y-z=9$, then $\ell^2+m^2$ is
explanation:
Line is perpendicular to normal of plane

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow(2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}) \cdot(l \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\mathrm{m} \hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}})=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 2 l-\mathrm{m}-3=0\quad\text{... (i)} \end{aligned}$$

$(3,-2,-4)$ lies on the plane $l x+m y-z=9$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad 3 l-2 m+4=9 \\ & \Rightarrow 3 l-2 m=5\quad\text{.... (ii)} \end{aligned}$$

Solving (i) and (ii), we get

$$\begin{aligned} & l=1, \mathrm{~m}=-1 \\ \therefore \quad & l^2+\mathrm{m}^2=2 \end{aligned}$$

content: The mirror image of $$\mathrm{P}(2,4,-1)$$ in the plane $$x-y+2 z-2=0$$ is $$(\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}, \mathrm{c})$$, then the value of $$a+b+c$$ is
explanation:
The d.r.s. of the normal to the plane are $$1,-1,2$$.

$$\therefore \quad$$ The equation of line PM is

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{x-2}{1}=\frac{y-4}{-1}=\frac{z+1}{2}=\lambda \text { (say) } \\ \Rightarrow & x=\lambda+2, y=-\lambda+4, z=2 \lambda-1 \end{aligned}$$

Let $$M \equiv(\lambda+2,-\lambda+4,2 \lambda-1)$$

$$\therefore \quad$$ Equation of plane becomes

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore \quad & \text { Equation of plane becomes } \\ & 1(\lambda+2)-1(-\lambda+4)+2(2 \lambda-1)-2=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \lambda=1 \\ \therefore \quad & M \equiv(3,3,1) \end{array}$$

Since $$M$$ is the mid-point of $$P Q$$.

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore & \quad\frac{2+a}{2}=3, \frac{4+b}{2}=3, \frac{-1+c}{2}=1 \\ & \Rightarrow a=4, b=2, c=3 \\ & \Rightarrow a+b+c=9 \end{aligned}$$

content: If the lines $$\frac{x-\mathrm{k}}{2}=\frac{y+1}{3}=\frac{\mathrm{z}-1}{4}$$ and $$\frac{x-3}{1}=\frac{y-\frac{9}{2}}{2}=\frac{\mathrm{z}}{1}$$ intersect, then the value of $$\mathrm{k}$$ is
explanation:
Since the given lines intersect,

$$\begin{aligned} &\left|\begin{array}{ccc} x_2-x_1 & y_2-y_1 & z_2-z_1 \\ a_1 & b_1 & c_1 \\ a_2 & b_2 & c_2 \end{array}\right|=0 \\ & \therefore \quad\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 3-k & \frac{9}{2}+1 & 0-1 \\ 2 & 3 & 4 \\ 1 & 2 & 1 \end{array}\right|=0 \\ & \Rightarrow\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 3-k & \frac{11}{2} & -1 \\ 2 & 3 & 4 \\ 1 & 2 & 1 \end{array}\right|=0 \\ & \Rightarrow-5+5 k=0 \\ & \Rightarrow k=1 \end{aligned}$$

content: A vector parallel to the line of intersection of the planes $$\bar{r} \cdot(3 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k})=1$$ and $$\bar{r} \cdot(\hat{i}+4 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k})=2$$ is
explanation:
The line of intersection of the planes $$\bar{r} \cdot(3 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k})=1$$ and $$\bar{r} \cdot(\hat{i}+4 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k})=2$$ is perpendicular to each of the normal vectors $$\overline{n_1}=3 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k} \text { and } \overline{n_2}=\hat{i}+4 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k} \text {. }$$

$$\therefore \quad$$ The line is parallel to the vector $$\bar{n}_1 \times \bar{n}_2$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \overline{\mathrm{n}}_1 \times \overline{\mathrm{n}}_2 & =\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{\mathrm{i}} & \hat{\mathrm{j}} & \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ 3 & -1 & 1 \\ 1 & 4 & -2 \end{array}\right| \\ & =-2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+7 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+13 \hat{\mathrm{k}} \end{aligned}$$

content: The length of the perpendicular drawn from the point $$(1,2,3)$$ to the line $$\frac{x-6}{3}=\frac{y-7}{2}=\frac{z-7}{-2}$$ is
explanation:
Let $$\frac{x-6}{3}=\frac{y-7}{2}=\frac{z-7}{-2}=\lambda$$ (say)

Any point on the line is

$$\mathrm{P}(3 \lambda+6,2 \lambda+7,-2 \lambda+7)$$

Let $$\mathrm{A} \equiv(1,2,3)$$

The d.r.s. of line AP are

$$\begin{aligned} & 3 \lambda+6-1,2 \lambda+7-2,-2 \lambda+7-3 \\ & \text { i.e. } 3 \lambda+5,2 \lambda+5,-2 \lambda+4 \end{aligned}$$

Since AP is perpendicular to the given line,

$$\begin{aligned} & 3(3 \lambda+5)+2(2 \lambda+5)-2(-2 \lambda+4)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 17 \lambda+17=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \lambda=-1 \\ & \therefore \quad \mathrm{P} \equiv(3,5,9) \\ & \therefore \quad \mathrm{AP}=\sqrt{(3-1)^2+(5-2)^2+(9-3)^2} \\ & =\sqrt{49} \\ & =7 \text { units } \\ & \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$$ is right angled at $$\mathrm{A}$$, where $$A \equiv(4,2, x), \mathrm{B} \equiv(3,1,8)$$ and $$C \equiv(2,-1,2)$$, then the value of $$x$$ is
explanation:
Since $$\triangle A B C$$ is right angled at $$A$$,

$$\overline{\mathrm{AB}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{AC}}=0$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow[-\hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+(8-x) \hat{\mathrm{k}}] \cdot(-2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+(2-x) \hat{\mathrm{k}})=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 2+3+(8-x)(2-x)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow x^2-10 x+21=0 \\ & \Rightarrow(x-3)(x-7)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow x=3 \text { or } x=7 \end{aligned}$$

content: The angle between the lines, whose direction cosines $$l, \mathrm{~m}, \mathrm{n}$$ satisfy the equations $$l+\mathrm{m}+\mathrm{n}=0$$ and $$2 l^2+2 \mathrm{~m}^2-\mathrm{n}^2=0$$, is
explanation:
Substituting $$\mathrm{n}=-l-\mathrm{m}$$ in $$2 l^2+2 m^2-\mathrm{n}^2=0$$, we get

$$2 l^2+2 \mathrm{~m}^2-(-l-\mathrm{m})^2=0$$

$$\Rightarrow l^2+\mathrm{m}^2-2 l \mathrm{~m}=0$$

$$\Rightarrow(l-\mathrm{m})^2=0$$

$$\Rightarrow l=\mathrm{m}$$

If $$l=\mathrm{m}$$, then $$\mathrm{n}=-2 \mathrm{~m}$$

$$\Rightarrow \frac{l}{1}=\frac{\mathrm{m}}{1}=\frac{\mathrm{n}}{-2}$$

The direction ratios of both the lines are same.

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore \quad & \cos \theta= \pm 1 \\ & \Rightarrow \theta=0^{\circ} \text { or } 180^{\circ} \end{array}$$

content: Equation of the plane passing through $$(1,-1,2)$$ and perpendicular to the planes $$x+2 y-2 z=4$$ and $$3 x+2 y+z=6$$ is
explanation:
The equation of plane passing through $$(1,-1,2)$$ is

$$\mathrm{a}(x-1)+\mathrm{b}(y+1)+\mathrm{c}(\mathrm{z}-2)=0$$ ..... (i)

Since plane (i) is perpendicular to the planes $$x+2 y-2 z=4$$ and $$3 x+2 y+z=6$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad a+2 b-2 c=0 \\ & \text { and } 3 a+2 b+c=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{a}{6}=\frac{b}{-7}=\frac{c}{-4} \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore \quad$$ The equation of the required plane is

$$\begin{aligned} & 6(x-1)-7(y+1)-4(z-2)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 6 x-7 y-4 z-5=0 \end{aligned}$$

content: A line with positive direction cosines passes through the point $$\mathrm{P}(2,-1,2)$$ and makes equal angles with the co-ordinate axes. The line meets the plane $$2 x+y+z=9$$ at point $$\mathrm{Q}$$. The length of the line segment $$P Q$$ equals
explanation:
Since direction cosines of $$\mathrm{PQ}$$ are equal and positive.

$$\therefore \quad$$ The d.r.s. of $$P Q$$ are $$\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}, \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}, \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$$

$$\therefore \quad$$ The equation of the line $$P Q$$ is

$$\frac{x-2}{\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}}=\frac{y+1}{\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}}=\frac{z-2}{\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}}$$

$$\Rightarrow x-2=y+1=\mathrm{z}-2=\mathrm{k} \text {, say }$$

$$\therefore \quad$$ Co-ordinates of the point $$\mathrm{Q}$$ are $$(\mathrm{k}+2, \mathrm{k}-1, \mathrm{k}+2)$$

$$\text { The point Q lies on the plane } 2 x+y+z=9$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad 2(k+2)+k-1+k+2=9 \\\\ & \Rightarrow 4 k+5=9 \quad \Rightarrow k=1 \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \mathrm{Q} & \equiv(3,0,3) \\\\ \therefore \quad \mathrm{PQ} & =\sqrt{(3-2)^2+(0+1)^2+(3-2)^2} \\\\ & =\sqrt{1+1+1}=\sqrt{3} \end{aligned}$$

content: If the shortest distance between the lines $$\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y-2}{3}=\frac{z-3}{\lambda}$$ and $$\frac{x-2}{1}=\frac{y-4}{4}=\frac{z-5}{5}$$ is $$\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$$, then sum of possible values of $$\lambda$$ is
explanation:
The shortest distance between the lines $$\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y-2}{3}=\frac{z-3}{\lambda}$$ and $$\frac{x-2}{1}=\frac{y-4}{4}=\frac{z-5}{5}$$ is

$$\mathrm{d}=\left|\frac{\left|\begin{array}{ccc} x_2-x_1 & y_2-y_1 & \mathrm{z}_2-\mathrm{z}_1 \\ \mathrm{a}_1 & \mathrm{~b}_1 & \mathrm{c}_1 \\ \mathrm{a}_2 & \mathrm{~b}_2 & \mathrm{c}_2 \end{array}\right|}{\sqrt{\left(\mathrm{a}_1 \mathrm{~b}_2-\mathrm{a}_2 \mathrm{~b}_1\right)^2+\left(\mathrm{a}_1 \mathrm{c}_2-\mathrm{a}_2 \mathrm{c}_1\right)^2+\left(\mathrm{b}_1 \mathrm{c}_2-\mathrm{b}_2 \mathrm{c}_1\right)^2}}\right|$$

$$\therefore \quad d=\left|\frac{\left|\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 2 & 2 \\ 2 & 3 & \lambda \\ 1 & 4 & 5 \end{array}\right|}{\sqrt{(8-3)^2+(10-\lambda)^2+(15-4 \lambda)^2}}\right|$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}=\left|\frac{5-2 \lambda}{\sqrt{17 \lambda^2-140 \lambda+350}}\right| \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{1}{3}=\frac{25-20 \lambda+4 \lambda^2}{17 \lambda^2-140 \lambda+350} \\ & \Rightarrow 5 \lambda^2-80 \lambda+275=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \lambda^2-16 \lambda+55=0 \\ & \Rightarrow(\lambda-5)(\lambda-11)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \lambda=5 \text { or } \lambda=11 \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore \quad$$ Sum of possible values of $$\lambda=16$$

content:
Consider the lines $$\mathrm{L}_1: \frac{x+1}{3}=\frac{y+2}{1}=\frac{\mathrm{z}+1}{2}$$

$$\mathrm{L}_2: \frac{x-2}{1}=\frac{y+2}{2}=\frac{\mathrm{z}-3}{3}$$, then the unit vector perpendicular to both $$\mathrm{L}_1$$ and $$\mathrm{L}_2$$ is
explanation:
Lines $$L_1$$ and $$L_2$$ are parallel to the vectors $$\bar{b}_1=3 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+2 \hat{k} \text { and } \bar{b}_2=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}$$ respectively.

$$\therefore \quad$$ The unit vector perpendicular to both $$\mathrm{L}_1$$ and $$\mathrm{L}_2$$ is $$\hat{\mathrm{n}}=\frac{\overline{\mathrm{b}}_1 \times \overline{\mathrm{b}}_2}{\left|\overline{\mathrm{b}}_1 \times \overline{\mathrm{b}}_2\right|}$$

Now, $$\overline{\mathrm{b}}_1 \times \overline{\mathrm{b}}_2=\left|\begin{array}{lll}\hat{\mathrm{i}} & \hat{\mathrm{j}} & \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ 3 & 1 & 2 \\ 1 & 2 & 3\end{array}\right|=-\hat{\mathrm{i}}-7 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+5 \hat{\mathrm{k}}$$

$$\therefore \quad \hat{\mathrm{n}}=\frac{1}{5 \sqrt{3}}(-\hat{\mathrm{i}}-7 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+5 \hat{\mathrm{k}})$$

content: A tetrahedron has vertices at $$P(2,1,3), Q(-1,1,2), R(1,2,1)$$ and $$O(0,0,0)$$, then angle between the faces $$O P Q$$ and $$P Q R$$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} \mathbf{O P} \times \mathbf{O Q} & =(2 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{j}}+3 \hat{\mathbf{k}}) \times(-\hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{j}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{k}}) \\ & =-\hat{\mathbf{i}}-7 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+3 \hat{\mathbf{k}}=a(\text { let }) \\ \mathbf{P Q} \times \mathbf{P R} & =(-3 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+0 \hat{\mathbf{j}}-\hat{\mathbf{k}}) \times(-\hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{j}}-2 \hat{\mathbf{k}}) \\ & =\hat{\mathbf{i}}-5 \hat{\mathbf{j}}-3 \hat{\mathbf{k}}=b \text { (let) } \\ \cos \theta & =\left[\frac{a \cdot b}{|a||b|}\right] \\ \cos \theta & =\left|\frac{-1+35-9}{\sqrt{59} \sqrt{35}}\right| \\ \theta & =\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{25}{\sqrt{59} \sqrt{35}}\right) \end{aligned}$$

content: A plane is parallel to two lines whose direction ratios are $$2,0,-2$$ and $$-2,2,0$$ and it contains the point $$(2,2,2)$$. If it cuts coordinate axes at $$A, B, C$$, then the volume of the tetrahedron $$O A B C$$ (in cubic units) is
explanation:
Let the equation of the plane through $$(2,2,2)$$ be $$a(x-2)+b(y-2)+c(z-2)=0$$

Since, it is parallel to the straight lines having Dr's $$(1,0,-1)$$ and $$(-1,1,0)$$, therefore $$a-c=0$$ and $$-a+b=0$$

$$\Rightarrow \quad a=b=c$$

Therefore, equation of plane is

$$\begin{aligned} & x-2+y-2+z-2=0 \\ & x+y+z=6 \Rightarrow \frac{x}{6}+\frac{y}{6}+\frac{z}{6}=1 \end{aligned}$$

Its intercepts on coordinate axes are $$A(6,0,0), B(0,6,0)$$ and $$C(0,0,6)$$.

Hence, the volume of tetrahedron $$O A B C$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{1}{6}[a b c]=\frac{1}{6}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 6 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 6 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 6 \end{array}\right] \\ & =\frac{1}{6} \times 6 \times 36=36 \text { cube units } \end{aligned}$$

content: The incentre of the $$\triangle A B C$$, whose vertices are $$A(0,2,1), B(-2,0,0)$$ and $$C(-2,0,2)$$, is
explanation:
We have, $$\triangle A B C$$ with $$A(0,2,1), B(-2,0,0)$$ and $$C(-2,0,2)$$.

$$\begin{aligned} \text { So, } A B & =\sqrt{9}=3=c, \\ B C & =\sqrt{4}=2=a \\ \text { and } A C & =\sqrt{9}=3=b \end{aligned}$$

We know that,

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { incentre }=\left(\frac{a x_1+b x_2+c x_3}{a+b+c}, \frac{a y_1+b y_2+c y_3}{a+b+c},\right. \\ & \left.\frac{a z_1+b z_2+c z_3}{a+b+c}\right) \\ & =\left(\frac{0-6-6}{8}, \frac{4+0+0}{8}, \frac{2+0+6}{8}\right) \\ & =\left(\frac{-3}{2}, \frac{1}{2}, 1\right) \end{aligned}$$

content: The acute angle between the line joining the points $$(2,1,-3),(-3,1,7)$$ and a line parallel to $$\frac{x-1}{3}=\frac{y}{4}=\frac{z+3}{5}$$ through the point $$(-1,0,4)$$ is
explanation:
Direction ratio of the line joining the points $$(2,1,-3)$$ and $$(-3,1,7)$$ are $$\left(a_1, b_1, c_1\right)$$ i.e. $$(-5,0,10)$$.

Direction ratio of the line parallel to line $$\frac{x-1}{3}=\frac{y}{4}=\frac{z+3}{5} \text { are }\left(a_2, b_2, c_2\right) \text { i.e., }(3,4,5)$$

Angle between two lines is given by

$$\begin{aligned} \cos \theta & =\frac{[(-5 \times 3)+(0 \times 4)+(10 \times 5)]}{\sqrt{25+0+100} \sqrt{9+16+25}}=\frac{35}{25 \sqrt{10}} \\ \therefore \quad \theta & =\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{7}{5 \sqrt{10}}\right) \end{aligned}$$

content: The foot of the perpendicular from the point $$(1,2,3)$$ on the line $$\mathbf{r}=(6 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+7 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+7 \hat{\mathbf{k}})+\lambda(3 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{j}}-2 \hat{\mathbf{k}})$$ has the coordinates
explanation:
The given point is $$P(1,2,3)$$.

and given line is $$\mathbf{r}=(6 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+7 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+7 \hat{\mathbf{k}})+\lambda(3 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{j}}-2 \hat{\mathbf{k}})$$

$$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{x-6}{3}=\frac{y-7}{2}=\frac{z-7}{-2}=k \text { (say) }$$ ..... (i)

So, any point on this line is given as $$ Q(3 k+6,2 k+7,-2 k+7)$$

Now, direction ratios of $$P Q$$ are $$3 k+5,2 k+5,-2 k+4$$

Also, $$P Q \perp \mathbf{r}$$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \Rightarrow & 3(3 k+5)+2(2 k+5)-2(-2 k+4)=0 \\ \Rightarrow & 17 k+17=0 \Rightarrow k=-1 \end{array}$$

Hence, foot of the perpendicular drawn on the given line is $$(3,5,9)$$.

content: The distance of the point $$(1,6,2)$$ from the point of intersection of the line $$\frac{x-2}{3}=\frac{y+1}{4}=\frac{z-2}{12}$$ and the plane $$x-y+z=16$$ is
explanation:
We have, equation of line.

$$\frac{x-2}{3}=\frac{y+1}{4}=\frac{z-2}{12}$$ and plane $$: x-y+z=16$$

any point on line is $$(3 t+2,4 t-1,12 t+2)$$ and this point will satisfy the plane.

$$\therefore 3 t+2-4 t+1+12 t+2=16 \Rightarrow 11 t=11 \Rightarrow t=1$$

So, point will be $$(5,3,14)$$

Hence, distance between $$(5,3,14)$$ and $$(1,6,2)$$ is

$$\begin{aligned} & =\sqrt{(1-5)^2+(6-3)^2+(2-14)^2} \\ & =\sqrt{16+9+144}=\sqrt{169}=13 \text { units } \end{aligned}$$

content: A line drawn from the point $$\mathrm{A}(1,3,2)$$ parallel to the line $$\frac{x}{2}=\frac{y}{4}=\frac{z}{1}$$, intersects the plane $$3 x+y+2 z=5$$ in point $$\mathrm{B}$$, then co-ordinates of point $$\mathrm{B}$$ are
explanation:
The d.r.s. of the line $$\frac{x}{2}=\frac{y}{4}=\frac{z}{1}$$ are $$2,4,1$$.

$$\therefore \quad$$ The d.r.s. of any line parallel to it are also $$2,4,1$$.

The equation of the line passing through $$\mathrm{A}(1,3,2)$$ is $$\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y-3}{4}=\frac{z-2}{1}=\lambda$$ (say)

Then, any point on the line is

$$B \equiv(2 \lambda+1,4 \lambda+3, \lambda+2)$$

The point $$\mathrm{B}$$ lies on the plane $$3 x+y+2 z=5$$.

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad & 3(2 \lambda+1)+4 \lambda+3+2(\lambda+2)=5 \\ & \Rightarrow 12 \lambda+10=5 \\ & \Rightarrow \lambda=\frac{-5}{12} \\ \therefore \quad & B \equiv\left(\frac{1}{6}, \frac{4}{3}, \frac{19}{12}\right) \end{aligned}$$

content: A line $$\mathrm{L}_1$$ passes through the point, whose p. v. (position vector) $$3 \hat{i}$$, is parallel to the vector $$-\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}$$. Another line $$\mathrm{L}_2$$ passes through the point having p.v. $$\hat{i}+\hat{j}$$ is parallel to vector $$\hat{i}+\hat{k}$$, then the point of intersection of lines $$L_1$$ and $$L_2$$ has p.v.
explanation:
Equation of line $$\mathrm{L}_1$$ is $$\overline{\mathrm{r}}=3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\lambda(-\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}})\quad$$ .... (i)

Equation of line $$L_2$$ is $$\overline{r^{\prime}}=\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\lambda^{\prime}(\hat{i}+\hat{k})$$

The point of intersection of $$L_1$$ and $$L_2$$ will satisfy $$\overline{\mathrm{r}}=\overline{\mathrm{r}^{\prime}}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow 3 \hat{i}+\lambda(-\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k})=\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\lambda^{\prime}(\hat{i}+\hat{k}) \\ & \Rightarrow(3-\lambda) \hat{i}+\lambda \hat{j}+\lambda \hat{k}=\left(1+\lambda^{\prime}\right) \hat{i}+\hat{j}+\lambda^{\prime} \hat{k} \\ & \Rightarrow 3-\lambda=1+\lambda^{\prime} \text { and } \lambda=1 \\ & \Rightarrow \lambda=1 \text { and } \lambda^{\prime}=1 \end{aligned}$$

Substituting the value of $$\lambda$$ in (i), we get the point of intersection.

$$\therefore \quad$$ The point of intersection of lines $$\mathrm{L}_1$$ and $$\mathrm{L}_2$$ has p.v. $$2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}$$.

content: The equation of the line passing through the point $$(-1,3,-2)$$ and perpendicular to each of the lines $$\frac{x}{1}=\frac{y}{2}=\frac{z}{3}$$ and $$\frac{x+2}{-3}=\frac{y-1}{2}=\frac{z+1}{5}$$ is
explanation:
Let $$\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}, \mathrm{c}$$ be the direction ratios of the required line. Since the line is perpendicular to the lines with d.r.s. $$1,2,3$$ and $$-3,2,5$$.

$$\therefore \quad \mathrm{a}+2 \mathrm{~b}+3 \mathrm{c}=0$$ .... (i)

and $$-3 a+2 b+5 c=0$$ .... (ii)

$$\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{a}}{2}=\frac{\mathrm{b}}{-7}=\frac{\mathrm{c}}{4}$$ ...[From (i) and (ii)]

$$\therefore \quad$$ Equation of the required line is

$$\frac{x+1}{2}=\frac{y-3}{-7}=\frac{z+2}{4}$$

content: If $$A(1,4,2)$$ and $$C(5,-7,1)$$ are two vertices of triangle $$A B C$$ and $$G\left(\frac{4}{3}, 0, \frac{-2}{3}\right)$$ is centroid of the triangle $$A B C$$, then the mid point of side $$B C$$ is
explanation:
Let $$\mathrm{B} \equiv\left(x_1, y_1, \mathrm{z}_1\right)$$

Co-ordinates of centroid

$$\equiv\left(\frac{1+x_1+5}{3}, \frac{4+y_1-7}{3}, \frac{2+z_1+1}{3}\right)$$

$$\Rightarrow\left(\frac{4}{3}, 0, \frac{-2}{3}\right) \equiv\left(\frac{6+x_1}{3}, \frac{y_1-3}{3}, \frac{3+\mathrm{z}_1}{3}\right)$$

$$\Rightarrow x_1=-2, y_1=3, \mathrm{z}_1=-5$$

$$\therefore \quad \mathrm{B} \equiv(-2,3,-5)$$

Midpoint side $$\mathrm{BC}=\left(\frac{-2+5}{2}, \frac{3-7}{2}, \frac{-5+1}{2}\right)$$

$$\qquad=\left(\frac{3}{2},-2,-2\right)$$

content: The distance of the point $$(-1,-5,-10)$$ from the point of intersection of the line $$\frac{x-2}{3}=\frac{y+1}{4}=\frac{z-2}{12}$$ and the plane $$x-y+z=5$$ is
explanation:
Let $$\frac{x-2}{3}=\frac{y+1}{4}=\frac{z-2}{12}=\lambda$$

$$\therefore \quad$$ The co-ordinates of any point on the line are

$$P \equiv(3 \lambda+2,4 \lambda-1,12 \lambda+2)$$

This point lies on the plane

$$\begin{array}{ll} & x-y+z=5 \\ \therefore \quad & 3 \lambda+2-(4 \lambda-1)+12 \lambda+2=5 \\ & \Rightarrow 11 \lambda=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \lambda=0 \\ \therefore \quad & \mathrm{P} \equiv(2,-1,2) \\ & \text { Let } \mathrm{Q} \equiv(-1,-5,-10) \\ \therefore \quad &\mathrm{PQ}=\sqrt{(-1-2)^2+(-5+1)^2+(-10-2)^2} \\ & =\sqrt{9+16+144} \\ & =13 \text { units } \end{array}$$

content: The equation of the line, passing through $$(1,2,3)$$ and parallel to planes $$x-y+2 z=5$$ and $$3 x+y+z=6$$, is
explanation:
Required equation of line is

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{x-1}{\left|\begin{array}{cc} -1 & 2 \\ 1 & 1 \end{array}\right|}=\frac{y-2}{-\left|\begin{array}{cc} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 1 \end{array}\right|}=\frac{z-3}{\left|\begin{array}{cc} 1 & -1 \\ 3 & 1 \end{array}\right|} \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{x-1}{-3}=\frac{y-2}{5}=\frac{z-3}{4} \\ \end{aligned}$$

content: The shortest distance (in units) between the lines $$\frac{x+1}{3}=\frac{y+2}{1}=\frac{z+1}{2}$$ and $$\bar{r}=(2 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k})+\lambda(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j})$$ is
explanation:
Given lines are: $$\frac{x+1}{3}=\frac{y+2}{1}=\frac{z+1}{2}$$ and $$\frac{x-2}{1}=\frac{y+2}{2}=\frac{z-3}{0}$$

$$\therefore \quad$$ Required distance

$$=\left|\frac{\left|\begin{array}{lll} 3 & 0 & 4 \\ 3 & 1 & 2 \\ 1 & 2 & 0 \end{array}\right|}{\sqrt{(6-1)^2+(0-2)^2+(0-4)^2}}\right|$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\left|\frac{3(0-4)+0+4(6-1)}{\sqrt{25+4+16}}\right| \\ & =\left|\frac{8}{\sqrt{45}}\right| \\ & =\frac{8}{3 \sqrt{5}} \end{aligned}$$

content: The length (in units) of the projection of the line segment, joining the points $$(5,-1,4)$$ and $$(4,-1,3)$$, on the plane $$x+y+z=7$$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \text {Let } A=(5,-1,4), B=(4,-1,3) \\\\ & \overline{\mathrm{AB}}=-\hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}} \Rightarrow \mid \overline{\mathrm{AB}}=\sqrt{2} \end{aligned}$$

Projection of $$\overline{\mathrm{AB}}$$ in the plane $$x+y+z=7$$ is $$|\overline{\mathrm{AB}}| \cos \theta=\left|\overline{\mathrm{A}^{\prime} \mathrm{B}^{\prime}}\right| \cos \theta$$

Direction ratios of normal to the given plane is $$1,1,1$$.

$$\begin{aligned} & \cos \left(90^{\circ}-\theta\right)=\left|\frac{1(-1)+1(0)+1(-1)}{\sqrt{1^2+1^2+1^2} \sqrt{1^2+0^2+1^2}}\right| \\\\ & \Rightarrow \sin \theta=\frac{2}{\sqrt{6}} \Rightarrow \cos \theta=\sqrt{1-\frac{4}{6}}=\sqrt{\frac{1}{3}} \\\\ & \text { Required projection }=|\overline{\mathrm{AB}}| \cos \theta \\\\ & =\sqrt{2} \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}=\sqrt{\frac{2}{3}} \end{aligned}$$

content: If the volume of tetrahedron, whose vertices are $$\mathrm{A}(1,2,3), \mathrm{B}(-3,-1,1), \mathrm{C}(2,1,3)$$ and $$D(-1,2, x)$$ is $$\frac{11}{6}$$ cubic units, then the value of $$x$$ is
explanation: $$\begin{array}{ll} & \overline{\mathrm{AB}}=-4 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ & \overline{\mathrm{AC}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}} \\ & \overline{\mathrm{AD}}=-2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+(x-3) \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ & \text { Volume }=\frac{1}{6}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} -4 & -3 & -2 \\ 1 & -1 & 0 \\ -2 & 0 & x-3 \end{array}\right| \\ & 11=4(x-3)+3(x-3)-2(-2) \\ \therefore \quad & 11=7 x-17 \\ \therefore \quad & x=4 \end{array}$$

content: Equation of plane containing the line $$\frac{x}{2}=\frac{y}{3}=\frac{z}{4}$$ and perpendicular to the plane containing the lines $$\frac{x}{3}=\frac{y}{4}=\frac{z}{2}$$ and $$\frac{x}{4}=\frac{y}{2}=\frac{z}{3}$$ is
explanation:
Equation of the plane containing $$\frac{x}{3}=\frac{y}{4}=\frac{z}{2}$$ and $$\frac{x}{4}=\frac{y}{2}=\frac{z}{3}$$ is

$$\begin{aligned} & \left|\begin{array}{lll} x & y & z \\ 3 & 4 & 2 \\ 4 & 2 & 3 \end{array}\right|=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 8 x-y-10 z=0 \end{aligned}$$

Now required plane is perpendicular to this plane.

Consider option (C)

$$(8)(1)+(-1)(-2)+(-10)(1)=0$$

$$\therefore \quad$$ Option $$(\mathrm{C})$$ is correct.

content: The centroid of tetrahedron with vertices at $$\mathrm{A}(-1,2,3), \mathrm{B}(3,-2,1), \mathrm{C}(2,1,3)$$ and $$\mathrm{D}(-1,-2,4)$$ is
explanation:
Centroid of tetrahedron

$$\begin{aligned} & \equiv\left(\frac{-1+3+2-1}{4}, \frac{2-2+1-2}{4}, \frac{3+1+3+4}{4}\right) \\ & \equiv\left(\frac{3}{4}, \frac{-1}{4}, \frac{11}{4}\right) \end{aligned}$$

content: A plane is parallel to two lines whose direction ratios are $$1,0,-1$$ and $$-1,1,0$$ and it contains the point $$(1,1,1)$$. If it cuts the co-ordinate axes at $$\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}, \mathrm{C}$$, then the volume of the tetrahedron $$\mathrm{OABC}$$ (in cubic units) is
explanation:
Equation of the plane passing through $$(1,1,1)$$ is given as

$$\mathrm{a}(x-1)+\mathrm{b}(y-1)+\mathrm{c}(\mathrm{z}-1)=0$$ .... (i)

As the plane is parallel to the lines having direction ratios $$1,0,-1$$ and $$-1,1,0$$, we get $$\mathrm{a}-\mathrm{c}=0$$ and $$-\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}=0$$

$$\Rightarrow \mathrm{a}=\mathrm{b}=\mathrm{c}$$ ..... (ii)

$$\therefore \quad$$ From (i) and (ii), we get

$$\begin{aligned} & x-1+y-1+z-1=0 \\ & \therefore x+y+z=3 \quad \Rightarrow \frac{x}{3}+\frac{y}{3}+\frac{z}{3}=1 \\ \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore \quad$$ Co-ordinates of A, B, C are $$(3,0,0),(0,3,0)$$ and $$(0,0,3)$$ respectively.

$$\therefore \quad$$ Volume of tetrahedron $$\mathrm{OABC}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{1}{6}\left|\begin{array}{lll} 3 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 3 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 3 \end{array}\right| \\ & =\frac{1}{6} \times 27 \\ & =\frac{9}{2} \text { cu. units } \end{aligned}$$
