content: The equation of the plane through $$(-1,1,2)$$ whose normal makes equal acute angles with co-ordinate axes is
explanation:
Note that $$(-1,1,2)$$ is satisfied by only option (B)

Alternate Method:

Let $$\mathrm{A} \equiv(-1,1,2)$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \overline{\mathrm{a}}=-\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ & \qquad\overline{\mathrm{n}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore \quad$$ equation of plane is $$\overline{\mathrm{r}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{n}}=\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{n}}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \bar{r} \cdot(\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k})=(-\hat{i}+\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}) \cdot(\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}) \\ & \Rightarrow \bar{r} \cdot(\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k})=2 \\ & \Rightarrow(x \hat{i}+y \hat{j}+z \hat{k}) \cdot(\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k})=2 \\ & \Rightarrow x+y+z-2=0 \end{aligned}$$

content: The distance of the point $$\mathrm{P}(-2,4,-5)$$ from the line $$\frac{x+3}{3}=\frac{y-4}{5}=\frac{z+8}{6}$$ is
explanation:
Since the point is $$(-2,4,-5)$$,

$$\therefore \quad \mathrm{a}=-2, \mathrm{~b}=4, \mathrm{c}=-5$$

Given equation of line is

$$\frac{x+3}{3}=\frac{y-4}{5}=\frac{z+8}{6}$$

$$x_1=-3, y_1=4, z_1=-8$$

d.r.s of the line are $$3,5,6$$

d.c.s are $$\frac{3}{\sqrt{70}}, \frac{5}{\sqrt{70}}, \frac{6}{\sqrt{70}}$$

Perpendicular distance of point from the line is

$$\begin{aligned} & \sqrt{\begin{array}{r} {\left[\left(\mathrm{a}-x_1\right)^2+\left(\mathrm{b}-y_1\right)^2+\left(\mathrm{c}-\mathrm{z}_1\right)^2\right]} \\ -\left[\left(\mathrm{a}-x_1\right) l+\left(\mathrm{b}-y_1\right) \mathrm{m}+\left(\mathrm{c}-\mathrm{z}_1\right) \mathrm{n}\right]^2 \end{array}} \\ & =\sqrt{1^2+0+3^2-\left[\frac{1(3)}{\sqrt{70}}+\frac{0(5)}{\sqrt{70}}+\frac{3(6)}{\sqrt{70}}\right]^2} \\ & =\sqrt{1+9-\left(\frac{3}{\sqrt{70}}+\frac{18}{\sqrt{70}}\right)^2} \\ & =\sqrt{\frac{37}{10}} \text {.units } \end{aligned}$$

content: If the line $$\frac{1-x}{3}=\frac{7 y-14}{2 p}=\frac{z-3}{2}$$ and $$\frac{7-7 x}{3 \mathrm{p}}=\frac{y-5}{1}=\frac{6-\mathrm{z}}{5}$$ are at right angles, then $$\mathrm{p}=$$
explanation:
Given lines can be written as

$$\frac{x-1}{-3}=\frac{y-2}{\frac{2 p}{7}}=\frac{z-3}{2} \text { and } \frac{x-1}{-\frac{3 p}{7}}=\frac{y-5}{1}=\frac{z-6}{-5}$$

As these lines are at right angles, we get

$$(-3)\left(-\frac{3 p}{7}\right)+\left(\frac{2 p}{7}\right)(1)+(2)(-5)=0$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \frac{9 p}{7}+\frac{2 p}{7}-10=0 \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{11 p}{7}=10 \Rightarrow p=\frac{70}{11} \end{aligned}$$

content: If the lines $\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y+1}{3}=\frac{z-1}{4}$ and $x-3=\frac{y-\mathrm{k}}{2}=\mathrm{z}$ intersect, then the value of $\mathrm{k}$ is
explanation:
As the given lines are intersecting, the shortest distance between them is zero.

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad\left|\begin{array}{ccc} x_2-x_1 & y_2-y_1 & \mathrm{z}_2-\mathrm{z}_1 \\ \mathrm{a}_1 & \mathrm{~b}_1 & \mathrm{c}_1 \\ \mathrm{a}_2 & \mathrm{~b}_2 & \mathrm{c}_2 \end{array}\right|=0 \\ & \therefore \quad\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 3-1 & \mathrm{k}+1 & 0-1 \\ 2 & 3 & 4 \\ 1 & 2 & 1 \end{array}\right|=0 \\ & \therefore \quad\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 2 & \mathrm{k}+1 & -1 \\ 2 & 3 & 4 \\ 1 & 2 & 1 \end{array}\right|=0 \\ & \therefore \quad \mathrm{k}=\frac{9}{2} \end{aligned}$$

content: If the line $$\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y+1}{3}=\frac{z-2}{4}$$ meets the plane $$x+2 y+3 z=15$$ at the point $$P$$, then the distance of $$\mathrm{P}$$ from the origin is
explanation:
Let $$\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y+1}{3}=\frac{z-2}{4}=\mathrm{k}$$ (say)

Let $$\mathrm{P}$$ be the any point on the above line.

$$\therefore \quad \mathrm{P} \equiv(2 \mathrm{k}+1,3 \mathrm{k}-1,4 \mathrm{k}+2)$$

The point $$\mathrm{P}$$ lies on the plane

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & 2 k+1+2(3 k-1)+3(4 k+2)=15 \\ \therefore & 2 k+1+6 k-2+12 k+6=15 \\ \therefore & 20 k=10 \\ \therefore & k=\frac{1}{2} \\ \therefore & P \equiv\left(2, \frac{1}{2}, 4\right) \end{array}$$

Distance of $$\mathrm{P}$$ from origin is

$$\sqrt{2^2+\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^2+4^2}=\sqrt{\frac{81}{4}}=\frac{9}{2}$$

content: The equation of line passing through the point $$(1,2,3)$$ and perpendicular to the lines $$\frac{x-2}{3}=\frac{y-1}{2}=\frac{z+1}{-2}$$ and $$\frac{x}{2}=\frac{y}{-3}=\frac{z}{1}$$ is
explanation:
Required line is perpendicular to the lines $$\frac{x-2}{3}=\frac{y-1}{2}=\frac{z+1}{-2}$$ and $$\frac{x}{2}=\frac{y}{-3}=\frac{z}{1}$$

$$\therefore \quad$$ Required line is parallel to vector

$$\bar{b}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 3 & 2 & -2 \\ 2 & -3 & 1 \end{array}\right|=-4 \hat{i}-7 \hat{j}-13 \hat{k}$$

$$\therefore \quad$$

The equation of the required line is $$(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k})+\lambda(-4 \hat{i}-7 \hat{j}-13 \hat{k})$$

content: The angle between the line $$\frac{x+1}{2}=\frac{y-2}{1}=\frac{z-3}{-2}$$ and plane $$x-2 y-\lambda z=3$$ is $$\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 \sqrt{2}}{3}\right)$$, then value of $$\lambda$$ is
explanation:
The acute angle $$\theta$$ between line $$\bar{a}+\lambda \bar{b}$$ and the plane $$\overline{\mathrm{r}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{n}}=\mathrm{p}$$ is given by

$$\sin \theta=\left|\frac{\overline{\mathrm{b}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{n}}}{|\overline{\mathrm{b}}| \cdot|\overline{\mathrm{n}}|}\right|$$ ..... (i)

Here, $$\bar{b}=2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-2 \hat{k}$$ and $$\bar{n}=\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}-\lambda \hat{k}$$

Also, $$\theta=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 \sqrt{2}}{3}\right)=\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \text { (i) } \Rightarrow \sin \left(\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)\right)=\left|\frac{2-2+2 \lambda}{\sqrt{4+1+4} \sqrt{1+4+\lambda^2}}\right| \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{1}{3}=\left|\frac{2 \lambda}{3 \sqrt{5+\lambda^2}}\right| \\ & \Rightarrow 5+\lambda^2=4 \lambda^2 \\ & \Rightarrow \lambda^2=\frac{5}{3} \\ & \Rightarrow \lambda=\sqrt{\frac{5}{3}} \end{aligned}$$

content: If the direction cosines $$l, \mathrm{~m}, \mathrm{n}$$ of two lines are connected by relations $$l-5 \mathrm{~m}+3 \mathrm{n}=0$$ and $$7 l^2+5 \mathrm{~m}^2-3 \mathrm{n}^2=0$$, then value of $$l+\mathrm{m}+\mathrm{n}$$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & l-5 \mathrm{~m}+3 \mathrm{n}=0 \text { and } 7 l^2+5 \mathrm{~m}^2-3 \mathrm{n}^2=0 \\ & \Rightarrow l=5 \mathrm{~m}-3 \mathrm{n} \text { and } 7 l^2=3 \mathrm{n}^2-5 \mathrm{~m}^2 \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow l=5 m-3 n \text { and } 7 l^2=3 n^2-5 m^2 \\ & \text { Putting } l=(5 m-3 n) \text { in } 7 l^2=3 n^2-5 m^2 \text {, we } \\ & 7(5 m-3 n)^2=3 n^2-5 m^2 \\ & \Rightarrow 7\left(25 m^2-30 m n+9 n^2\right)=3 n^2-5 m^2 \\ & \Rightarrow 180 m^2-210 m n+60 n^2=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 6 m^2-7 m n+2 n^2=0 \\ & \Rightarrow(3 m-2 n)(2 m-n)=0 \end{aligned}$$

$$\Rightarrow 3 \mathrm{~m}=2 \mathrm{n} \text { or } 2 \mathrm{~m}=\mathrm{n}\quad$$ ..... (i)

If $$3 \mathrm{~m}=2 \mathrm{n}$$, then $$l=\frac{\mathrm{n}}{3}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{m}}{2}=\frac{\mathrm{n}}{3}=\frac{l}{1}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{14}} \\ & \therefore \quad l+\mathrm{m}+\mathrm{n}=\frac{6}{\sqrt{14}} \end{aligned}$$

If $$2 \mathrm{~m}=\mathrm{n}$$, then $$l=\frac{-\mathrm{n}}{2}$$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \frac{\mathrm{m}}{1}=\frac{\mathrm{n}}{2}=\frac{l}{-1}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{6}} \\ \therefore & l+\mathrm{m}+\mathrm{n}=\frac{2}{\sqrt{6}} \end{array}$$

$$\therefore \quad$$ The possible values of $$l+\mathrm{m}+\mathrm{n}$$ is $$\frac{2}{\sqrt{6}}$$ or $$\frac{6}{\sqrt{14}}$$

content: The mirror image of the point $$(1,2,3)$$ in a plane is $$\left(-\frac{7}{3},-\frac{4}{3},-\frac{1}{3}\right)$$. Thus, the point _________ lies on this plane.
explanation:
$$\mathrm{M}$$ is the midpoint.

$$\therefore \quad \mathrm{M} \equiv\left(-\frac{2}{3}, \frac{1}{3}, \frac{4}{3}\right)$$

D.r.s of $$A B$$ are $$\frac{-10}{3}, \frac{-10}{3}, \frac{-10}{3}$$ i.e., $$1,1,1$$

Equation of plane is

$$\begin{aligned} & 1\left(x+\frac{2}{3}\right)+1\left(y-\frac{1}{3}\right)+1\left(z-\frac{4}{3}\right)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow x+y+z=1 \end{aligned}$$

Option (A) satisfies this equation of the plane.

content: A plane is parallel to two lines, whose direction ratios are $$1,0,-1$$ and $$-1,1,0$$ and it contains the point $$(1,1,1)$$. If it cuts co-ordinate axes $$(\mathrm{X}, \mathrm{Y}, \mathrm{Z}$$ - axes resp.) at $$\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}, \mathrm{C}$$, then the volume of the tetrahedron $$\mathrm{OABC}$$ is _________ cu. units.
explanation:
Equation of the plane passing through $$(1,1,1)$$ is given as

$$\mathrm{a}(x-1)+\mathrm{b}(y-1)+\mathrm{c}(\mathrm{z}-1)=0$$ .... (i)

As the plane is parallel to the lines having direction ratios $$1,0,-1$$ and $$-1,1,0$$, we get

$$\mathrm{a}-\mathrm{c}=0 \text { and }-\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}=0$$

$$\Rightarrow \mathrm{a}=\mathrm{b}=\mathrm{c}$$ .... (ii)

$$\therefore \quad$$ From (i) and (ii), we get

$$\begin{aligned} & x \quad x-1+y-1+z-1=0 \\ & \therefore \quad x+y+z=3 \quad \Rightarrow \frac{x}{3}+\frac{y}{3}+\frac{z}{3}=1 \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore \quad$$ Co-ordinates of A, B, C are $$(3,0,0),(0,3,0)$$ and $$(0,0,3)$$ respectively.

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \text { Volume of tetrahedron } \mathrm{OABC} & =\frac{1}{6}\left|\begin{array}{lll} 3 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 3 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 3 \end{array}\right| \\ & =\frac{1}{6} \times 27 \\ & =\frac{9}{2} \text { cu. units } \end{aligned}$$

content: The lines $$\frac{x-1}{3}=\frac{y+1}{2}=\frac{z-1}{5} \quad$$ and $$\frac{x+2}{4}=\frac{y-1}{3}=\frac{z+1}{2}$$

options.content: intersect each other and point of intersection is $$(2,1,3)$$
intersect each other and point of intersection is $$(3,2,4)$$
intersect each other and point of intersection is $$(-2,3,3)$$

explanation:
The given lines are $$\frac{x-1}{3}=\frac{y+1}{2}=\frac{z-1}{5}$$ and $$\frac{x+2}{4}=\frac{y-1}{3}=\frac{z+1}{2}$$

Here,

$$\begin{aligned} \left(x_1, y_1, z_1\right) & \equiv(1,-1,1) \\ \left(x_2, y_2, z_2\right) & \equiv(-2,1,-1) \\ \left(a_1, b_1, c_1\right) & \equiv(3,2,5) \\ \left(a_2, b_2, c_2\right) & \equiv(4,3,2) \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Consider }\left|\begin{array}{ccc} x_2-x_1 & y_2-y_1 & z_2-z_1 \\ a_1 & b_1 & c_1 \\ a_2 & b_2 & c_2 \end{array}\right| \\ & =\left|\begin{array}{ccc} -3 & 2 & -2 \\ 3 & 2 & 5 \\ 4 & 3 & 2 \end{array}\right| \\ & =-3(-11)-2(-14)-2(1) \\ & =33+28-2 \\ & =59 \neq 0 \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore$$ The lines are not intersecting.

content: The vector equation of the line $$2 x+4=3 y+1=6 z-3$$ is
explanation:
The equation of line is

$$\begin{aligned} & 2 x+4=3 y+1=6 z-3 \\ & \Rightarrow 2(x+2)=3\left(y+\frac{1}{3}\right)=6\left(z-\frac{1}{2}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{x+2}{\frac{1}{2}}=\frac{y+\frac{1}{3}}{\frac{1}{3}}=\frac{z-\frac{1}{2}}{\frac{1}{6}} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{x+2}{3}=\frac{y+\frac{1}{3}}{2}=\frac{z-\frac{1}{2}}{1} \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore \quad$$ The given line passes through $$\left(-2,-\frac{1}{3}, \frac{1}{2}\right)$$ and has direction ratios proportional to $$3,2,1$$.

$$\therefore \quad$$ Vector equation of the line is

$$\bar{r}=\left(-2 \hat{i}-\frac{1}{3} \hat{j}+\frac{1}{2} \hat{k}\right)+\lambda(3 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+\hat{k})$$

content: The plane through the intersection of planes $$x+y+z=1$$ and $$2 x+3 y-z+4=0$$ and parallel to $$\mathrm{Y}$$-axis also passes through the point
explanation:
Equation of plane through the intersection of given planes is

$$\begin{aligned} & (x+y+z-1)+\lambda(2 x+3 y-z+4)=0 \quad \ldots \text { (i) } \\ & \Rightarrow(1+2 \lambda) x+(1+3 \lambda) y+(1-\lambda) z+4 \lambda-1=0 \end{aligned}$$

Since the plane is parallel to $$\mathrm{Y}$$-axis.

$$\begin{array}{r} \therefore \quad 1+3 \lambda=0 \\ \Rightarrow \lambda=\frac{-1}{3} \end{array}$$

Substituting $$\lambda=\frac{-1}{3}$$ in (i), we get

$$\begin{aligned} & (x+y+z-1)-\frac{1}{3}(2 x+3 y-z+4)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow x+4 z-7=0 \end{aligned}$$

Point $$(3,2,1)$$ satisfies this equation.

content: The perpendicular distance of the origin from the plane $$x-3 y+4 z-6=0$$ is
explanation:
Length of perpendicular from point $$O(0,0,0)$$ to plane $$x-3 y+4 z-6=0$$ is given by

$$\begin{aligned} d & =\left|\frac{0(1)+0(-3)+0(4)-6}{\sqrt{(1)^2+(-3)^2+(4)^2}}\right| \\ & =\left|\frac{-6}{\sqrt{1+9+16}}\right|=\frac{6}{\sqrt{26}} \end{aligned}$$

content: Two lines $$\frac{x-3}{1}=\frac{y+1}{3}=\frac{z-6}{-1}$$ and $$\frac{x+5}{7}=\frac{y-2}{-6}=\frac{z-3}{4} \quad$$ intersect at the point R. Then reflection of $$\mathrm{R}$$ in the $$x y$$-plane has co-ordinates
explanation:
Let $$\frac{x-3}{1}=\frac{y+1}{3}=\frac{z-6}{-1}=\lambda$$

$$\Rightarrow x=3+\lambda, y=3 \lambda-1, \mathrm{z}=-\lambda+6$$

Let $$\frac{x+5}{7}=\frac{y-2}{-6}=\frac{z-3}{4}=\mu$$

$$\Rightarrow x=7 \mu-5, y=-6 \mu+2, z=4 \mu+3$$

Both the given lines intersect each other.

So, $$\lambda+3=7 \mu-5$$

$$\Rightarrow 7 \mu-\lambda=8$$ .... (i)

Also, $$3 \lambda-1=-6 \mu+2$$

$$\Rightarrow 6 \mu+3 \lambda=3$$ .... (ii)

From (i) and (ii), we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \mu=1, \lambda=-1 \\ & \text { i.e., } x=2, y=-4, z=7 \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore \quad$$ Co-ordinates of the intersection of the given lines are $$R(2,-4,7)$$

Hence, reflection of $$\mathrm{R}$$ in the $$x y$$-plane is $$(2,-4,-7)$$.

content: The line $$\frac{x-2}{3}=\frac{y-1}{-5}=\frac{z+2}{2}$$ lies in the plane $$x+3 y-\alpha z+\beta=0$$, then the value of $$\alpha^2+\alpha \beta+\beta^2$$ is
explanation:
Line $$\frac{x-2}{3}=\frac{y-1}{-5}=\frac{z+2}{2}$$ lies in the plane $$x+3 y-\alpha z+\beta=0$$

The direction ratios of the line are $$3,-5,2$$.

The direction ratios of the normal to the plane are $$1,3,-\alpha$$.

The given line is perpendicular to the normal of plane.

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad & 3(1)+(-5)(3)+2(-\alpha)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 3-15-2 \alpha=0 \\ & \Rightarrow-12-2 \alpha=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \alpha=-6 \end{aligned}$$

Also, point $$(2,1,-2)$$ lies on the plane

$$\begin{aligned} & x+3 y-\alpha z+\beta=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 2+3-(-6)(-2)+\beta=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 2+3-12+\beta=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \beta=7 \\ \therefore \quad & \alpha^2+\alpha \beta+\beta^2=36-42+49=43 \end{aligned}$$

content: Let $$\mathrm{P}$$ be a plane passing through the points $$(2,1,0),(4,1,1)$$ and $$(5,0,1)$$ and $$R$$ be the point $$(2,1,6)$$. Then image of $$R$$ in the plane $$P$$ is
explanation:
Equation of the plane passing through $$(2,1,0), (4,1,1)$$ and $$(5,0,1)$$ is

$$\begin{aligned} & \left|\begin{array}{lll} x-2 & y-1 & z-0 \\ 4-2 & 1-1 & 1-0 \\ 5-2 & 0-1 & 1-0 \end{array}\right|=0 \\ & \Rightarrow x+y-2 z=3 \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{array}{ll} & \mathrm{R}^{\prime}(x, y, \mathrm{z}) \text { is image of } \mathrm{R}(2,1,6) \text { w.r.t. to plane } \\ & x+y-2 \mathrm{z}=3 \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{x-2}{1}=\frac{y-1}{1}=\frac{\mathrm{z}-6}{-2}=\frac{-2[2+1-2(6)-3]}{1+1+4} \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{x-2}{1}=\frac{y-1}{1}=\frac{\mathrm{z}-6}{-2}=4 \\ \Rightarrow & x=6, y=5, \mathrm{z}=-2 \\ \therefore \quad & \mathrm{R}^{\prime}(x, y, \mathrm{z}) \equiv(6,5,-2) \end{array}$$

content: The co-ordinates of the point, where the line through $$A(3,4,1)$$ and $$B(5,1,6)$$ crosses the $$\mathrm{XZ}$$-plane, are
explanation:
Let $$\mathrm{A}\left(x_1, y_1, \mathrm{z}_1\right)=\mathrm{A}(3,4,1)$$ and $$\mathrm{B}\left(x_2, y_2, \mathrm{z}_2\right)=\mathrm{B}(5,1,6)$$

The equation of the line passing through the points $$\left(x_1, y_1, z_1\right)$$ and $$\left(x_2, y_2, z_2\right)$$ is given by

$$\frac{x-x_1}{x_2-x_1}=\frac{y-y_1}{y_2-y_1}=\frac{z-z_1}{z_2-z_1}$$

$$\therefore \quad \frac{x-3}{5-3}=\frac{y-4}{1-4}=\frac{z-1}{6-1}$$

$$\therefore \quad \frac{x-3}{2}=\frac{y-4}{-3}=\frac{z-1}{5}$$

Since the line crosses the $$\mathrm{XZ}$$ plane, $$y=0$$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \frac{x-3}{2}=\frac{4}{3}=\frac{z-1}{5} \\ \therefore & \frac{x-3}{2}=\frac{4}{3} \text { and } \frac{z-1}{5}=\frac{4}{3} \\ & \Rightarrow x=\frac{17}{3} \text { and } z=\frac{23}{3} \end{array}$$

$$\therefore \quad$$ The required point is $$\left(\frac{17}{3}, 0, \frac{23}{3}\right)$$.

content: $$\mathrm{ABC}$$ is a triangle in a plane with vertices $$\mathrm{A}(2,3,5), \mathrm{B}(-1,3,2)$$ and $$\mathrm{C}(\lambda, 5, \mu)$$. If median through $$\mathrm{A}$$ is equally inclined to the co-ordinate axes, then value of $$\lambda+\mu$$ is
explanation:
Let $$A D$$ be the median

$$\therefore \quad$$ Co-ordinates of

$$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{D} \equiv\left(\frac{x_1+x_2}{2}, \frac{y_1+y_2}{2}, \frac{z_1+z_2}{2}\right) \\ & \mathrm{D} \equiv\left(\frac{\lambda-1}{2}, 4, \frac{\mu+2}{2}\right) \\ \therefore \quad & \overline{\mathrm{AD}}=\left(\frac{\lambda-1}{2}-2\right) \hat{\mathrm{i}}+(4-3) \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\left(\frac{\mu+2}{2}-5\right) \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ \therefore \quad & \overline{\mathrm{AD}}=\left(\frac{\lambda-5}{2}\right) \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\left(\frac{\mu-8}{2}\right) \hat{\mathrm{k}} \end{aligned}$$

Since $$A D$$ makes equal angle with co-ordinate axes, the direction ratios are equal.

$$\therefore \quad \frac{\lambda-5}{2}=1=\frac{\mu-8}{2}$$

Consider,

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{\lambda-5}{2}=1 \\ & \Rightarrow \lambda-5=2 \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \lambda=7 \\ & \text { and } \frac{\mu-8}{2}=1 \\ & \Rightarrow \mu-8=2 \\ & \Rightarrow \mu=10 \\ \therefore \quad & \lambda+\mu=7+10=17 \end{aligned}$$

content: If a line $$\mathrm{L}$$ is the line of intersection of the planes $$2 x+3 y+z=1$$ and $$x+3 y+2 z=2$$. If line $$\mathrm{L}$$ makes an angle $$\alpha$$ with the positive $$\mathrm{X}$$-axis, then the value of $$\sec \alpha$$ is
explanation:
Given equations:

$$\begin{aligned} & 2 x+3 y+z=1 \\ & 2 x+3 y=1-z \quad \text{...... (i)}\\ & x+3 y+2 z=2 \\ & x+3 y=2-2 z \quad \text{...... (ii)} \end{aligned}$$

Subtracting (ii) from (i), we get

$$\begin{aligned} & 2 x+3 y-x-3 y=1-z-2+2 z \\ & x=-1+z \\ & z=\frac{x+1}{1} \quad \text{...... (iii)} \end{aligned}$$

Putting value of $$x$$ in equation (ii), we get

$$\begin{aligned} & -1+z+3 y=2-2 z \\ & 3 z=3-3 y \\ & z=\frac{y-1}{-1} \quad \text{...... (iv)} \end{aligned}$$

From (iii), (iv)

$$\frac{x+1}{1}=\frac{y-1}{-1}=\frac{z}{1}$$

Thus, angle between above line and $$\mathrm{X}$$-axis having Direction Ratio's $$(1,0,0)$$ is given as

$$\begin{aligned} \cos \alpha & =\left|\frac{1 \cdot(1)+0+0}{\sqrt{1+1+1} \cdot \sqrt{1}}\right|=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \\ \therefore \quad \sec \alpha & =\sqrt{3} \end{aligned}$$

content: The shortest distance between the lines $$\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y-2}{3}=\frac{z-3}{4}$$ and $$\frac{x-2}{3}=\frac{y-4}{4}=\frac{z-5}{5}$$ is
explanation:
The lines arě

$$\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y-2}{3}=\frac{z-3}{4} \text { and } \frac{x-2}{3}=\frac{y-4}{4}=\frac{z-5}{5}$$

Comparing equations with

$$\frac{x-x_1}{\mathrm{a}_1}=\frac{y-y_1}{\mathrm{~b}_1}=\frac{\mathrm{z}-\mathrm{z}_1}{\mathrm{c}_1} \text { and } \frac{x-x_2}{\mathrm{a}_2}=\frac{y-y_2}{\mathrm{~b}_2}=\frac{\mathrm{z}-\mathrm{z}_2}{\mathrm{c}_2} \text {, }$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { we get } \\ & x_1=1, y_1=2, \mathrm{z}_1=3 \quad x_2=2, y_2=4, \mathrm{z}_2=5 \\ & \mathrm{a}_1=2, \mathrm{~b}_1=3, \mathrm{c}_1=4 \quad \mathrm{a}_2=3, \mathrm{~b}_2=4, \mathrm{c}_2=5 \\ & \left|\begin{array}{ccc} x_2-x_1 \cdot & y_2-y_1 & \mathrm{z}_2-\mathrm{z}_1 \\ \mathrm{a}_1 & \mathrm{~b}_1 & \mathrm{c}_1 \\ \mathrm{a}_2 & \mathrm{~b}_2 & \mathrm{c}_2 \end{array}\right|=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 2 & 2 \\ 2 & 3 & 4 \\ 3 & 4 & 5 \end{array}\right| \\ & =1(15-16)-2(10-12)+2(8-9) \\ & =1 \\ & \therefore \quad \sqrt{\left(a_1 b_2-a_2 b_1\right)^2+\left(b_1 c_2-b_2 c_1\right)^2+\left(c_1 a_2-c_2 a_1\right)^2} \\ & =\sqrt{(2 \times 4-3 \times 3)^2+(3 \times 5-4 \times 4)^2+(4 \times 3-5 \times 2)^2} \\ & =\sqrt{1+1+4} \\ & =\sqrt{6} \\ \end{aligned}$$

Shortest distance between line is $$\mathrm{d}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \mathrm{d} & =\frac{\left|\begin{array}{ccc} x_2-x_1 & y_2-y_1 & z_2-z_1 \\ a_1 & b_1 & c_1 \\ a_2 & b_2 & c_2 \end{array}\right|}{\sqrt{\left(a_1 b_2-a_2 b_1\right)^2+\left(b_1 c_2-b_2 c_1\right)^2+\sqrt{\left(c_1 a_2-c_2 a_1\right)^2}}} \\ & =\frac{1}{\sqrt{6}} \end{aligned}$$

content: The co-ordinates of the point, where the line $$\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y-2}{-3}=\frac{z+5}{4}$$ meets the plane $$2 x+4 y-\mathrm{z}=3$$, are
explanation:
Given line is

$$\begin{array}{ll} & \frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y-2}{-3}=\frac{z+5}{4} \\ & \text { Let } \frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y-2}{-3}=\frac{z+5}{4}=\lambda \\ \therefore \quad & x-1=2 \lambda, y-2=-3 \lambda, z+5=4 \lambda \\ & x=2 \lambda+1, y=-3 \lambda+2, z=4 \lambda-5 \\ \therefore \quad & 2 x+4 y-z=3 \\ & \Rightarrow 2(2 \lambda+1)+4(-3 \lambda+2)-(4 \lambda-5)=3 \\ & \Rightarrow 4 \lambda+2-12 \lambda+8-4 \lambda+5=3 \\ & \Rightarrow-12 \lambda=3-15 \\ \therefore \quad & x=3, y=-1, z=-1, \\ \therefore \quad & \text { Required co-ordinates are: }(3,-1,-1) \end{array}$$

content: The equation of a plane, containing the line of intersection of the planes $$2 x-y-4=0$$ and $$y+2 z-4=0$$ and passing through the point $$(2,1,0)$$, is
explanation:
Equation of plane passing through the intersection of given planes is

$$2 x-y-4+\lambda(y+2 z-4)=0$$ .... (i)

Since, the plane passes through $$(2,1,0)$$

$$\begin{aligned} & 2(2)-1-4+\lambda(1+2(0)-4)=0 \\ & 4-1-4-3 \lambda=0 \\ & -1-3 \lambda=0 \\ & \lambda=\frac{-1}{3} \end{aligned}$$

Substituting $$\lambda=\frac{-1}{3}$$ in equation (i), we get

$$\begin{aligned} & 2 x-y-4-\frac{1}{3}(y+2 z-4)=0 \\ & 6 x-3 y-12-y-2 z+4=0 \\ & 6 x-4 y-2 z-8=0 \\ & 3 x-2 y-z=4 \end{aligned}$$

content: The foot of the perpendicular drawn from the origin to the plane is $$(4,-2,5)$$, then the Cartesian equation of the plane is
explanation:
Substitute $$x=4, y=-2, \mathrm{z}=5$$ in all options.

$$\therefore \quad$$ For option $$\mathrm{A}$$

$$\begin{aligned} 4 x-2 y+5 z & =4(4)-2(-2)+5(5) \\ & =16+4+25 \\ & =45 \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore \quad$$ Only option A is satisfied by $$(4,-2,5)$$

content: A vector $$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}$$ is inclined to $$\mathrm{X}$$-axis at $$45^{\circ}$$, $$\mathrm{Y}$$-axis at $$60^{\circ}$$ and at an acute angle to Z-axis If $$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}$$ is normal to a plane passing through the point $$(-\sqrt{2}, 1,1)$$, then equation of the plane is
explanation:
Let $$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}$$ be inclined at angles $$\alpha, \beta, \gamma$$ to $$\mathrm{X}, \mathrm{Y}, \mathrm{Z}$$ axes respectively.

$$\begin{array}{ll} & \alpha=45^{\circ}, \beta=60^{\circ}, \gamma=? \\ & \cos ^2 \alpha+\cos ^2 \beta+\cos ^2 \gamma=1 \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{4}+\cos ^2 \gamma=1 \\ \therefore \quad & \cos ^2 \gamma=\frac{1}{4} \\ \therefore \quad & \gamma=60^{\circ} \\ & \vec{n}=\cos \alpha \hat{i}+\cos \beta \hat{j}+\cos \gamma \hat{k} \\ \therefore \quad & \vec{n}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \hat{i}+\frac{1}{2} \hat{j}+\frac{1}{2} \hat{k} \end{array}$$

$$\therefore \quad$$ Equation of the required plane is

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(x+\sqrt{2})+\frac{1}{2}(y-1)+\frac{1}{2}(z-1)=0$$

i.e., $$\sqrt{2} x+y+z=0$$

content: If the Cartesian equation of a line is $$6 x-2=3 y+1=2 z-2$$, then the vector equation of the line is
explanation:
Given Cartesian equation of the line is

$$\begin{aligned} & 6 x-2=3 y+1=2 z-2 \\ & \Rightarrow 6\left(x-\frac{1}{3}\right)=3\left(y+\frac{1}{3}\right)=2(z-1) \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow & \frac{x-\frac{1}{3}}{\frac{1}{6}}=\frac{y+\frac{1}{3}}{\frac{1}{3}}=\frac{z-1}{\frac{1}{2}} \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{x-\frac{1}{3}}{1}=\frac{y+\frac{1}{3}}{2}=\frac{z-1}{3} \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore \quad$$ The given line passes through $$\left(\frac{1}{3}, \frac{-1}{3}, 1\right)$$ and has direction ratios proportional to $$1,2,3$$.

$$\therefore \quad$$ Vector equation is

$$\overline{\mathrm{r}}=\left(\frac{1}{3} \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\frac{1}{3} \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}\right)+\lambda(\hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}})$$

content:
The distance between parallel lines

$$\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y-2}{-2}=\frac{z-3}{1}$$ and

$$\frac{x}{2}=\frac{y}{-2}=\frac{z}{1}$$ is :
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & L_1: \frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y-2}{-2}=\frac{z-3}{1} \\ & L_2=\frac{x-0}{2}=\frac{y-0}{-2}=\frac{z-0}{1} \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore$$ their vector equations are

$$\begin{aligned} & \bar{r}=\bar{a}_1+m \bar{b} \text { and } \bar{r}=\bar{a}_2+n \bar{b}, \text { where } \\ & \bar{a}_1 \equiv(1,2,3), \bar{a}_2 \equiv(0,0,0), \bar{b} \equiv(2,-2,1) \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore\left(\bar{a}_1-\bar{a}_2\right) \times \bar{b}=\left|\begin{array}{rrr} \hat{\boldsymbol{i}} & \hat{\boldsymbol{j}} & \hat{\boldsymbol{k}} \\ -1 & -2 & -3 \\ 2 & -2 & 1 \end{array}\right| \equiv(-8,-5,6) \\ & \therefore\left|\left(\bar{a}_2-\bar{a}_1\right) \times \bar{b}\right|=\sqrt{64+25+36}=5 \sqrt{5} \\ & \quad|\bar{b}|=\sqrt{4+4+1}=3 \\ & \therefore \mathrm{d}\left(L_1, L_2\right)=\left|\frac{\left(\bar{a}_2-\bar{a}_1\right) \times \bar{b}}{b}\right|=\frac{5 \sqrt{5}}{3} \end{aligned}$$

content: A line makes the same angle '$$\alpha$$' with each of the $$x$$ and $$y$$ axes. If the angle '$$\theta$$', which it makes with the $$z$$-axis, is such that $$\sin ^2 \theta=2 \sin ^2 \alpha$$, then the angle $$\alpha$$ is
explanation:
Let us consider the direction cosines of the line that makes the same angle $$\alpha$$ with the $$x$$ and $$y$$ axes. The direction cosines $$l, m,$$ and $$n$$ are related to the angles made by the line with the $$x, y,$$ and $$z$$ axes respectively.

The direction cosines are given by:

  • $$l = \cos(\alpha)$$ (angle with $$x$$-axis)
  • $$m = \cos(\alpha)$$ (angle with $$y$$-axis)
  • $$n = \cos(\theta)$$ (angle with $$z$$-axis)

We know that the sum of the squares of the direction cosines equals 1, which is expressed as:

$$l^2 + m^2 + n^2 = 1$$

Substituting the values of $$l, m,$$ and $$n$$, we get:

$$\cos^2(\alpha) + \cos^2(\alpha) + \cos^2(\theta) = 1$$

Given that $$\sin^2(\theta) = 2\sin^2(\alpha)$$, we can express $$\cos^2(\theta)$$ in terms of $$\cos^2(\alpha)$$ using the Pythagorean identity $$\sin^2(\theta) + \cos^2(\theta) = 1$$.

First, let's solve for $$\cos^2(\theta)$$:

$$\begin{aligned} \sin^2(\theta) &= 2\sin^2(\alpha) \\ 1 - \cos^2(\theta) &= 2(1 - \cos^2(\alpha)) \\ 1 - \cos^2(\theta) &= 2 - 2\cos^2(\alpha) \\ \cos^2(\theta) &= 2\cos^2(\alpha) - 1 \end{aligned}$$

We then substitute this expression into the sum of squares equation mentioned earlier:

$$2\cos^2(\alpha) + (2\cos^2(\alpha) - 1) = 1$$

Simplify and solve for $$\cos^2(\alpha)$$:

$$\begin{aligned} 4\cos^2(\alpha) - 1 &= 1 \\ 4\cos^2(\alpha) &= 2 \\ \cos^2(\alpha) &= \frac{1}{2} \\ \cos(\alpha) &= \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \text{ or } \cos(\alpha) = -\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \end{aligned}$$

Since angles with both axes are given to be the same and it is generally assumed that these are acute angles, we will consider only the positive value.

The positive angle $$\alpha$$ whose cosine is $$\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$$ corresponds to an angle of $$\frac{\pi}{4}$$ radians.

Therefore, the angle $$\alpha$$ is $$\frac{\pi}{4}$$, which corresponds to Option A.

content: A tetrahedron has verticles $$P(1,2,1), Q(2,1,3), R(-1,1,2)$$ and $$O(0,0,0)$$. Then the angle between the faces $$O P Q$$ and $$P Q R$$ is
The Cartesian equation of a line passing through $$(1,2,3)$$ and parallel to $$x-y+2 z=5$$ and $$3 x+y+z=6$$ is
explanation:
Required line $$L \rightarrow P(1,2,3)$$

$$\begin{aligned} & L \| E_1: 1 x-1 y+2 z=5 \\ & L \| E_2: 3 x+1 y+1 z=6 \end{aligned}$$

$$\overline{n_1} \equiv(1,-1,2), \overline{n_2} \equiv(3,1,1)$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \overline{n_1} \times \overline{n_2} & =\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{\boldsymbol{i}} & \hat{\boldsymbol{j}} & \hat{\boldsymbol{k}} \\ 1 & -1 & 2 \\ 3 & 1 & 1 \end{array}\right|=-3 \hat{\boldsymbol{i}}-5 \hat{\boldsymbol{j}}+4 \hat{\boldsymbol{k}} \\ & \equiv(-3,-5,4) \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore$$ d.R.s. of line $$L$$ are $$(-3,-5,4)$$ and it passes through $$(1,2,3)$$

$$\therefore$$ its cartesian equations are

$$\frac{x-1}{-3}=\frac{y-2}{-5}=\frac{z-3}{4}$$

content: The equation of the plane passing through the points $$(2,3,1),(4,-5,3)$$ and parallel to $$X$$-axis is
explanation:
To find the equation of a plane passing through two points and parallel to the X-axis, we need to understand that a plane parallel to the X-axis would have a normal vector that is orthogonal (perpendicular) to the X-axis. Since the X-axis is represented by the vector $$(1, 0, 0)$$, any normal vector to the plane will have the form $$(0, b, c)$$, where $$b$$ and $$c$$ are real numbers. This means that the plane's normal vector does not have an X component.

The general equation of a plane in three dimensions is given by:

$$ A(x - x_0) + B(y - y_0) + C(z - z_0) = 0 $$

where $$(x_0, y_0, z_0)$$ is a point on the plane, and $$ (A, B, C) $$ is a normal vector to the plane.

Since the plane is parallel to the X-axis, we know that $$ A = 0 $$. Therefore, the equation simplifies to:

$$ B(y - y_0) + C(z - z_0) = 0 $$

Now we need to find values for $$ B $$ and $$ C $$ that will allow the plane to pass through $$ (2,3,1) $$ and $$ (4,-5,3) $$. These two points will provide us the direction vectors along the plane:

  • From point $$ (2,3,1) $$ to $$ (4,-5,3) $$, we get the direction vector by subtracting the respective components:

$$ (4 - 2, -5 - 3, 3 - 1) = (2, -8, 2) $$.

  • However, we need a direction vector that lies on the plane and is perpendicular to the X-axis direction vector. Given that the plane is parallel to the X-axis, the direction vector of the plane can simply be taken from the YZ components of the direction vector we found:

$$ (0, -8, 2) $$.

Now we can normalize this vector to get the plane's normal vector. Multiplying by a constant does not change the direction, so we can use $$(0, B, C) = (0, -8, 2)$$ directly or choose to simplify it by dividing by a common factor, for instance, dividing by 2 to get $$(0, -4, 1)$$ so:

$$ (B, C) = (-4, 1) $$

Now let's use one of the given points, $(2, 3, 1)$, to find the equation:

$$ -4(y - 3) + 1(z - 1) = 0 $$

$$ -4y + 12 + z - 1 = 0 $$

$$ -4y + z + 11 = 0 $$

This can be rearranged to:

$$ 4y - z = 11 $$

So the correct equation of the plane should be:

$$ 4y - z = 11 $$

This is not present in the given options. Let's check the options to see if any of them would be equivalent:

If we consider Option A, we get:

$$ 3y + 4z = 13 $$

For this equation to represent the same plane as $$ 4y - z = 11 $$, we would need the ratio between coefficients of $$ y $$ and $$ z $$ to be the same, and the independent term to scale accordingly.

Let's evaluate this by putting $$ y $$ and $$ z $$ from the point $$(2, 3, 1)$$ into Option A:

$$ 3(3) + 4(1) = 9 + 4 = 13 $$

Option A satisfies the point $$(2, 3, 1)$$. Now we need to do the same for the second point $$(4, -5, 3)$$:

$$ 3(-5) + 4(3) = -15 + 12 = -3 $$

But for this plane,

$$ 4y - z = 11 $$

$$ 4(-5) - 3 = -20 - 3 $$

$$ -23 \neq 11 $$

Hence Option A does not represent the same plane as $$ 4y - z = 11 $$.

Option B:

$$ y - 4z = -1 $$

Using the given points we can test for Option B in a similar fashion:

$$ (2, 3, 1): \quad 3 - 4(1) = 3 - 4 = -1 $$

$$ (4, -5, 3): \quad -5 - 4(3) = -5 - 12 = -17 \neq -1 $$

So, Option B can be ruled out as well.

Performing a similar verification for Options C and D:

Option C:

$$ 2y + 4z = 19 $$

Using the given points:

$$ (2, 3, 1): \quad 2(3) + 4(1) = 6 + 4 = 10 \neq 19 $$

$$ (4, -5, 3): \quad 2(-5) + 4(3) = -10 + 12 = 2 \neq 19 $$

Option C is not the correct option.

Option D:

$$ y + 4z = 7 $$

Using the given points:

$$ (2, 3, 1): \quad 3 + 4(1) = 3 + 4 = 7 $$

$$ (4, -5, 3): \quad -5 + 4(3) = -5 + 12 = 7 $$

Option D works with both points. Moreover, the ratio of coefficients between $$ y $$ and $$ z $$ in our correct equation $$ 4y - z = 11 $$ is 4 to -1. We can invert this to get -1 to 4, which matches the ratio for the coefficients of $$ y $$ and $$ z $$ in Option D, after rearranging it to be $$ y + 4z - 7 = 0 $$. This indicates that Option D could represent the same plane as our equation but with the terms moved around.

Based upon this analysis, the correct answer should be:

Option D

$$ y + 4z = 7 $$

content: The equation of the plane which passes through (2, $$-$$3, 1) and is normal to the line joining the points (3, 4, $$-$$1) and (2, $$-$$1, 5) is given by
explanation:
The required plane passes through $$(2,-3,1)$$.

It is normal to the line having d.r.s. $$(1,5,-6)$$.

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { The required plane line having d.r.s. }(1,5,-6) \text {. } \\ & \text { It is normal to the. } k=-19 \\ & \therefore x+5 y-6 z=k \quad \Rightarrow 2+5(-3)-6(1)=k \text { i.e } \end{aligned}$$

Hence equation of plane is $$x+5 y-6 z+19=0$$

content: If $$G(3,-5, r)$$ is the centroid of $$\triangle A B C$$, where $$A \equiv(7,-8,1), B \equiv(p, q, 5), C \equiv(q+1,5 p, 0)$$ are vertices of the triangle $$A B C$$, then the values of $$p, q, r$$ are respectively
explanation:
$$A(7,-8,1) ; B(p, q, 5)$$ and $$C(q+1,5 p, 0)$$ are vertices of $$\triangle A B C$$ having centroid $$G(3,-5, r)$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore 3=\frac{7+p+q+1}{3}, \quad-5=\frac{-8+q+5 p}{3}, r=\frac{1+5+0}{3} \\ & \therefore p+q=1 \quad \ldots(1), 5 p+q=-7 \quad \ldots(2), r=2 \end{aligned}$$

Solving (1) and (2), we get $$\mathrm{p}=-2, \mathrm{q}=3$$

content: If the lines $$\frac{2 x-4}{\lambda}=\frac{y-1}{2}=\frac{z-3}{1}$$ and $$\frac{x-1}{1}=\frac{3 y-1}{\lambda}=\frac{z-2}{1}$$ are perpendicular to each other, then $$\lambda=$$
explanation:
Lines $$\frac{2(x-2)}{\lambda}=\frac{y-1}{2}=\frac{z-3}{1}$$ and $$\frac{x-1}{1}=\frac{3\left(y-\frac{1}{3}\right)}{\lambda}=\frac{z-2}{1}$$ are perpendicular to one another.

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore\left(\frac{\lambda}{2}\right)(1)+(2)\left(\frac{\lambda}{3}\right)+(1)(1)=0 \\ & \therefore \frac{\lambda}{2}+\frac{2 \lambda}{3}=-1 \Rightarrow \lambda=\frac{-6}{7} \end{aligned}$$

content: The co-ordinates of the points on the line $$\frac{x+2}{1}=\frac{y-1}{2}=\frac{z+1}{-2}$$ at a distance of 12 units from the point A($$-$$2, 1, $$-$$1) are
explanation:
$$\frac{x+2}{1}=\frac{y-1}{2}=\frac{z+1}{-2}=\lambda \quad \text{.... (say)}$$

Hence coordinates of any point on the given line are $$(\lambda-2,2 \lambda+1,-2 \lambda-1)$$.

This point is at a distance of 12 units from $$(-2,1,-1)$$.

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { This point is at a distance of } 12 \text { units from }(-2,1,-1) . \\ & \therefore 12=\sqrt{(\lambda-2+2)^2+(2 \lambda+1-1)^2+(-2 \lambda-1+1)^2}=\sqrt{\lambda^2+4 \lambda^2+4 \lambda^2}=3 \lambda \\ & \therefore \lambda= \pm 4 \Rightarrow \text { Required point }=(2,9,-9) \text { or }(-6,-7,7) \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore \lambda= \pm 4 \Rightarrow$$ Required point $$=(2,9,-9)$$ or $$(-6,-7,7)$$

content: If the vector equation of the plane $$\bar{r}=(2 \hat{i}+\hat{k})+\lambda \hat{i}+\mu(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k})$$ in scalar product form is given by $$\overline{\mathrm{r}} \cdot(3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}})=\alpha$$ then $$\alpha=$$
explanation:
Here given plane passes through the point $$(2,0,1)$$ and let $$\bar{b}=\hat{i}$$ and $$\bar{c}=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}$$

Normal vector $$\overline{\mathrm{n}}=\overline{\mathrm{b}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{i} & \hat{\mathrm{j}} & \hat{k} \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 2 & -3\end{array}\right|=3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}$$

The equation of plane in scalar product from is $$\overline{\mathrm{r}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{n}}=\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{n}}$$

Here $$\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{n}}=(2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}) \cdot(3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}})=2$$

$$\therefore \alpha=2$$

content: If the lines $$\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y+1}{3}=\frac{z-1}{4}$$ and $$\frac{x-2}{1}=\frac{y+m}{2}=\frac{z-2}{1}$$ intersect each other, then value of m is
explanation:
Two intersection lines are

$$\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y+1}{3}=\frac{z-1}{4}=\lambda$$ and $$\frac{x-2}{1}=\frac{y+m}{2}=\frac{z-2}{1}=\mu$$

$$\therefore 2 \lambda+1=\mu+2\quad \text{.... (1)}$$

$$4 \lambda+1=\mu+2\quad \text{.... (2)}$$

$$3 \lambda-1=2 \mu-\mathrm{m}\quad \text{.... (3)}$$

From (1) and (2), we infer that $$\lambda=0$$ and $$\mu=-1$$.

Then from (3), we get $$\mathrm{m=-1}$$

content: The length of perpendicular drawn from the point $$2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+5 \hat{k}$$ to the line $$\overline{\mathrm{r}}=(11 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}-8 \hat{k})+\lambda(10 \hat{i}-4 \hat{j}-11 \hat{k})$$ is
explanation:
Let $$P \equiv(2,-1,5)$$ and co-ordinates of any point on the given line be

$$Q \equiv(10 \lambda+11,-4 \lambda-2,-11 \lambda-8)$$

d.r. of $$P Q$$ are $$(10 \lambda+9,-4 \lambda-1,-11 \lambda-13)$$

d.r. of given line are $$(10,-4,-11)$$.

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore(10 \lambda+9)(10)+(-4 \lambda-1)(-4)+(-11 \lambda-13)(-11)=0 \\ & \therefore 100 \lambda+90+16 \lambda+4+121 \lambda+143=0 \Rightarrow \lambda=-1 \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore \mathrm{Q} \equiv(1,2,3)$$ and $$\mathrm{d}(\mathrm{PQ})=\sqrt{1^2+3^2+2^2}=\sqrt{14}$$ units

content: Equation of the plane passing through the point $$(1,2,3)$$ and parallel to the plane $$2 x+3 y-4 z=0 $$
explanation:
Required plane passes through $$(1,2,3)$$ and is parallel to plane $$2 x+3 y-4 z=0$$. Hence equation of required plane is

$$2(x-1)+3(y-2)-4(z-3)=0 \quad \Rightarrow 2 x+3 y-4 z+4=0$$

content: If $$\mathrm{A}$$ and $$\mathrm{B}$$ are the foot of the perpendicular drawn from the point $$\mathrm{Q}(\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}, \mathrm{c})$$ to the planes $$\mathrm{YZ}$$ and $$\mathrm{ZX}$$ respectively, then the equation of the plane through the points $$\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}$$, and $$\mathrm{O}$$ is (where $$\mathrm{O}$$ is the origin)
explanation:
From given data, we write $$A \equiv(0, b, c)$$, and $$B \equiv(a, 0, c)$$ Equation of plane passing through A, B and O is

$$\begin{aligned} & \left|\begin{array}{lll} x-0 & y-0 & z-0 \\ 0-0 & b-0 & c-0 \\ a-0 & 0-0 & c-0 \end{array}\right|=0 \Rightarrow\left|\begin{array}{lll} x & y & z \\ 0 & b & c \\ a & 0 & c \end{array}\right|=0 \\ & \therefore x(b c)-y(-a c)+z(-a b)=0 \Rightarrow b c x+a c y-a b z=0 \\ \end{aligned}$$

Dividing both sides by abc, we get $$\frac{x}{a}+\frac{y}{b}-\frac{z}{c}=0$$

content: If $$\mathrm{A}=(-2,2,3), \mathrm{B}=(3,2,2), \mathrm{C}=(4,-3,5)$$ and $$\mathrm{D}=(7,-5,-1)$$ Then the projection of $$\overline{\mathrm{AB}}$$ on $$\overline{\mathrm{CD}}$$ is
explanation:
$$\mathrm{A=(-2,2,3) ; B=(3,2,2) ; C=(4,-3,5) \text { and } D=(7,-5,-1)}$$

$$\overline{A B}=5 \hat{i}-\hat{k} \quad \text { and } \quad \overline{C D}=3 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}-6 \hat{k}$$

$$\text { Projection of } \overline{\mathrm{AB}} \text { on } \overline{\mathrm{CD}}=\frac{\overline{\mathrm{AB}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{CD}}}{|\overline{\mathrm{CD}}|}$$ $$=\frac{(5 \hat{i}-\hat{k}) \cdot(3 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}-6 \hat{k})}{\sqrt{(3)^2+(-2)^2+(-6)^2}}=\frac{15+6}{7}=3$$

content: The Cartesian equation of a plane which passes through the points $$\mathrm{A}(2,2,2)$$ and making equal nonzero intercepts on the co-ordinate axes is
explanation:
Let $$\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}, \mathrm{c}$$ be the intercepts on the coordinate axes and we have $$\mathrm{a}=\mathrm{b}=\mathrm{c}$$.

$$\therefore \frac{x}{a}+\frac{y}{a}+\frac{z}{a}=1$$ is required equation of plane.

Since plane passes through point $$(2,2,2)$$, we write

$$\frac{2+2+2}{a}=1 \quad \Rightarrow a=6$$

$$\therefore$$ Equation of plane is $$x+y+z=6$$

content: The co-ordinates of the foot of the perpendicular drawn from the point $$2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+5 \hat{k}$$ to the line $$\vec{r}=(11 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}-8 \hat{k})+\lambda(10 \hat{i}-4 \hat{j}-11 \hat{k})$$ are
explanation:
We have $$\bar{r}=(11 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}-8 \hat{k})+\lambda(10 \hat{i}-4 \hat{j}-11 \hat{k})$$

So coordinates of any point on this line are $$[(10 \lambda+11),(-4 \lambda-2),(-11 \lambda-8)]$$

Let $$\mathrm{P} \equiv(2,-1,5)$$ and let $$\mathrm{M}$$ be foot of perpendicular.

$$\therefore$$ d.r. of PM are $$(10 \lambda+9),(-4 \lambda-1),(-11 \lambda-13)$$

Since PM is perpendicular to given line, we write

$$\begin{aligned} & (10 \lambda+9)(10)+(-4 \lambda-1)(-4)+(-11 \lambda-13)(-11)=0 \\ & \therefore 100 \lambda+90+16 \lambda+4+121 \lambda+143=0 \Rightarrow 237=-237 \lambda \Rightarrow \lambda=-1 \\ & \mathrm{M}=(-10+11,4-2,11-8) \text { i.e. }(1,2,3) \end{aligned}$$

content: If A(3, 2, $$-$$1), B($$-$$2, 2, $$-$$3) and D($$-$$2, 5, $$-$$4) are the vertices of a parallelogram, then the area of the parallelogram is
explanation:
We have $$\mathrm{A} \equiv(3,2,-1), \mathrm{B} \equiv(-2,2,-3)$$ and $$\mathrm{D} \equiv(-2,5,-4)$$

$$\therefore \overline{A B}=-5 \hat{i}-2 \hat{k} \quad \text { and } \quad \overline{A D}=-5 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}$$

Area of parallelogram $$=|\overline{\mathrm{AB}} \times \overline{\mathrm{AD}}|$$

Now $$\overline{\mathrm{AB}} \times \overline{\mathrm{AD}}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ -5 & 0 & -2 \\ -5 & 3 & -3\end{array}\right|=\hat{i}(6)-\hat{j}(5)+\hat{k}(-15) \quad=6 \hat{i}-5 \hat{j}-15 \hat{k}$$

$$\therefore$$ Area $$=\sqrt{(6)^2+(-5)^2+(-15)^2}=\sqrt{286} ~\text{sq. units}$$

content: The distance between the parallel lines $$\frac{x-2}{3}=\frac{y-4}{5}=\frac{z-1}{2}$$ and $$\frac{x-1}{3}=\frac{y+2}{5}=\frac{z+3}{2}$$ is
explanation:
Let $$\bar{a}_1=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}$$ and $$\overline{\mathrm{a}}_2=\hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}$$

$$\therefore \bar{a}_2-\bar{a}_1=-\hat{i}-6 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k}$$ and let $$\bar{b}=3 \hat{i}+5 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \quad \overline{\mathrm{b}} \times\left|\overline{\mathrm{a}}_2-\overline{\mathrm{a}}_1\right|=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{\mathrm{i}} & \hat{\mathrm{j}} & \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ 3 & 5 & 2 \\ -1 & -6 & -4 \end{array}\right|=\hat{\mathrm{i}}(-20+12)-\hat{\mathrm{j}}(-12+2)+\hat{\mathrm{k}}(-18+5)=-8 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+10 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-13 \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ & \therefore\left|\overline{\mathrm{b}} \times\left(\overline{\mathrm{a}}_2-\overline{\mathrm{a}}_1\right)\right|=\sqrt{64+100+169}=\sqrt{333} \\ & \text { Also }|\overline{\mathrm{b}}|=\sqrt{9+25+4}=\sqrt{38} \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore$$ Distance between given lines $$=\sqrt{\frac{333}{38}}$$ units.

content: The coordinates of the foot of the perpendicular drawn from the origin to the plane $$2 x+y-2 z=18$$ are
explanation:
$$2 x+y-2 z=18$$

Dividing both sides by $$\sqrt{(2)^2+(1)^2+(-2)^2}=3$$, we get

$$\left(\frac{2}{3}\right) x+\left(\frac{1}{3}\right) y-\left(\frac{2}{3}\right) z=6$$

Hence length of perpendicular from origin to the plane is 6.

Therefore coordinates of foot of perpendicular are $$\left[\left(\frac{2}{3}\right)(6),\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)(6),\left(\frac{-2}{3}\right)(6)\right]$$ i.e. $$(4,2,-4)$$

content: The vector equation of the line passing through $$\mathrm{P}(1,2,3)$$ and $$\mathrm{Q}(2,3,4)$$ is
explanation: d.r. of line through $$P Q$$ are $$[(2-1),(3-2),(4-3)]$$ i.e. $$(1,1,1)$$ Hence required equation of line is $$(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k})+\lambda(\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k})$$

content: Equation of planes parallel to the plane $$x-2y+2z+4=0$$ which are at a distance of one unit from the point (1, 2, 3) are
explanation:
The equation of planes parallel to the plane $$x-2 y+2 z+4=0$$ is

$$x-2 y+2 z+\lambda=0 \quad \text{... (i)}$$

The required planes are at a distance of one unit from the point $$(1,2,3)$$

$$\left|\frac{a x_1+b y_1+c z_1+d}{\sqrt{a^2+b^2+c^2}}\right|=1$$

$$\left|\frac{1(1)+(-2)(2)+2(3)+\lambda}{\sqrt{1+4+4}}\right|=1 \Rightarrow\left|\frac{1-4+6+\lambda}{3}\right|=1 \Rightarrow 3+\lambda= \pm 3$$

$$\begin{array}{rlrlrl} \therefore & 3+\lambda & =3 & \text { or } & 3+\lambda & =-3 \\ \lambda & =0 & \text { or } & \lambda & =-6 \end{array}$$

The equation of planes are $$x-2 y+2 z=0$$ and $$x-2 y+2 z-6=0$$

content: The area of triangle with vertices $$(1,2,0),(1,0, a)$$ and $$(0,3,1)$$ is $$\sqrt{6}$$ sq. units, then the values of '$$a$$' are
explanation:
Refer figure

$$\mathrm{A}(\triangle \mathrm{ABC}) \quad=\frac{1}{2}|\overline{\mathrm{BA}} \times \overline{\mathrm{BC}}|$$

Here

$$\begin{aligned} & \overline{\mathrm{BA}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-a \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ & \overline{\mathrm{BC}}=-\hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+(1-\mathrm{a}) \hat{\mathrm{k}} \end{aligned}$$

Now

$$\overline{\mathrm{BA}} \times \overline{\mathrm{BC}}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{\mathrm{i}} & \hat{\mathrm{j}} & \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ 0 & 2 & -\mathrm{a} \\ -1 & 3 & 1-\mathrm{a} \end{array}\right|$$

$$=\hat{i}(2-2 a+3 a)-\hat{j}(-a)+\hat{k}(2)=(a+2) \hat{i}+(a) \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}$$

$$\therefore|\overline{\mathrm{BA}} \times \overline{\mathrm{BC}}|=\sqrt{(\mathrm{a}+2)^2+(\mathrm{a})^2+(2)^2}$$

From given data, we write

$$\begin{aligned} & \sqrt{6}=\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{2 a^2+4 a+8} \\ & \therefore 4(6)=2 a^2+4 a+8 \quad \Rightarrow a^2+2 a-8=0 \Rightarrow(a+4)(a-2)=0 \Rightarrow a=-4,2 \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$\mathrm{G}(4,3,3)$$ is the centroid of the triangle $$\mathrm{ABC}$$ whose vertices are $$\mathrm{A}(\mathrm{a}, 3,1), \mathrm{B}(4,5, \mathrm{~b})$$ and $$C(6, c, 5)$$, then the values of $$a, b, c$$ are
explanation:
We have vertices $$A(a, 31) ; B(4,5, b) ; C(6 ; c, 5)$$ and centroid $$G(4,3,3)$$ of $$\triangle A B C$$.

$$\therefore \frac{a+4+6}{3}=4, \frac{3+5+c}{3}=3, \frac{1+b+5}{3}=3 \Rightarrow a=2, c=1, b=3$$

content: The d.r.s. of the normal to the plane passing through the origin and the line of intersection of the planes $$x+2 y+3 z=4$$ and $$4 x+3 y+2 z=1$$ are
explanation:
Equation of the plane passing through the line of intersection of the given planes, is

$$\begin{aligned} & (x+2 y+3 z-4)+\lambda(4 x+3 y+2 z-1)=0 \\ & \therefore(1+4 \lambda) x+(2+3 \lambda) y+(3+2 \lambda) z+(-4-\lambda)=0 \quad \text{.... (1)} \end{aligned}$$

Since the plane (1) passes through origin,

We get $$-4-\lambda=0 \Rightarrow \lambda=-4$$

Substituting value of $$\lambda$$ in equation (1), we get

$$\begin{aligned} & -15 x-10 y-5 z=0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 3 x+2 y+z=0 \\ & \therefore \text { d.r.s. are }(3,2,1) \text {. } \end{aligned}$$

content: The line $$\frac{x-2}{3}=\frac{y-1}{-5}=\frac{z+2}{2}$$ lies in the plane $$x+3 y-\alpha z+\beta=0$$, then value of $$\alpha \beta$$ is
explanation:
Line $$\frac{x-2}{3}=\frac{y-1}{-5}=\frac{z+2}{2}$$ lies in plane $$x+3 y-\alpha z+\beta=0$$

$$\therefore(3)(1)+(-5)(3)+2(-\alpha)=0 \Rightarrow \alpha=-6$$

Thus equation of plane is $$x+3 y+6 z+\beta=0$$ and point $$(2,1,-2)$$ lies in it.

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore 2+3(1)+6(-2)+\beta=0 \Rightarrow \beta=7 \\ & \therefore \alpha \beta=(-6)(7)=-42 \end{aligned}$$

content: If the points $$P(4,5, x), Q(3, y, 4)$$ and $$R(5,8,0)$$ are collinear, then the value of $$x+y$$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{array}{ll} \overline{P Q} & =-\hat{i}+(y-5) \hat{j}+(4-x) \hat{k} \\ \overline{P R} & =\hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-x \hat{k} \end{array}$$

Since points $$\mathrm{P}, \mathrm{Q}, \mathrm{R}$$ are collinear,

$$\begin{aligned} & \overline{P Q}=a \overline{P R} \\ & \therefore-\hat{i}+(y-5) \hat{j}+(4-x) \hat{k}=a(\hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-x \hat{k}) \\ & \therefore a=-1,3 a=y-5,-a x=4-x \\ & \therefore a=-1 \quad \Rightarrow-3=y-5 \quad \text { i.e. } y=2 \text { and } x=4-x \Rightarrow x=2 \Rightarrow x+y=4 \end{aligned}$$

content: A line drawn from a point $$A(-2,-2,3)$$ and parallel to the line $$\frac{x}{-2}=\frac{y}{2}=\frac{z}{-1}$$ meets the $$\mathrm{YOZ}$$ plane in point $$\mathrm{P}$$, then the co-ordinates of the point $$\mathrm{P}$$ are
explanation:
Equation of required line is $$\frac{x+2}{-2}=\frac{y+2}{2}=\frac{z-3}{-1}$$ and this line meets YZ plane in P.

Coordinates of any point on this line are $$(-2 \lambda-2,2 \lambda-2,-\lambda+3)$$, where $$\lambda$$ is a scalar. Since $$\mathrm{P}$$ is on $$\mathrm{YZ}$$ plane, we write

$$\begin{aligned} & -2 \lambda-2=0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda=-1 \\ & \therefore P \equiv(0,-4,4) \end{aligned}$$

content: The Cartesian equation of a line is $$3 x+1=6 y-2=1-z$$, then its vector equation is
explanation: $\begin{aligned} & 3 x+1=6 y-2=1-z \\ \\& \therefore 3\left(x+\frac{1}{3}\right)=6\left(y-\frac{2}{6}\right)=-(z-1) \\\\ & \therefore \frac{\left(x+\frac{1}{3}\right)}{\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)}=\frac{\left(y-\frac{1}{3}\right)}{\left(\frac{1}{6}\right)}=\frac{(z-1)}{(-1)} \Rightarrow \text { d.r.s. are } 2,1,-6 \\\\ & \text { Thus } \bar{r}=\left(-\frac{1}{3} \hat{i}+\frac{1}{3} \hat{j}+\hat{k}\right)+\lambda(2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-6 \hat{k})\end{aligned}$

content: The plane $$\frac{x}{2}+\frac{y}{3}+\frac{z}{4}=1$$ cuts the $$X$$-axis at A, Y-axis at B and Z-axis at C, then the area of $$\triangle \mathrm{ABC}=$$
explanation:
The vertices of triangle $$\mathrm{ABC}$$ are

$$\begin{aligned} & A=(2,0,0) ; \quad B=(0,3,0) ; C=(0,0,4) \\ & \overline{\mathrm{AB}}=-2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}} \text { and } \overline{\mathrm{AC}}=-2 \hat{i}+4 \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ & \overline{A B} \times \overline{A C}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ -2 & 3 & 0 \\ -2 & 0 & 4 \end{array}\right|=\hat{i}(12)-\hat{j}(-8)+\hat{k}(6)=12 \hat{i}+8 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k} \\ & \therefore \mathrm{A}(\triangle \mathrm{ABC})=\frac{1}{2}|\overline{\mathrm{AB}} \times \overline{\mathrm{AC}}| \\ & =\frac{1}{2}(\sqrt{144+64+36}) \\ & =\sqrt{61} \text { sq. units } \\ \end{aligned}$$

content: If a plane meets the axes $$\mathrm{X}, \mathrm{Y}, \mathrm{Z}$$ in $$\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}, \mathrm{C}$$ respectively such that centroid of $$\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$$ is $$(1,2,3)$$, then the equation of the plane is
explanation:
Let's consider the given points where the plane meets the coordinate axes:

  • The plane meets the X-axis at point $$A(a, 0, 0)$$
  • The plane meets the Y-axis at point $$B(0, b, 0)$$
  • The plane meets the Z-axis at point $$C(0, 0, c)$$

The centroid of triangle $$\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$$ is calculated as follows:

The coordinates of the centroid $$G$$ of a triangle with vertices $$(x_1, y_1, z_1)$$, $$(x_2, y_2, z_2)$$, and $$(x_3, y_3, z_3)$$ are given by:

$$ \left( \frac{x_1 + x_2 + x_3}{3}, \frac{y_1 + y_2 + y_3}{3}, \frac{z_1 + z_2 + z_3}{3} \right) $$

For triangle $$\mathrm{ABC}$$, the centroid is given as $$(1,2,3)$$:

$$ \left( \frac{a + 0 + 0}{3}, \frac{0 + b + 0}{3}, \frac{0 + 0 + c}{3} \right) = (1, 2, 3) $$

This can be decomposed into three separate equations:

$$ \frac{a}{3} = 1 \implies a = 3 $$

$$ \frac{b}{3} = 2 \implies b = 6 $$

$$ \frac{c}{3} = 3 \implies c = 9 $$

Thus, the intercepts of the plane are $$3$$ on the X-axis, $$6$$ on the Y-axis, and $$9$$ on the Z-axis.

The general equation of a plane with intercepts $$a$$, $$b$$, and $$c$$ is:

$$ \frac{x}{a} + \frac{y}{b} + \frac{z}{c} = 1 $$

Substituting the values of $$a$$, $$b$$, and $$c$$, we get:

$$ \frac{x}{3} + \frac{y}{6} + \frac{z}{9} = 1 $$

Therefore, the equation of the plane is:

Option C: $$\frac{x}{3}+\frac{y}{6}+\frac{z}{9}=1$$

content: The shortest distance between lines $$\bar{r}=(2 \hat{i}-\hat{j})+\lambda(2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-3 \hat{k})$$ and $$\bar{r}=(\hat{r}-\hat{j}+2 \hat{k})+\mu(2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-5 \hat{k})$$ is
explanation:
We have lines $$\bar{r}=(2 \hat{i}-\hat{j})+\lambda(2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-3 \hat{k})$$ and $$\bar{r}=(\hat{i}-\hat{j}+2 \hat{k})+\mu(2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-5 \hat{k})$$

Let $$\bar{a}=2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}$$ and $$\bar{b}=\hat{i}-\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}$$

$$\therefore \overline{\mathrm{AB}}=-\hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}$$

Vector perpendicular to given lines is

$$\overline{\mathrm{n}}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{\mathrm{i}} & \hat{\mathrm{j}} & \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ 2 & 1 & -3 \\ 2 & 1 & -5 \end{array}\right|=\hat{\mathrm{i}}(-5+3)-\hat{\mathrm{j}}(-10+6)+\hat{\mathrm{k}}(2-2)=-2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{j}}$$

Shortest distance between given lines

$$=\overline{\mathrm{AB}} \cdot \frac{\overline{\mathrm{n}}}{|\overline{\mathrm{n}}|}=\frac{(-\hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}) \cdot(-2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{j}})}{\sqrt{(-2)^2+(4)^2}} \quad=\frac{2}{\sqrt{20}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{5}}$$

content: The direction cosines $$\ell, \mathrm{m}, \mathrm{n}$$ of the line $$\frac{\mathrm{x}+2}{2}=\frac{2 \mathrm{y}-5}{3} ; \mathrm{z}=-1$$ are
explanation:
We have line $$\frac{x+2}{2}=\frac{2 y-5}{3}, z=-1$$

i.e. $$\frac{x-(-2)}{2}=\frac{2\left(y-\frac{5}{2}\right)}{3}, z=-1$$

i.e. $$\frac{x-(-2)}{2}=\frac{\left(y-\frac{5}{2}\right)}{\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)}, z=-1$$

Here direction ratios are $$2, \frac{3}{2}, 0$$

Also $$\sqrt{(2)^2+\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)^2+0}= \pm \frac{5}{2}$$

Here required direction cosines are

$$\frac{2}{\left( \pm \frac{5}{2}\right)}, \frac{\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)}{\left( \pm \frac{5}{2}\right)}, \frac{0}{\left( \pm \frac{5}{2}\right)} \text { i.e. } \pm \frac{4}{5}, \pm \frac{3}{5}, 0$$

content: Equation of the plane passing through the point (2, 0, 5) and parallel to the vectors $$\widehat i - \widehat j + \widehat k$$ and $$3\widehat i + 2\widehat j - \widehat k$$ is
explanation:
Normal to the plane is perpendicular to the given vectors.

Hence equation of normal is

$$\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 1 & -1 & 1 \\ 3 & 2 & -1\end{array}\right|=\hat{i}(-1)-\hat{j}(-4)+\hat{k}(5)=-\hat{i}+4 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k}$$

Hence equation of required plane is

$$\begin{aligned} & (-1)(x-2)+(4)(y-0)+(5)(z-5)=0 \\ & \therefore-x+2+4 y+5 z-25=0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x-4 y-5 z+23=0 \end{aligned}$$

content: The co-ordinates of the point $$\mathrm{P} \equiv(1,2,3)$$ and $$\mathrm{O} \equiv(0,0,0)$$, then the direction cosines of $$\overline{\mathrm{OP}}$$ are
explanation:
We have $$\mathrm{O} \equiv(0,0,0)$$ and $$\mathrm{P} \equiv(1,2,3)$$

Hence direction ratios of $$\overline{\mathrm{OP}}$$ are

$$\frac{1-0}{\sqrt{1^2+2^2+3^2}}, \frac{2-0}{\sqrt{1^2+2^2+3^2}}, \frac{3-0}{\sqrt{1^2+2^2+3^2}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{14}}, \frac{2}{\sqrt{14}}, \frac{3}{\sqrt{14}}$$

content: The equation of the plane containing the line $$\frac{x+1}{-3}=\frac{y-3}{2}=\frac{z+2}{1}$$ and the point $$(0,7,-7)$$ is
explanation:
Let $$\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}, \mathrm{c}$$ be the direction cosines of the required plane.

It contains the line $$\frac{x+1}{-3}=\frac{y-3}{2}=\frac{z+2}{1}$$ and passes through the point $$(0,7,-7)$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \mathrm{a}(\mathrm{x}+1)+\mathrm{b}(\mathrm{y}-3)+\mathrm{c}(\mathrm{z}+2)=0 \quad \text{......(1)}\\ & \therefore \mathrm{a}(0+1)+\mathrm{b}(7-3)+\mathrm{c}(-7+2)=0 \quad \Rightarrow \mathrm{a}+4 \mathrm{~b}-5 \mathrm{c}=0 \quad \text{......(2)} \end{aligned}$$

Also $$-3 \mathrm{a}+2 \mathrm{~b}+\mathrm{c}=0$$ ..... (3)

From (2) and (3), we write

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{a}{\left|\begin{array}{cc} 4 & -5 \\ 2 & 1 \end{array}\right|}=\frac{-b}{\left|\begin{array}{cc} 1 & -5 \\ -3 & 1 \end{array}\right|}=\frac{c}{\left|\begin{array}{cc} 1 & 4 \\ -3 & 2 \end{array}\right|} \\ & \therefore \frac{a}{14}=\frac{b}{14}=\frac{c}{14} \quad \Rightarrow \quad a=b=c=1 \end{aligned}$$

Hence eq. (1) becomes

$$x+1+y-3+z+2=0 \Rightarrow x+y+z=0$$

content: The equation of a line passing through $$(3,-1,2)$$ and perpendicular to the lines $$\bar{r}=(\hat{i}+\hat{j}-\hat{k})+\lambda(2 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+\hat{k})$$ and $$\bar{r}=(2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-3 \hat{k})+\mu(\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k})$$ is
explanation:
Let $$\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}, \mathrm{c}$$ be the direction ratios of the required line.

$$\therefore 2 \mathrm{a}-2 \mathrm{~b}+\mathrm{c}=0$$ ....... (1) and $$\mathrm{a}-2 \mathrm{~b}+2 \mathrm{c}=0$$ ..... (2)

From (1) and (2), we write

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{a}}{\left|\begin{array}{ll} -2 & 1 \\ -2 & 2 \end{array}\right|}=\frac{-\mathrm{b}}{\left|\begin{array}{ll} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{array}\right|}=\frac{\mathrm{c}}{\left|\begin{array}{ll} 2 & -2 \\ 1 & -2 \end{array}\right|} \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{a}}{-2}=\frac{\mathrm{b}}{-3}=\frac{\mathrm{c}}{-2} \\ & \therefore(\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}, \mathrm{c})=(2,3,2) \end{aligned}$$

So equation of required line is

$$\frac{x-3}{2}=\frac{y+1}{3}=\frac{z-2}{2}$$

content: The area of the parallelogram with vertices A(1, 2, 3), B(1, 3, a), C(3, 8, 6) and D(3, 7, 3) is $$\sqrt{265}$$ sq. units, then a =
explanation:
Refer figure

A($$\Delta$$ABC) = $$\mathrm{\frac{A(\square ABCD)}{2}}$$

$$=\frac{\sqrt{265}}{2}$$

Also $$\mathrm{A}(\triangle \mathrm{ABC})=\frac{1}{2}|\overline{\mathrm{BA}} \times \overline{\mathrm{BC}}|$$

Here $$\overline{B A}=-\hat{j}+(3-a) \hat{k} \cdot$$ and $$\overline{B C}=2 \hat{i}+5 \hat{j}+(6-a) \hat{k}$$

Now $$\overline{B A} \times \overline{B C}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 0 & -1 & (3-a) \\ 2 & 5 & (6-a)\end{array}\right|$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\hat{i}(-6+a-15+5 a)-\hat{j}(-6+2 a)+\hat{k}(2) \\\\ & =(6 a-21) \hat{i}-(2 a-6) \hat{j}+2 \hat{k} \end{aligned}$$

Magnitude of $$\overline{\mathrm{BA}} \times \overline{\mathrm{BC}}=\sqrt{(6 \mathrm{a}-21)^2+(2 \mathrm{a}-6)^2+4}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \frac{1}{2} \sqrt{(6 a-21)^2+(2 a-6)^2+4}=\frac{\sqrt{265}}{2} \\\\ & \therefore(6 a-21)^2+(2 a-6)^2+4=265 \\\\ & \therefore 40 a^2-276 a+216=0 \\\\ & \therefore 10 a^2-69 a+54=0 \\\\ & \therefore a=\frac{69 \pm \sqrt{(69)^2-(4)(54)(10)}}{20}=\frac{69 \pm 51}{20}=\frac{120}{20} \text { or } \frac{18}{20} \\\\ & \therefore a=6 \text { from options given. } \end{aligned}$$

content: If the lines $\frac{1-x}{3}=\frac{7 y-14}{2 \lambda}=\frac{z-3}{2}$ and $\frac{7-7 x}{3 \lambda}=\frac{y-5}{1}=\frac{6-z}{5}$ are at right angles, then $\lambda=$
explanation:
We have lines

$$\frac{x-1}{-3}=\frac{7(y-2)}{2 \lambda}=\frac{z-3}{2} \text { and } \frac{7(1-x)}{3 \lambda}=\frac{y-5}{1}=\frac{z-6}{-5}$$

i.e. $$\frac{x-1}{-3}=\frac{y-2}{\left(\frac{2 \lambda}{7}\right)}=\frac{z-3}{2}$$ and $$\frac{x-1}{\left(\frac{-3 \lambda}{7}\right)}=\frac{y-5}{1}=\frac{z-6}{-5}$$

Since given lines are at right angles, we write

$$\begin{aligned} & (-3)\left(\frac{-3 \lambda}{7}\right)+\left(\frac{2 \lambda}{7}\right)(1)+(2)(-5)=0 \\ & \therefore \frac{9 \lambda}{7}+\frac{2 \lambda}{7}-10=0 \Rightarrow 11 \lambda=70 \Rightarrow \lambda=\frac{70}{11} \end{aligned}$$

content: The Cartesian equation of the plane passing through the point A(7, 8, 6) and parallel to the XY plane is
explanation:
The required plane passes through the point (7, 8, 6) and is parallel to XY plane.

$$\therefore$$ It is perpendicular to z axis and direction ratios of z axis are 0, 0, 1.

$$\therefore$$ Required equation of plane is

$$\mathrm{0(x-7)+0(y-8)+1(z-6)=0\Rightarrow z=6}$$

content: The equation of the plane passing through $$(-2,2,2)$$ and $$(2,-2,-2)$$ and perpendicular to the plane $$9 x-13 y-3 z=0$$ is
explanation:
Equation of plane passing through $$(-2,2,2)$$ is $$a(x+2)+b(y-2)+c(z-2)=0$$

Since this plane also passes through $$(2,-2,-2)$$, we get

$$4 a-4 b-4 c=0 \Rightarrow a-b-c=0$$ ....... (1)

Normal of the plane is parallel to

$$9 x-13 y-3 z=0 \Rightarrow 9 a-13 b-3 c=0$$ ....... (2)

Solving (1) and (2), we write

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{a}}{\left|\begin{array}{ll} -1 & -1 \\ -13 & -3 \end{array}\right|}=\frac{-\mathrm{b}}{\left|\begin{array}{cc} 1 & -1 \\ 9 & -3 \end{array}\right|}=\frac{\mathrm{c}}{\left|\begin{array}{cc} 1 & -1 \\ 9 & -13 \end{array}\right|} \\ & \therefore \frac{\mathrm{a}}{-10}=\frac{-\mathrm{b}}{6}=\frac{\mathrm{c}}{-4} \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{a}}{5}=\frac{\mathrm{b}}{3}=\frac{\mathrm{c}}{2} \end{aligned}$$

Hence equation of required plane is $$5(x+2)+3(y-2)+2(z-2)=0$$ i.e. $$5 x+3 y+2 z=0$$

content: The Cartesian equation of the line passing through the points A(2, 2, 1) and B(1, 3, 0) is
explanation:
The required Cartesian equation of line is

$$\frac{x-2}{1-2}=\frac{y-2}{3-2}=\frac{z-1}{0-1} \quad \text { i.e. } \frac{x-2}{-1}=\frac{y-2}{1}=\frac{z-1}{-1}$$

content: The Cartesian equation of the plane $$\overline{\mathrm{r}}=(\hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}})+\lambda(\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}})+\mu(\hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}})$$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \overline{\mathrm{r}}=(\hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}})+\lambda(\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}})+\mu(\hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{k}) \\ & \overline{\mathrm{a}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}, \overline{\mathrm{A}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}, \overline{\mathrm{B}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k} \end{aligned}$$

$$\bar{n}$$ is $$\bot$$er to $$\overline{\mathrm{A}}$$ and $$\overline{\mathrm{B}}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \begin{aligned} \therefore \overline{\mathrm{n}}=\overline{\mathrm{A}} \times \overline{\mathrm{B}} & =\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{\mathrm{i}} & \hat{\mathrm{j}} & \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -2 & 3 \end{array}\right| \\ & =\hat{\mathrm{i}}(3+2)-\hat{\mathrm{j}}(3-1)+\hat{\mathrm{k}}(-2-1)=5 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-3 \hat{\mathrm{k}} \end{aligned} \\ & \begin{array}{l} \overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{n}}=\mathrm{d} \quad=(\hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}) \cdot(5 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}) \quad=5+2=7 \end{array} \\ & \begin{array}{l} \therefore \overline{\mathrm{r}} \cdot(5 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-3 \hat{\mathrm{k}})=7 \Rightarrow \text { Cartesian equation is } 5 \mathrm{x}-2 \mathrm{y}-3 \mathrm{z}-7=0 \end{array} \end{aligned}$$

content: The equation of the plane that contains the line of intersection of the planes. $$x+2 y+3 z-4=0$$ and $$2 x+y-z+5=0$$ and is perpendicular to the plane $$5 x+3 y-6 z+8=0$$ is
explanation:
The equation of the required plane is $$(x+2 y+3 z-4)+\lambda(2 x+y-z+5)=0$$ i.e. ...... (1)

$$(1+2 \lambda) x+(2+\lambda) y+(3-\lambda) z+(-4+5 \lambda)=0$$

Since (1) is perpendicular to the plane $$5 x+3 y-6 z+8=0$$, we write

$$\begin{aligned} & (1+2 \lambda)(5)+(2+\lambda)(3)+(3-\lambda)(-6)=0 \\ & \therefore 5+10 \lambda+6+3 \lambda-18+6 \lambda=0 \end{aligned}$$

$$19 \lambda=7 \Rightarrow \lambda=\frac{7}{19} $$

Substituting value of $$\lambda$$ in eq. (1), we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \left(1+\frac{14}{19}\right) x+\left(2+\frac{7}{19}\right) y+\left(3-\frac{7}{19}\right) z+\left(-4+\frac{35}{19}\right)=0 \\ & \therefore 33 x+45 y+50 z-41=0 \end{aligned}$$

content: The vector equation of the line whose Cartesian equations are y = 2 and 4x $$-$$ 3z + 5 = 0 is
explanation:
We have lines $$y-2=0$$ and $$4 x-3 z+5=0$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore 4 x=3 z-5=3\left[z-\left(\frac{5}{3}\right)\right] \\ & \therefore \frac{4 x}{12}=\frac{3\left[z-\left(\frac{5}{3}\right)\right]}{12} \Rightarrow \frac{x}{3}=\frac{z-\left(\frac{5}{3}\right)}{4}, y=2 \end{aligned}$$

Thus line passes through the point $$\left(0,2, \frac{5}{3}\right)$$ i.e. a point having position vector $$2 \mathrm{j}+\frac{5}{3} \hat{k}$$

Also direction ratios of a line are $$3,0,4$$.

Hence required vector equation is

$$\overline{\mathrm{r}}=\left(2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\frac{5}{3} \hat{\mathrm{k}}\right)+\lambda(3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{k}})$$

content: The Cartesian equation of the plane passing through the point $$(0,7,-7)$$ and containing the line $$\frac{x+1}{-3}=\frac{y-3}{2}=\frac{z+2}{1}$$ is
explanation:
The plane passes through the point $$(0,7,-7)$$ and contains the line $$\frac{x+1}{-3}=\frac{y-3}{2}=\frac{z+2}{1}$$.

$$\therefore$$ Required equation of the plane is

$$\begin{aligned} & \left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{\mathrm{i}} & \hat{\mathrm{j}} & \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ -1-0 & 3-7 & -2+7 \\ -3 & 2 & 1 \end{array}\right| \quad=0 \quad \Rightarrow\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{\mathrm{i}} & \hat{\mathrm{j}} & \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ -1 & -4 & 5 \\ -3 & 2 & 1 \end{array}\right|=0 \\ & \therefore \hat{\mathrm{i}}(-4-10)-\hat{\mathrm{j}}(-1+15)+\hat{\mathrm{k}}(-2-12)=0 \\ & \therefore-14 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-14 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-14 \hat{\mathrm{k}}=0 \quad \Rightarrow \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}=0 \end{aligned}$$

Hence Cartesian equation of the plane is $$x+y+z=0$$

content: If the lines $$\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y+1}{3}=\frac{z-1}{4}$$ and $$\frac{x-3}{1}=\frac{y-k}{2}=\frac{z}{1}$$ intersect, then the values of $$k$$ is
explanation:
Let $$\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y+1}{3}=\frac{z-1}{4}=\lambda$$ and $$\frac{x-3}{1}=\frac{y-k}{2}=\frac{z}{1}=\mu$$

Since given lines intersect, we write

$$2 \lambda+1=\mu+3$$ ..... (1)

$$3 \lambda-1=2 \mu+\mathrm{k}$$ .... (2)

$$4 \lambda+1=\mu$$ ..... (3)

Substituting value of $$\mu$$ from (3) in (1), we get

$$\begin{aligned} & 2 \lambda+1=(4 \lambda+1)+3 \Rightarrow 2 \lambda=-3 \Rightarrow \lambda=\frac{-3}{2} \\ & \therefore \mu=4\left(\frac{-3}{2}\right)+1=-6+1=-5 \end{aligned}$$

Substituting values of $$\lambda$$ and $$\mu$$ in (2), we get

$$3\left(\frac{-3}{2}\right)-1=2(-5)+k \quad \Rightarrow k=\frac{9}{2}$$

content: The parametric equations of a line passing through the points $$\mathrm{A}(3,4,-7)$$ and $$\mathrm{B}(1,-1,6)$$ are
explanation:
The direction ratios of the line passing through the points A(3,4,-7) and B(1,-1,6) can be found by subtracting the coordinates of A from B :

$ \text{Direction ratio of x (l)} = 1 - 3 = -2 $

$ \text{Direction ratio of y (m)} = -1 - 4 = -5 $

$ \text{Direction ratio of z (n)} = 6 + 7 = 13 $

So, the direction ratios of the line are $(-2, -5, 13)$.

The parametric equation of a line passing through $ (x_1, y_1, z_1) $ and having direction ratios $ (l, m, n) $ is :

$ \frac{x - x_1}{l} = \frac{y - y_1}{m} = \frac{z - z_1}{n} = \lambda $

Where $ \lambda $ is a parameter.

Using the above formula and the coordinates of point A and the direction ratios :

$ \frac{x - 3}{-2} = \frac{y - 4}{-5} = \frac{z + 7}{13} = \lambda $

From this equation, the parametric equations are :

$ x = 3 - 2\lambda $

$ y = 4 - 5\lambda $

$ z = -7 + 13\lambda $

Thus, the correct option is :

Option D :

$ x=3-2 \lambda, y=4-5 \lambda, z=-7+13 \lambda $

content: The angle between a line with direction ratios 2, 2, 1 and a line joining (3, 1, 4) and (7, 2, 12) is
explanation:
Direction ratios of a line joining $$(3,1,4)$$ and $$(7,2,12)$$ are $$4,1,8$$

Let $$\left(a_1, b_1, c_1\right)=(2,2,1)$$ and $$\left(a_2, b_2, c_2\right)=(4,1,8)$$.

Hence angle $$\theta$$ between the lines is given by

$$\begin{aligned} \cos \theta & =\frac{a_1 a_2+b_1 b_2+c_1 c_2}{\sqrt{a_1^2+b_1^2+c_1} \sqrt{a_2^2+b_2^2+c_2^2}} \\ & =\frac{8+2+8}{\sqrt{4+4+1} \cdot \sqrt{16+1+64}}=\frac{18}{\sqrt{9} \cdot \sqrt{81}}=\frac{18}{(3)(9)}=\frac{2}{3} \\ \therefore \theta \quad & =\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{2}{3}\right)\end{aligned}$$

content: If the line $$\frac{x+1}{2}=\frac{y-m}{3}=\frac{z-4}{6}$$ lies in the plane $$3 x-14 y+6 z+49=0$$, then the value of $$m$$ is
explanation:
Line $$\frac{x+1}{2}=\frac{y-m}{3}=\frac{z-4}{6}$$ lies in plane $$3 x-14 y+6 z+49=0$$

$$\therefore$$ Point $$(-1, \mathrm{~m}, 4)$$ lies in the plane.

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore 3(-1)-14(\mathrm{~m})+6(4)+49=0 \Rightarrow-3-14 \mathrm{~m}+24+49=0 \\ & \therefore 14 \mathrm{~m}=70 \Rightarrow \mathrm{m}=5\end{aligned}$$

content: The point $P$ lies on the line $A, B$ where $A=(2,4,5)$ and $B \equiv(1,2,3)$. If $z$ co-ordinate of point $P$ is 3 , the its $y$ co-ordinate is
explanation:
We have,

$$A \equiv(2,4,5) \text { and } B \equiv(1,2,3)$$

Equation of $A B$ is,

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{x-2}{1-2}=\frac{y-4}{2-4}=\frac{z-5}{3-5}=\lambda \\ & \frac{x-2}{-1}=\frac{y-4}{-2}=\frac{z-5}{-2}=\lambda \end{aligned}$$

Putting $z=3$, we get

$$\begin{aligned} \qquad\lambda=\frac{3-5}{-2} & =1 \\ \therefore\quad\frac{y-4}{-2} & =1 \\ \Rightarrow\quad y-4 & =-2 \\ y & =2 \end{aligned}$$

content: A line makes angles $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ with the co-ordinate axes and $\alpha+\beta=90^{\circ}$, then $\gamma=$
explanation:
A line makes angles $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ with the coordinate axes and $\alpha+\beta=90^{\circ}$

$$\begin{array}{lr} \therefore & \cos ^2 \alpha+\cos ^2 \beta+\cos ^2 \gamma=1 \\ \Rightarrow & \cos ^2 \alpha+\cos ^2(90-\alpha)+\cos ^2 \gamma=1 \\ \Rightarrow & \cos ^2 \alpha+\sin ^2 \alpha+\cos ^2 \gamma=1 \\ \Rightarrow & 1+\cos ^2 \gamma=1 \\ \Rightarrow & \cos ^2 \gamma=0 \\ \Rightarrow & \gamma=90^{\circ} \end{array}$$

content: The equations of planes parallel to the plane $x+2 y+2 z+8=0$, which are at a distance of 2 units from the point $(1,1,2)$ are
explanation:
Equation of plane parallel to the plane

$$x+2 y+2 z+8=0 \text { is } x+2 y+2 z+\lambda=0$$

Given distance from the point $(1,1,2)$ to the plane.

$$\begin{array}{l} x+2 y+2 z+\lambda=0 \text { is } 2 \text { units } \\ \therefore \quad 2 =\left|\frac{1+2+4+\lambda}{\sqrt{1+4+4}}\right| \\ \Rightarrow \quad\pm 2 =\frac{\lambda+7}{3} \\ \Rightarrow \quad\lambda+7 = \pm 6 \\ \therefore \quad\lambda =-13,-1 \end{array}$$

Hence, equation of plane is

$$x+2 y+2 z-13=0 \text { or } x+2 y+2 z-1=0$$

content: The equation of a plane containing the point $(1,-1,2)$ and perpendicular to the planes $2 x+3 y-2 z=5$ and $x+2 y-3 z=8$ is
explanation:
Given plane,

$$2 x+3 y-2 z=5 \text { and } x+2 y-3 z=8$$

Normal of given plane are $2 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+3 \hat{\mathbf{j}}-2 \hat{\mathbf{k}}$ and $\hat{\mathbf{i}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{j}}-3 \hat{\mathbf{k}}$ respectively,

Normal of plane perpendicular to given plane is,

$$\begin{aligned} (2 \hat{\mathbf{i}} & +3 \hat{\mathbf{j}}-2 \hat{\mathbf{k}}) \times(\hat{\mathbf{i}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{j}}-3 \hat{\mathbf{k}}) \\ & =\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{\mathbf{i}} & \hat{\mathbf{j}} & \hat{\mathbf{k}} \\ 2 & 3 & -2 \\ 1 & 2 & -3 \end{array}\right|=-5 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+4 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+\hat{\mathbf{k}} \end{aligned}$$

Equation of plane passing through $(1,-1,2)$ and whose normal vector $-5 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+4 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+\hat{\mathbf{k}}$, is

$$\begin{aligned} & (\mathbf{r}-(\hat{\mathbf{i}}-\hat{\mathbf{j}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{k}}) \cdot(-5 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+4 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+\hat{\mathbf{k}})=0 \\ & \mathbf{r} \cdot(5 \hat{\mathbf{i}}-4 \hat{\mathbf{j}}-\hat{\mathbf{k}})=-(-5-4+2) \\ & \mathbf{r} \cdot(5 \hat{\mathbf{i}}-4 \hat{\mathbf{j}}-\hat{\mathbf{k}})=7 \end{aligned}$$

content: The equation of the line passing through $(1,2,3)$ and perpendicular to the lines $x-1=\frac{y+2}{2}=\frac{z+4}{4}$ and $\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y-2}{2}=z+3$ is
explanation:
Given line,

$$\frac{x-1}{1}=\frac{y+2}{2}=\frac{z+4}{4}$$

and $\quad \frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y-2}{2}=\frac{z+3}{1}$

The direction ratio of given line are $(\hat{\mathbf{i}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+4 \hat{\mathbf{k}})$ and $2 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+1 \hat{\mathbf{k}}$ respectively.

Direction ratio of line perpendicular to both the given line is $(\hat{\mathbf{i}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+4 \hat{\mathbf{k}}) \times(2 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+\hat{\mathbf{k}})$

$$=\left|\begin{array}{lll} \hat{\mathbf{i}} & \hat{\mathbf{j}} & \hat{\mathbf{k}} \\ 1 & 2 & 4 \\ 2 & 2 & 1 \end{array}\right|=-6 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+7 \hat{\mathbf{j}}-2 \hat{\mathbf{k}}$$

Equation of line passing through $(1,2,3)$ and whose parallel vector $-6 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+7 \hat{\mathbf{j}}-2 \hat{\mathbf{k}}$ ) is

or $\frac{x-1}{-6}=\frac{y-2}{7}=\frac{z-3}{-2}$

$$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{x-1}{6}=\frac{2-y}{7}=\frac{z-3}{2}$$

content: If the plane $$2 x+3 y+5 z=1$$ intersects the co-ordinate axes at the points $$A, B, C$$, then the centroid of $$\triangle A B C$$ is
explanation:
Given plane,

$$2 x+3 y+5 z=1$$ intersect the co-ordinate axes at $$A, B, C$$

$$\therefore$$ Equation of plane in intercept form is,

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{x}{1 / 2}+\frac{y}{1 / 3}+\frac{z}{1 / 5}=1 \\ & \therefore \quad A\left(\frac{1}{2}, 0,0\right), B\left(0, \frac{1}{3}, 0\right), C\left(0,0, \frac{1}{5}\right) \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore$$ Centroid of $$\triangle A B C$$ is $$\left(\frac{1}{6}, \frac{1}{9}, \frac{1}{15}\right)$$

content: The direction co-sines of the line which bisects the angle between positive direction of $$Y$$ and $$Z$$ axes are
explanation:
A point lie on the line which bisects the angle between positive direction of $$Y$$ and $$Z$$ axes on $$(0, a, a)$$

Direction ratio of line passing through origin and point $$(0, a, a)$$ is $$(0, a, a)$$

Direction cosine of line is

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{0}{\sqrt{0+a^2+a^2}}, \frac{a}{\sqrt{2 a^2}}, \frac{a}{\sqrt{2 a^2}} \\ &=0, \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \end{aligned}$$

content: The angle between the lines $$\frac{x-1}{4}=\frac{y-3}{1}=\frac{z}{8}$$ and $$\frac{x-2}{2}=\frac{y+1}{2}=\frac{z-4}{1}$$ is
explanation:
Given,

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{x-1}{4} & =\frac{y-3}{1}=\frac{z}{8} \\ \text { and } \quad \frac{x-2}{2} & =\frac{y+1}{2}=\frac{z-4}{1} \\ \text { Here, } \quad a_1 & =4, b_1=1, c_1=8 \\ a_2 & =2, b_2=2, c_2=1\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \cos \theta=\frac{a_1 a_2+b_1 b_2+c_1 c_2}{\left(\sqrt{a_1^2+b_1^2+c_1^2}\right)\left(\sqrt{a_2^2+b_2^2+c_2^2}\right)} \\ \Rightarrow & \cos \theta=\frac{8+2+8}{(\sqrt{16+1+64})(\sqrt{4+4+1})} \\ \Rightarrow & \cos \theta=\frac{18}{9 \times 3}=\frac{2}{3} \\ \Rightarrow & \theta=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{2}{3}\right) \end{array}$$

content: If the line $$r=(\hat{\mathbf{i}}-2 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+3 \hat{\mathbf{k}})+\lambda(2 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{j}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{k}})$$ is parallel to the plane $$r \cdot(3 \hat{i}-2 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+m \hat{\mathbf{k}})=10$$, then the value of $$m$$ is
explanation:
Given line,

$$\mathbf{r}=(\hat{\mathbf{i}}-2 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+3 \hat{\mathbf{k}})+\lambda(2 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{j}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{k}})$$

is parallel to plane $$\mathbf{r} \cdot(3 \hat{\mathbf{i}}-2 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+m \hat{\mathbf{k}})=0$$

We know that, if line $$\mathbf{r}=\mathbf{a}+\lambda \mathbf{b}$$ is parallel to plane $$\mathbf{r} \cdot \mathbf{n}=d$$

Then, $$\mathbf{b} \cdot \mathbf{n}=\mathbf{0}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad(2 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{j}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{k}}) \cdot(3 \hat{\mathbf{i}}-2 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+m \hat{\mathbf{k}}) & =0 \\ 6-2+2 m & =0 \\ m & =-2 \end{aligned}$$

content: The points $$A(-a,-b), B(0,0), C(a, b)$$ and $$D\left(a^2, a b\right)$$ are
explanation:
We have,

$$A(-a,-b), B(0,0), C(a, b)$$ and $$D\left(a^2, a b\right)$$

Slope of $$A B=\frac{b}{a}$$

Slope of $$B C=b / a$$

Slope of $$C D=b / a$$

$$\therefore$$ Slope of $$A B=$$

Slope of $$B C=$$ slope of $$C D$$

Hence, $$A, B, C, D$$ are collinears.

content: The cosine of the angle included between the lines $$\mathbf{r}=(2 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{j}}-2 \hat{\mathbf{k}})+\lambda(\hat{\mathbf{i}}-2 \hat{\mathbf{j}}-2 \hat{\mathbf{k}})$$ and $$\mathbf{r}=(\hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{j}}+3 \hat{\mathbf{k}})+\mu(3 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{j}}-6 \hat{\mathbf{k}})$$ where $$\lambda, \mu \in R$$ is.
explanation:
Given lines,

$$\begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & \mathbf{r}=(2 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{j}}-2 \hat{\mathbf{k}})+\lambda(\hat{\mathbf{i}}-2 \hat{\mathbf{j}}-2 \hat{\mathbf{k}}) \\ & \mathbf{r}=(\hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{j}}+3 \hat{\mathbf{k}})+\mu(3 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{j}}-6 \hat{\mathbf{k}}) \end{aligned}\\ &\text { Here, } \mathbf{b}_1=\hat{\mathbf{i}}-2 \hat{\mathbf{j}}-2 \hat{\mathbf{k}} \text { and } \mathbf{b}_2=3 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{j}}-6 \hat{\mathbf{k}} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \cos \theta & =\frac{\mathbf{b}_1 \cdot \mathbf{b}_2}{\left|\mathbf{b}_1\right|\left|\mathbf{b}_2\right|}=\frac{(\hat{\mathbf{l}}-2 \hat{\jmath}-2 \hat{\mathbf{k}}) \cdot(3 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{j}}-6 \hat{\mathbf{k}})}{\sqrt{1+4+4} \sqrt{9+4+36}} \\ \cos \theta & =\frac{3-4+12}{3 \times 7}=\frac{11}{21} \end{aligned}$$

content: If the foot of perpendicular drawn from the origin to the plane is $$(3,2,1)$$, then the equation of plane is
explanation:
Here, $$P(3,2,1)$$ is the foot of the perpendicular drawn from the origin to the plane

$$\therefore O P$$ is normal to the plane

Thus, direction ratio of the normal to the plane are $$(3,2,1)$$

Now, since the plane passes through $$(3,2,1)$$

$$\therefore$$ It equation is

$$\begin{array}{r} 3(x-3)+2(y-2)+1(z-1)=0 \\ 3 x-9+2 y-4+z-1=0 \\ 3 x+2 y+z=14 \end{array}$$

content: The angle between the line $$r =(\hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{j}}-\hat{\mathbf{k}})+\lambda(3 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{j}})$$ and the plane $$\mathbf{r} \cdot(\hat{\mathbf{i}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+3 \hat{\mathbf{k}})=8$$ is
explanation:
Given, line $$\mathbf{r}=(\hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{j}}-\hat{\mathbf{k}})+\lambda(3 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{j}})$$ and plane $$=\mathbf{r} \cdot(\hat{\mathbf{i}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+3 \hat{\mathbf{k}})=8$$

Here, $$\mathbf{b}=3 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{j}}$$ and $$\mathbf{n}=\hat{\mathbf{i}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+3 \hat{\mathbf{k}}$$

Angle between line and plane is

$$\begin{aligned} \sin \theta & =\frac{\mathbf{b} \cdot \mathbf{n}}{|\mathbf{b}||\mathbf{n}|} \\ \sin \theta & =\frac{(3 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{j}}) \cdot(\hat{\mathbf{i}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+3 \hat{\mathbf{k}})}{\sqrt{9+1} \sqrt{1+4+9}}=\frac{3+2}{\sqrt{10 \cdot \sqrt{14}}} \\ \sin \theta & =\frac{5}{2 \sqrt{5} \sqrt{7}}=\frac{\sqrt{5}}{2 \sqrt{7}} \\ \because \quad \theta & =\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{5}}{2 \sqrt{7}}\right) \end{aligned}$$

content: The direction cosines of a line which is perpendicular to lines whose direction ratios are $$3,-2,4$$ and $$1,3,-2$$ are
explanation:
Given direction ratio $$(3,-2,4)$$ and $$(1,3,-2)$$

Direction ratio of line which is perpendicular to both vector is

$$(3 \hat{\mathbf{i}}-2 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+4 \hat{\mathbf{k}}) \times(\hat{\mathbf{i}}+3 \hat{\mathbf{j}}-2 \hat{\mathbf{k}})$$

i.e., $$\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{\mathbf{i}} & \hat{\mathbf{j}} & \hat{\mathbf{k}} \\ 3 & -2 & 4 \\ 1 & 3 & -2\end{array}\right|=-8 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+10 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+11 \hat{\mathbf{k}}$$

i.e., $$(-8,10,11)$$

Direction cosine is

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{-8}{\sqrt{8^2+10^2+11^2}}, \frac{10}{\sqrt{8^2+10^2+11^2}} \\ & \frac{11}{\sqrt{8^2+10^2+11^2}},\left(\frac{-8}{\sqrt{285}}, \frac{10}{\sqrt{285}}, \frac{11}{\sqrt{285}}\right) \end{aligned}$$

content: If the lines given by $$\frac{x-1}{2 \lambda}=\frac{y-1}{-5}=\frac{z-1}{2}$$ and $$\frac{x+2}{\lambda}=\frac{y+3}{\lambda}=\frac{z+5}{1}$$ are parallel, then the value of $$\lambda$$ is
explanation:
Given, lines

$$\frac{x-1}{2 \lambda}=\frac{y-1}{-5}=\frac{z-1}{2}$$

and $$\frac{x+2}{\lambda}=\frac{y+3}{\lambda}=\frac{z+5}{1}$$

are parallel

$$\therefore \frac{2 \lambda}{\lambda}=\frac{-5}{\lambda}=\frac{2}{1} \Rightarrow 2=\frac{-5}{\lambda}=2=\lambda=-5 / 2$$

content: The vector equation of the plane $\mathbf{r}=(2 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{k}})+\lambda(\hat{\mathbf{i}})+\mu(\hat{\mathbf{i}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{j}}-3 \hat{\mathbf{k}})$ in scalar product form is $\mathbf{r} \cdot(3 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{k}})=\alpha$, then $\alpha=\ldots$
explanation:
Given, equation of plane

$$\mathbf{r}=(2 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{k}})+\lambda \hat{\mathbf{i}}+\mu(\hat{\mathbf{i}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{j}}-3 \hat{\mathbf{k}})\quad\text{.... (i)}$$

Here, plane (i) passing through a (let) $=2 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{k}}$

and parallel to vector $\mathbf{b}($ let $)=\hat{\mathbf{i}}$ and

$$\mathbf{c}=\hat{\mathbf{i}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{j}}-3 \hat{\mathbf{k}}$$

We know that equation of plane passing through a point a and parallel to non-parallel vectors band $\mathbf{c}$ is

$$r \cdot(b \times c)=a \cdot(b \times c)=[a b c]$$

Now,

$$\begin{aligned} & {\left[\begin{array}{lll} a & b & c \end{array}\right]=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 2 & 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 2 & -3 \end{array}\right|} \\ & \quad=2(0)-0+1(2-0)=2 \end{aligned}$$

and $\quad \mathbf{b} \times \mathbf{c}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{\mathbf{i}} & \hat{\mathbf{j}} & \hat{\mathbf{k}} \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 2 & -3\end{array}\right|=3 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{k}}$

$$\therefore \quad \mathbf{r} \cdot(3 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{k}})=2$$

Therefore, $\quad \alpha=2$

content: The direction ratios of the normal to the plane passing through origin and the line of intersection of the planes $x+2 y+3 z=4$ and $4 x+3 y+2 z=1$ are $\ldots \ldots$
explanation:
We have, line of intersection of the planes $x+2 y+3 z=4$ and $4 x+3 y+2 z=1$
